> Does the IS improve or diminish human welfare? *Example:* Warehouse robots in Amazon fulfilment centers increase productivity but contribute to repetitive-stress injuries and worker surveillance. The IS must be evaluated for its *net effect on dignity, employment, and health.*
>
> **Conclusion (½ mark):** These five dimensions are *non-exclusive*; a single IS issue often spans two or more. The most ethical IS design addresses *all five simultaneously*.

---

**Q.A. (b) Discuss the Fair Information Practices (FIP) framework. Explain any four core principles with examples. (7 marks)**

> **Model Answer (7 Marks):**
>
> **Introduction (1 mark):** The Fair Information Practices (FIP) framework originated with the U.S. Department of Health, Education and Welfare's *Heath Report (1973)* and was codified in the OECD Guidelines (1980) and later the EU Directive 95/46/EC, GDPR (2018), and India's DPDP Act (2023). FIP defines the *minimum ethical standards* for any IS that processes personal data.
>
> **Principle 1: Notice / Awareness (1.5 marks):**
> Data subjects must be informed *before* collection about what data is being collected, why, and how it will be used. *Example:* Every website now displays a cookie banner that lists the *categories* of cookies, the *purpose* (analytics, advertising), and the *retention period*. Without this notice, the collection is unlawful under GDPR Art. 12.
>
> **Principle 2: Consent / Choice (1.5 marks):**
> Beyond notice, the data subject must give *affirmative, freely-given, specific, and unambiguous* consent. Pre-ticked boxes are invalid. *Example:* A banking app must obtain a separate, explicit consent for sharing financial data with a credit-bureau partner, in addition to the general terms-of-service consent.
>
> **Principle 3: Access / Participation (1.5 marks):**
> The data subject has the right to (i) view their data, (ii) correct inaccurate data, and (iii) request deletion. *Example:* Google's *Takeout* service lets users download all their data; Meta's *Account Center* allows deletion of specific posts and ad-preferences.
>
> **Principle 4: Security / Integrity (1.5 marks):**
> The data controller must take *reasonable* technical and organizational measures to prevent unauthorized access, alteration, or destruction. *Example:* AES-256 encryption at rest, TLS 1.3 in transit, and quarterly third-party penetration tests are the *de facto* standard for healthcare IS handling PHI.
>
> **Conclusion (½ mark):** FIP is the *global lingua franca* of privacy law. Any IS that violates these principles exposes the firm to regulatory fines (up to **4% of global turnover** under GDPR) and reputational damage.

---

### ➤ CHOICE B — `[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Style]`

**Q.B. (a) Explain the major ethical issues related to Information Systems. Discuss any four with suitable examples. (7 marks)**

> **Model Answer (7 Marks):**
>
> **Introduction (½ mark):** Ethical issues in IS arise when the *use* of technology conflicts with the *values* of individuals, organizations, or society. They are categorized as *individual*, *organizational*, and *societal*.
>
> **Issue 1: Privacy Violation (1.5 marks):**
> IS enable mass surveillance, profiling, and re-identification. *Example:* A retail IS using loyalty-card data combined with CCTV footage to track an individual across stores and infer their religion or political affiliation — a clear privacy breach.
>
> **Issue 2: Intellectual Property Infringement (1.5 marks):**
> IS make copying and redistribution trivial. *Example:* Pirated copies of Tally, Adobe, and Microsoft Office are widely used in Indian small businesses, costing the software industry thousands of crores annually. The ethical (and legal) stance requires honouring licenses.
>
> **Issue 3: Digital Divide (1.5 marks):**
> Unequal access to IS deepens socio-economic inequality. *Example:* During COVID-19, online education excluded rural students without smartphones or 4G. The ethical duty of the state and edtech firms is to provide offline or low-bandwidth alternatives.
>
> **Issue 4: Algorithmic Bias (1.5 marks):**
> IS inherit and amplify biases in training data. *Example:* Amazon's recruiting AI (2018) down-ranked women's résumés because the training data was historically male-dominated. The firm scrapped the tool, but the *ethical* lesson is to audit training data for *representational fairness*.
>
> **Issue 5 (Bonus):** *Health and Safety (e.g., repetitive-stress injury, screen-time addiction), Employment (automation displacing workers), Environmental Impact (e-waste from data centres).*
>
> **Conclusion (½ mark):** Ethical IS design requires a *sociotechnical* lens — not just code quality, but the *human consequences* of every technical choice.

---

**Q.B. (b) Discuss the code of ethics proposed by AITP (Association of Information Technology Professionals). How does it help in resolving ethical dilemmas? (7 marks)**

> **Model Answer (7 Marks):**
>
> **Introduction (1 mark):** The AITP (formerly DPMA — Data Processing Management Association) issued its *Code of Ethics and Standards of Conduct* as a normative framework for IT professionals. It binds members to a set of *general obligations* and *specific conduct standards*.
>
> **Core Obligations (4 marks — 1 mark each):**
>
> 1. **Obligation to the Public:** Members shall *not knowingly* participate in or condone activities that harm the public, such as deploying surveillance systems that violate fundamental rights.
> 2. **Obligation to the Client/Employer:** Members shall perform work *with due diligence*, protect confidential information, and disclose conflicts of interest. *Example:* Refusing to silently insert a backdoor into a banking IS even if the employer pressures the engineer.
> 3. **Obligation to the Profession:** Members shall uphold the *integrity* and *reputation* of the profession, report unethical conduct of peers, and pursue continuous learning.
> 4. **Obligation to Colleagues and the Profession at large:** Members shall treat colleagues fairly, avoid discrimination, and credit others' contributions.
>
> **How it Resolves Dilemmas (2 marks):**
>
> - **Decision-Support Tool:** When an engineer faces a *gray area* (e.g., "should I deploy this facial-recognition feature?"), the AITP code provides a *checklist* — does it harm the public? Is it disclosed to the employer? Does it violate any law? Does it bring disrepute to the profession?
> - **Accountability Anchor:** The code is a *reference* in disciplinary proceedings; violation can lead to suspension or expulsion from AITP, which is a reputational signal.
> - **Industry-Wide Standardization:** The code aligns with ACM, BCS, and IEEE codes, enabling a *global normative baseline* for IT ethics.
>
> **Limitations (½ mark):** The AITP code is *non-binding* in a legal sense, lacks enforcement teeth, and may conflict with *local cultural norms* in multinational deployments.
>
> **Conclusion (½ mark):** AITP code is a *moral compass* — it does not replace law, but it elevates professional practice above mere compliance.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Mere listing without explanation** — Writing "GDPR has 7 principles" without explaining *any* of them scores 1 out of 7 marks. *Always explain 2–3 principles in depth.*
> 2. **No real-world example** — Abstract answers without an *Indian* or *global* case study (e.g., Aadhaar, WhatsApp, Cambridge Analytica) lose the *application* mark.
> 3. **Confusing Privacy with Security** — Privacy is *control by the user*; Security is *protection by the firm*. Examiners deduct ½ mark for conflating them.
> 4. **Skipping the ethical lens** — In a 14-mark answer, failing to mention *Kantian* or *Utilitarian* ethics loses 1 mark. Always include at least one ethical framework.
> 5. **Ignoring Indian laws** — KTU is an Indian university. Citing only GDPR without mentioning the *DPDP Act 2023* or the *IT Act 2000* weakens the *regional relevance* score.
> 6. **No diagram** — A 14-mark essay without *at least one* diagram or table loses 1–2 marks in the *presentation* category.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Ethics and Privacy (Module 1)**
>
> - ✅ **Information Ethics** = moral reasoning on information creation, processing, and use.
> - ✅ **Privacy** = data subject's *boundary control* over their PII.
> - ✅ **Five Moral Dimensions** = Information Rights, Property Rights, Accountability, System Quality, Quality of Life.
> - ✅ **FIP Core Principles** = Notice, Consent, Access, Security, Disclosure, Use Limitation, Accountability.
> - ✅ **GDPR 7 Principles** (Art. 5) = Lawfulness, Purpose Limitation, Data Minimization, Accuracy, Storage Limitation, Integrity & Confidentiality, Accountability.
> - ✅ **DPDP Act 2023 (India)** = *Digital Personal Data Protection Act* — India's flagship privacy law; introduces *Data Principal Rights* and *Data Fiduciary Obligations*.
> - ✅ **IT Act 2000 (India)** = Legal framework for *electronic records, digital signatures, cybercrime*. Amended in 2008 to add Section 66A (struck down) and 43A (data-protection compensation).
> - ✅ **Privacy Risk Index** = $PRI = (S \times E) / C_{\text{eff}}$ — used in DPIA prioritization.
> - ✅ **Privacy by Design (PbD)** = Embed privacy in the *architecture*, not as a retrofit.
> - ✅ **AITP / ACM / BCS codes** = Professional normative standards; non-binding but reputational.
> - ✅ **Kantian Ethics** = Universalizability test (do only what can be a universal maxim).
> - ✅ **Utilitarian Ethics** = Greatest-good-for-the-greatest-number cost-benefit reasoning.
> - ✅ **Stakeholders in IS Ethics** = Data Subject, Data Controller, Data Processor, Regulator, Society, Employees.
> - ✅ **Three-Pillar Model** = Ethics (morals) + Privacy (rights) + Security (controls) → Trust.
> - ✅ **Common KTU examples** = Aadhaar, WhatsApp privacy policy, Cambridge Analytica, Apple Card bias, Amazon recruiting AI, Pegasus spyware.
> - ✅ **Always answer with:** (1) Definition, (2) Framework, (3) Indian + Global example, (4) Ethical lens, (5) Conclusion + Remedy.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Information Security and Controls
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT523 - INFORMATION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Information Security and Controls

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Information Security (InfoSec)** is the disciplined practice of protecting information by mitigating risks. It involves preventing unauthorized access, use, disclosure, disruption, modification, or destruction of information to ensure **Confidentiality, Integrity, and Availability (CIA Triad)** — the three foundational pillars of information security as mandated by the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CIA Triad (The Core Mandate of InfoSec):**
> - **Confidentiality**: Ensures that information is accessible only to those authorized to view it.
> - **Integrity**: Ensures that information is accurate, consistent, and has not been tampered with.
> - **Availability**: Ensures that information and systems are accessible to authorized users when needed.

**Information Controls** are the administrative, physical, and technical safeguards implemented by an organization to protect the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of its information assets. They are the *mechanisms* through which Information Security objectives are operationalized.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Bank Vault Analogy**
> 
> Imagine an organization's information system as a **bank vault** holding precious diamonds (data):
> 
> 1. **Confidentiality** is like the **locked door with a guard** — only people with the right key (authorization) can enter and see the diamonds.
> 2. **Integrity** is like **CCTV cameras and seals on the vault boxes** — you can detect if anyone has touched, swapped, or stolen a diamond, ensuring the contents are exactly as the bank recorded.
> 3. **Availability** is like **a working elevator and electricity** — even the right person cannot access the vault if the building has no power. The vault must be ready when needed.
> 
> **Controls** are the combination locks, guards, alarms, and CCTV — the layered protections that together make the vault secure.

## 1.3 Why Information Security Matters in Modern Enterprises

Modern organizations depend on information systems for transactions, communication, supply chain coordination, and strategic decisions. A single security breach can result in:

- **Financial Loss**: Direct theft, regulatory fines, lawsuits.
- **Reputational Damage**: Loss of customer trust and brand equity.
- **Operational Disruption**: Downtime halting critical business processes.
- **Legal Liability**: Non-compliance with data protection laws (e.g., IT Act 2000, GDPR).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight**: Students must understand the *strategic* role of information security — it is not just an IT issue, but a **business continuity and governance** priority.

## 1.4 Components of an Information Security Framework

| Component | Definition | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **People** | Human users, employees, attackers | Employees trained in security awareness |
| **Process** | Policies, procedures, standards | Access control policy |
| **Technology** | Hardware/software tools | Firewalls, antivirus, encryption |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The CIA Triad — Expanded Theoretical Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Confidentiality
Confidentiality ensures that **sensitive information is not disclosed** to unauthorized individuals, entities, or processes.

**How it is achieved:**
- **Encryption**: Scrambling data so only authorized parties with a key can read it.
- **Access Controls**: Authentication (who are you?) and Authorization (what can you do?).
- **Data Classification**: Labeling data as Public, Internal, Confidential, Restricted.
- **Non-Disclosure Agreements (NDAs)**: Legal contracts binding employees/contractors.

### 2.1.2 Integrity
Integrity ensures that **information is accurate and trustworthy** throughout its lifecycle.

**How it is achieved:**
- **Hashing**: Producing a fixed-length digital fingerprint (e.g., SHA-256) to detect tampering.
- **Digital Signatures**: Cryptographic verification of sender identity and message integrity.
- **Audit Logs**: Records of who changed what and when.
- **Checksums**: Mathematical verification of data completeness.

### 2.1.3 Availability
Availability ensures that **systems and data are accessible when needed** by authorized users.

**How it is achieved:**
- **Redundancy**: Backup servers, redundant power supplies.
- **Disaster Recovery Plans (DRP)**: Procedures to restore operations after disruption.
- **Business Continuity Plans (BCP)**: Maintaining essential functions during a crisis.
- **DDoS Mitigation**: Defending against Distributed Denial-of-Service attacks.
- **Regular Maintenance & Patching**: Preventing system failures.

## 2.2 Types of Security Threats

> [!NOTE]
> **A Threat** is any potential danger that can exploit a vulnerability to cause harm. A **Vulnerability** is a weakness in a system. **Risk** is the likelihood and impact of a threat exploiting a vulnerability.

### 2.2.1 Common Threat Categories

1. **Unauthorized Access**: Gaining entry to systems without permission (hacking, password cracking).
2. **Malware**: Malicious software — viruses, worms, trojans, ransomware, spyware.
3. **Social Engineering**: Manipulating people into revealing confidential information (phishing, pretexting, baiting).
4. **Denial-of-Service (DoS/DDoS)**: Overwhelming systems to make them unavailable.
5. **Insider Threats**: Employees or contractors misusing legitimate access.
6. **Data Leakage**: Unintentional or malicious exposure of sensitive data.
7. **Man-in-the-Middle (MitM)**: Intercepting communications between two parties.
8. **SQL Injection / Zero-Day Exploits**: Targeting application or software vulnerabilities.

### 2.2.2 Sources of Threats

| Source | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Internal** | Employees, contractors, vendors | Disgruntled employee stealing data |
| **External** | Hackers, competitors, nation-states | Cybercriminal group launching ransomware |
| **Natural** | Earthquakes, floods, fires | Flood destroying a data center |
| **Technical** | System failures, software bugs | Server crash causing data loss |

## 2.3 The Three Categories of Security Controls

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip**: This is a **high-frequency topic**. You must be able to classify controls into Preventive, Detective, and Corrective — and give an example of each.

### 2.3.1 Preventive Controls
**Purpose**: Stop a security incident *before* it occurs.

| Control Type | Example |
|---|---|
| Administrative | Security policies, hiring practices, separation of duties |
| Physical | Fences, locks, biometric door access, security guards |
| Technical | Firewalls, encryption, antivirus software, multi-factor authentication |

### 2.3.2 Detective Controls
**Purpose**: Identify a security incident *while it is occurring* or after it has happened.

| Control Type | Example |
|---|---|
| Administrative | Audit reviews, security awareness training |
| Physical | CCTV cameras, motion detectors |
| Technical | Intrusion Detection Systems (IDS), log monitoring, SIEM tools |

### 2.3.3 Corrective Controls
**Purpose**: Restore systems and data *after* an incident.

| Control Type | Example |
|---|---|
| Administrative | Incident response procedures, disaster recovery plans |
| Physical | Backup power generators, fire suppression systems |
| Technical | Antivirus quarantine, patching vulnerabilities, system restoration from backups |

## 2.4 Risk Management Framework

> [!NOTE]
> **Risk** = the probability of a threat exploiting a vulnerability, multiplied by the resulting impact on the organization.

### 2.4.1 The Risk Management Process

1. **Asset Identification**: Catalog all information assets (hardware, software, data, people).
2. **Threat Identification**: Identify potential threats to each asset.
3. **Vulnerability Assessment**: Identify weaknesses that threats could exploit.
4. **Risk Analysis**: Calculate the likelihood and impact of each risk.
5. **Risk Treatment**: Accept, mitigate, transfer, or avoid the risk.
6. **Monitoring & Review**: Continuously assess the effectiveness of controls.

### 2.4.2 Risk Treatment Strategies

| Strategy | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Risk Acceptance** | Accept the risk because cost of mitigation exceeds impact | Minor software bug with low impact |
| **Risk Mitigation** | Implement controls to reduce risk | Installing a firewall |
| **Risk Transfer** | Shift risk to another party | Purchasing cyber insurance |
| **Risk Avoidance** | Eliminate the risk by removing the activity | Not storing credit card data |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **These quantitative formulas are essential for solving KTU Part B problems on Risk Analysis.**

| Formula | Description | Variables |
|---|---|---|
| **SLE = Asset Value (AV) × Exposure Factor (EF)** | Single Loss Expectancy — the expected loss from a single incident | AV in monetary terms, EF as a decimal (0 to 1) |
| **ALE = SLE × ARO** | Annual Loss Expectancy — expected annual loss | SLE in monetary terms, ARO = Annualized Rate of Occurrence |
| **ROSI = (ALE before − ALE after − ACS) / ACS** | Return on Security Investment — cost-benefit justification | ALE in monetary terms, ACS = Annual Cost of Control/Solution |
| **Risk = Threat × Vulnerability × Impact** | Qualitative risk calculation | Threat likelihood, Vulnerability severity, Business impact |
| **Total Risk = Threat × Vulnerability × Asset Value** | Risk prioritization formula | Quantified inputs |

**Key Constants / Standard Metrics:**

- **Confidentiality metric**: Bit strength of encryption keys (e.g., **128-bit, 256-bit AES**).
- **Integrity metric**: Hash output length in bits (e.g., **SHA-256 = 256 bits**).
- **Availability metric**: Uptime percentage — **99.9% (Three Nines)** ≈ 8.77 hours downtime/year; **99.999% (Five Nines)** ≈ 5.26 minutes downtime/year.
- **Recovery Time Objective (RTO)**: Maximum acceptable downtime.
- **Recovery Point Objective (RPO)**: Maximum acceptable data loss measured in time.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Information security is not abstract — it is operationalized across every engineering discipline:

- **IT / Software Engineering**: Secure coding, DevSecOps, penetration testing.
- **Electrical / Electronics**: Securing SCADA systems, IoT device hardening.
- **Mechanical / Industrial**: Protecting Industrial Control Systems (ICS) from cyber-physical attacks.
- **Civil Engineering**: Securing Building Management Systems (BMS) and smart infrastructure.
- **All disciplines**: Protecting intellectual property (research, designs, patents) from industrial espionage.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Calculating Single Loss Expectancy (SLE)

**Problem Statement:**
A small company has an online customer database valued at **₹5,00,000**. A successful SQL injection attack would expose **60%** of the records. Calculate the **Single Loss Expectancy (SLE)**.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1 — Identify the given values.**

$$\text{Asset Value (AV)} = ₹5{,}00{,}000$$

$$\text{Exposure Factor (EF)} = 60\% = 0.60$$

**Step 2 — State the formula.**

$$\text{SLE} = \text{AV} \times \text{EF}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the values into the formula.**

$$\text{SLE} = 5{,}00{,}000 \times 0.60$$

**Step 4 — Perform the multiplication.**

$$\text{SLE} = 3{,}00{,}000$$

**Step 5 — State the result with units.**

$$\boxed{\text{SLE} = ₹3{,}00{,}000}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation**: If a single SQL injection attack occurs and exposes 60% of the database, the company stands to lose **₹3,00,000** in that single incident.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Calculating Annual Loss Expectancy (ALE)

**Problem Statement:**
Using the SLE from Worked Example 1, and assuming such SQL injection attacks occur on average **3 times per year**, calculate the **Annual Loss Expectancy (ALE)**.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1 — Identify the given values.**

$$\text{SLE} = ₹3{,}00{,}000$$

$$\text{Annualized Rate of Occurrence (ARO)} = 3$$

**Step 2 — State the formula.**

$$\text{ALE} = \text{SLE} \times \text{ARO}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the values.**

$$\text{ALE} = 3{,}00{,}000 \times 3$$

**Step 4 — Perform the multiplication.**

$$\text{ALE} = 9{,}00{,}000$$

**Step 5 — State the result.**

$$\boxed{\text{ALE} = ₹9{,}00{,}000 \text{ per year}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight**: If a control costs **less than ₹9,00,000/year** and reduces this risk significantly, it is **financially justified**. This is the basis for the next formula.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: Return on Security Investment (ROSI)

**Problem Statement:**
The company is considering deploying a Web Application Firewall (WAF) that costs **₹4,00,000 per year**. The WAF is expected to **eliminate 80%** of SQL injection attacks (reducing ALE). Calculate the **ROSI**.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1 — Identify given values.**

$$\text{ALE before} = ₹9{,}00{,}000$$

$$\text{Risk Reduction} = 80\% = 0.80$$

$$\text{Annual Cost of Solution (ACS)} = ₹4{,}00{,}000$$

**Step 2 — Calculate ALE after implementing the control.**

$$\text{ALE after} = \text{ALE before} \times (1 - \text{Risk Reduction})$$

$$\text{ALE after} = 9{,}00{,}000 \times (1 - 0.80)$$

$$\text{ALE after} = 9{,}00{,}000 \times 0.20$$

$$\text{ALE after} = 1{,}80{,}000$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the Risk Reduction benefit.**

$$\text{Risk Reduction Benefit} = \text{ALE before} - \text{ALE after}$$

$$\text{Risk Reduction Benefit} = 9{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}80{,}000$$

$$\text{Risk Reduction Benefit} = 7{,}20{,}000$$

**Step 4 — State the ROSI formula.**

$$\text{ROSI} = \frac{\text{ALE before} - \text{ALE after} - \text{ACS}}{\text{ACS}}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute and simplify the numerator.**

$$\text{ROSI} = \frac{7{,}20{,}000 - 4{,}00{,}000}{4{,}00{,}000}$$

$$\text{ROSI} = \frac{3{,}20{,000}{4{,}00{,}000}$$

**Step 6 — Perform the division.**

$$\text{ROSI} = 0.80$$

**Step 7 — Convert to percentage.**

$$\boxed{\text{ROSI} = 80\%}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation**: For every **₹1 invested** in the WAF, the company saves **₹1.80** in expected losses. The investment is **highly justified**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Controls to the CIA Triad

| Control | CIA Pillar Protected | Type of Control | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Encryption** | Confidentiality | Preventive | AES-256 encrypting database |
| **Firewall** | Confidentiality, Integrity | Preventive | Blocking unauthorized network traffic |
| **Backup & Recovery** | Availability | Corrective | Restoring data after ransomware |
| **Intrusion Detection System (IDS)** | All three | Detective | Alerting on suspicious patterns |
| **Hash Function** | Integrity | Detective | SHA-256 verifying file integrity |
| **Multi-Factor Authentication** | Confidentiality | Preventive | OTP + password login |
| **Antivirus Software** | Integrity, Availability | Preventive/Detective | Quarantining malware |
| **Disaster Recovery Plan** | Availability | Corrective | Switching to backup data center |
| **Security Audit** | All three | Detective | Annual review of access logs |
| **Security Policy** | All three | Preventive | Password complexity rules |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Information Security Management Architecture

The following **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** illustrates how information security controls are organized in an enterprise. It maps the relationships between threats, vulnerabilities, the CIA triad, control types, and risk management.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[Organization Information Assets] --> B1[Identify Threats]
    B1 --> B2[Identify Vulnerabilities]
    B2 --> C1[Assess Risk]
    
    C1 --> D1{Risk Treatment}
    D1 --> D2[Accept Risk]
    D1 --> D3[Mitigate Risk]
    D1 --> D4[Transfer Risk]
    D1 --> D5[Avoid Risk]
    
    D3 --> E1[Apply Security Controls]
    
    E1 --> F1[Preventive Controls]
    E1 --> F2[Detective Controls]
    E1 --> F3[Corrective Controls]
    
    F1 --> G1[Protect CIA Triad]
    F2 --> G1
    F3 --> G1
    
    G1 --> H1[Confidentiality]
    G1 --> H2[Integrity]
    G1 --> H3[Availability]
    
    H1 --> I1[Secured Information System]
    H2 --> I1
    H3 --> I1
    
    I1 --> J1[Monitor and Review]
    J1 --> B1
```

**Diagram Interpretation:**
- **Assets** are the starting point — without knowing what to protect, controls cannot be designed.
- **Risk Treatment** branches into four strategies; in practice, organizations combine all four.
- The **CIA Triad** is the destination — every control exists to protect at least one pillar.
- The loop back from **Monitor and Review** to **Identify Threats** indicates that security is a **continuous process**, not a one-time project.

## 4.2 Defense-in-Depth Layered Security Model

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1[Layer 1: Policies and Procedures] --> L2[Layer 2: Physical Security]
    L2 --> L3[Layer 3: Perimeter Network Security]
    L3 --> L4[Layer 4: Internal Network Security]
    L4 --> L5[Layer 5: Host Security]
    L5 --> L6[Layer 6: Application Security]
    L6 --> L7[Layer 7: Data Security]
    L7 --> CORE[Protected Information Asset]
```

**Diagram Interpretation:**
This is the **Defense-in-Depth** model — multiple layers of security so that if one fails, others still protect the asset.

- **Layer 1 — Policies**: Governance foundation (acceptable use, BYOD, incident response).
- **Layer 2 — Physical**: Locks, guards, CCTV, biometrics.
- **Layer 3 — Perimeter**: Firewalls, DMZ, intrusion prevention.
- **Layer 4 — Internal Network**: VLANs, network segmentation, IDS.
- **Layer 5 — Host**: Antivirus, host-based firewalls, OS patching.
- **Layer 6 — Application**: Secure coding, input validation, WAF.
- **Layer 7 — Data**: Encryption at rest and in transit, DLP (Data Loss Prevention).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip**: When asked "Explain Defense-in-Depth," always mention that **no single control is sufficient** — security must be layered.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Explain the CIA Triad of information security with an example for each component.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):

The **CIA Triad** is the foundational model of information security, consisting of three pillars:

1. **Confidentiality (1 Mark)**: Ensures that information is accessible only to authorized users. *Example:* Encrypting patient medical records so only authorized doctors can view them.

2. **Integrity (1 Mark)**: Ensures that data is accurate and has not been altered without authorization. *Example:* Using a SHA-256 hash to verify that a software download has not been tampered with during transit.

3. **Availability (1 Mark)**: Ensures that systems and data are accessible when needed. *Example:* Using redundant servers and a Disaster Recovery Plan so that an e-commerce website remains operational during server failure.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Differentiate between Preventive, Detective, and Corrective controls. Give one example of each.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):

| Control Type | Purpose | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Preventive** (1 Mark) | Stops a security incident *before* it occurs | Firewall blocking unauthorized traffic |
| **Detective** (1 Mark) | Identifies a security incident *during* or *after* occurrence | Intrusion Detection System alerting on suspicious activity |
| **Corrective** (1 Mark) | Restores systems and data *after* an incident | Restoring a database from backup after a ransomware attack |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Following KTU ESE convention, **students answer ONE of TWO choices**. Both questions are provided below with full sub-parts and model solutions.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, aligned with Dec 2023 pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the four risk treatment strategies with one example each. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A manufacturing company's ERP system is valued at **₹20,00,000**. A ransomware attack would encrypt **70%** of the data. Such attacks occur on average **twice per year**. The company plans to deploy an Endpoint Detection and Response (EDR) solution costing **₹5,00,000 per year** that is expected to **eliminate 90%** of attacks. Calculate SLE, ALE before control, ALE after control, and ROSI. Comment on whether the investment is justified. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Four Risk Treatment Strategies (7 Marks):**

1. **Risk Acceptance (1.75 Marks)**: The organization acknowledges the risk and decides to accept it without action, typically because the cost of mitigation exceeds the impact. *Example:* Accepting the risk of minor email phishing attempts by providing user training rather than buying specialized anti-phishing software.

2. **Risk Mitigation (1.75 Marks)**: Implementing controls to reduce either the likelihood or impact of the risk. *Example:* Installing antivirus software and a firewall to reduce the risk of malware infection.

3. **Risk Transfer (1.75 Marks)**: Shifting the financial impact of risk to a third party. *Example:* Purchasing a cyber-insurance policy that covers losses from data breaches.

4. **Risk Avoidance (1.75 Marks)**: Eliminating the risk entirely by discontinuing the risky activity. *Example:* A company deciding not to store customer credit card data, thereby avoiding the risk of a payment data breach.

---

**(b) Quantitative Calculation (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Calculate SLE.**

$$\text{SLE} = \text{Asset Value} \times \text{Exposure Factor}$$

$$\text{SLE} = 20{,}00{,}000 \times 0.70 = 14{,}00{,}000$$

**Step 2 — Calculate ALE before control.**

$$\text{ALE before} = \text{SLE} \times \text{ARO} = 14{,}00{,}000 \times 2 = 28{,}00{,}000$$

**Step 3 — Calculate ALE after control.**

$$\text{ALE after} = \text{ALE before} \times (1 - 0.90) = 28{,}00{,}000 \times 0.10 = 2{,}80{,}000$$

**Step 4 — Calculate ROSI.**

$$\text{ROSI} = \frac{(28{,}00{,}000 - 2{,}80{,}000) - 5{,}00{,}000}{5{,}00{,}000}$$

$$\text{ROSI} = \frac{20{,}20{,}000}{5{,}00{,}000} = 4.04 = 404\%$$

**Conclusion (1 Mark):** Since ROSI = **404%**, every ₹1 spent on EDR saves ₹5.04 in expected losses. **The investment is highly justified.**

---

### Question B (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, aligned with June 2024 pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of **Defense-in-Depth** in information security. Describe any five layers with examples. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Distinguish between **Threat, Vulnerability, and Risk**. A financial services firm stores customer credit card data. Identify three potential threats, three corresponding vulnerabilities, and recommend one appropriate control for each vulnerability. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Defense-in-Depth (7 Marks):**

**Definition (2 Marks):** Defense-in-Depth is a security strategy that employs **multiple overlapping layers of controls** so that if one layer fails, subsequent layers continue to provide protection. It is based on the principle that no single control is sufficient.

**Five Layers (1 Mark per layer):**

1. **Policies and Procedures**: Governance foundation. *Example:* Information Security Policy, Acceptable Use Policy.

2. **Physical Security**: Protects physical access. *Example:* Biometric door access to the server room.

3. **Perimeter Security**: Protects the network boundary. *Example:* Firewall and DMZ.

4. **Host Security**: Protects individual devices. *Example:* Antivirus, host-based firewall, OS patching.

5. **Data Security**: Protects the data itself. *Example:* AES-256 encryption of stored credit card data.

---

**(b) Threat, Vulnerability, Risk Distinction + Analysis (7 Marks):**

**Distinction (3 Marks — 1 Mark each):**
- **Threat**: Any potential danger that could exploit a vulnerability to cause harm. *Example:* A hacker attempting to breach a system.
- **Vulnerability**: A weakness in a system that can be exploited. *Example:* An unpatched software bug.
- **Risk**: The probability and impact of a threat exploiting a vulnerability. *Example:* The likelihood that a hacker exploits the unpatched bug and steals 10,000 customer records.

**Analysis Table (4 Marks):**

| Threat | Vulnerability | Recommended Control |
|---|---|---|
| Hacker stealing card data | Unencrypted database | AES-256 encryption of data at rest |
| Phishing attack stealing admin credentials | No multi-factor authentication | Implement MFA for all admin accounts |
| Insider leaking data | Excessive user access privileges | Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) and least privilege principle |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 
> 1. **Do not confuse Threat with Vulnerability** — a *threat* is the agent; a *vulnerability* is the weakness. Many students lose 1–2 marks by interchanging them.
> 2. **Always show units (₹) and percentages** in ALE/ROSI calculations — losing the unit loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Defense-in-Depth requires MULTIPLE layers** — listing only firewalls and antivirus without policy/physical/data layers scores below average.
> 4. **Risk Treatment is NOT just "mitigation"** — examiners expect all four strategies (Accept, Mitigate, Transfer, Avoid).
> 5. **CIA Triad must be defined BEFORE examples** — examples alone without definitions are insufficient for full marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — High-Yield Points for KTU 2024 Exam**

- **CIA Triad** = Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability — the core of InfoSec.
- **Threat** = the danger; **Vulnerability** = the weakness; **Risk** = the probability × impact.
- **Three Control Categories** = Preventive (before), Detective (during/after), Corrective (restore).
- **Risk Treatment Strategies** = Accept, Mitigate, Transfer, Avoid.
- **SLE = AV × EF** | **ALE = SLE × ARO** | **ROSI = (ALE_before − ALE_after − ACS) / ACS**.
- **Defense-in-Depth** = layered security (Policy → Physical → Perimeter → Network → Host → Application → Data).
- **Common Threats** = Malware, Phishing, DoS/DDoS, Insider threats, SQL injection, MitM.
- **Common Controls** = Firewalls, Encryption, Antivirus, MFA, IDS/IPS, Backups, SIEM.
- **Availability Metric**: 99.999% uptime = ~5 minutes downtime/year (Five Nines).
- **Encryption protects Confidentiality**; **Hashing protects Integrity**; **Redundancy protects Availability**.
- **Information Security is a continuous process**, not a one-time project — always "Monitor and Review".
- **Business Continuity Plan (BCP)** maintains operations; **Disaster Recovery Plan (DRP)** restores systems after disaster.
- **Authentication** answers "Who are you?"; **Authorization** answers "What can you do?".
- **RTO** = max acceptable downtime; **RPO** = max acceptable data loss (in time).
- For KTU exam: **Always quantify risk using SLE/ALE/ROSI** — do not give only qualitative answers when numerical data is provided.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Information Security
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT523 - INFORMATION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Information Security

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition:**
> **Information Security (InfoSec)** is the practice of protecting information by mitigating risks. It involves preventing unauthorized access, use, disclosure, disruption, modification, inspection, recording, or destruction of data. It is a multidisciplinary field encompassing physical security, cybersecurity, and procedural controls, fundamentally anchored to the **CIA Triad** (Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability).

### 1.1 The CIA Triad — The Heart of Information Security

The **CIA Triad** is the foundational model upon which all information security policies, frameworks, and architectures are built. Every security control in existence can be traced back to protecting one or more of these three pillars.

| Pillar | Definition | Real-World Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Confidentiality** | Ensuring that information is accessible only to those authorized to view it. | A patient's medical records hidden from public view, accessible only to their doctor. |
| **Integrity** | Ensuring that information is accurate, consistent, and has not been tampered with. | A bank account balance that is not altered during a transaction. |
| **Availability** | Ensuring that information and systems are accessible to authorized users when needed. | A website that remains online 24/7 without downtime. |

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The High-Value Jewel Vault

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Diamond Vault of a Museum**
>
> Imagine a priceless diamond stored in a museum.
> - **Confidentiality** is the **laser-triggered alarm system and fingerprint locks** — only authorized curators can see and touch the diamond.
> - **Integrity** is the **sealed glass case with tamper-evident seals** — if a thief swaps the diamond with a fake, the system detects the change immediately.
> - **Availability** is the **backup power generators and redundant display units** — even during a power outage or fire, the museum guarantees the diamond can be retrieved for insurance and verification.
>
> In this analogy, the diamond is your **information**, the museum is your **system/network**, and the security systems are the **controls**. Lose any one, and the asset is compromised.

### 1.3 Why Information Security Matters in KTU Context

In the modern enterprise landscape, information is the most valuable asset — often more valuable than physical infrastructure. The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes that information security is not a purely technical problem; it is a **business, legal, and ethical imperative**. Organizations face:

- **Financial Loss** from data breaches (average cost measured in millions).
- **Reputational Damage** that destroys customer trust.
- **Legal Liability** under regulations like the IT Act, 2000 (India), GDPR (EU), and HIPAA.
- **Operational Disruption** from ransomware or DDoS attacks.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Board Tip:** Examiners frequently frame questions around *"Why is InfoSec not just an IT issue?"* — always tie your answer back to **business continuity, governance, and regulatory compliance**, not just firewalls and antivirus.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Key Terminology — The Building Blocks

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Master these definitions — they appear verbatim in KTU Part A (3-mark) questions.

- **Asset:** Anything of value to the organization (data, hardware, software, personnel, reputation).
- **Threat:** Any potential event that could cause harm to an information system.
- **Vulnerability:** A weakness in a system that can be exploited by a threat.
- **Risk:** The probability that a threat will exploit a vulnerability, multiplied by the impact of that exploitation.
- **Control (Countermeasure/Safeguard):** A safeguard put in place to mitigate risk.
- **Attack:** An intentional attempt to bypass security controls and compromise a system.

### 2.2 The Threat–Vulnerability–Risk Relationship

The operational logic of information security can be broken down into a clear causal chain:

1. **Identify Assets** → Catalog what needs protection (databases, servers, intellectual property).
2. **Identify Threats** → Determine what could harm the assets (hackers, malware, insiders, natural disasters).
3. **Identify Vulnerabilities** → Find weaknesses that threats could exploit (unpatched software, weak passwords, open ports).
4. **Assess Risk** → Calculate the likelihood and impact of each threat–vulnerability pair being realized.
5. **Implement Controls** → Deploy safeguards to reduce risk to an acceptable level.
6. **Monitor & Review** → Continuously evaluate the effectiveness of controls.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / High-Yield Metrics Table

> [!NOTE]
> While Information Security is largely qualitative, **quantitative risk analysis** is a high-yield area in KTU numerical problems. Memorize these formulas.

| Formula / Metric | Expression | Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Risk ($R$)** | $R = T \times V \times I$ | Risk = Threat likelihood $\times$ Vulnerability $\times$ Impact | Dimensionless (0–1 scale) |
| **Annualized Loss Expectancy (ALE)** | $\text{ALE} = \text{SLE} \times \text{ARO}$ | Expected annual financial loss from a risk | Currency (₹, \$) |
| **Single Loss Expectancy (SLE)** | $\text{SLE} = \text{Asset Value} \times \text{Exposure Factor}$ | Loss from a single occurrence of a threat | Currency (₹, \$) |
| **Annualized Rate of Occurrence (ARO)** | Empirical / historical input | Estimated frequency of threat occurrence per year | Events/year |
| **Cost–Benefit of Control** | $\text{Net Benefit} = \text{ALE}_{\text{before}} - \text{ALE}_{\text{after}} - \text{Annual Cost of Control}$ | Justifies whether a security investment is worthwhile | Currency (₹, \$) |
| **Return on Security Investment (ROSI)** | $\text{ROSI} = \dfrac{\text{ALE}_{\text{before}} - \text{ALE}_{\text{after}} - \text{Cost of Control}}{\text{Cost of Control}} \times 100\%$ | Percentage return on security spending | Percentage (\%) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse **SLE** with **ALE**. SLE is for *one* incident; ALE is *per year*. If a flood damages a ₹10,00,000 server with a 30% Exposure Factor and is expected to occur once every 4 years:
> - $\text{SLE} = 10,00,000 \times 0.30 = 3,00,000$
> - $\text{ARO} = 1/4 = 0.25$
> - $\text{ALE} = 3,00,000 \times 0.25 = 75,000$ per year.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In engineering fields, information security underpins:
- **Industrial Control Systems (ICS)** and SCADA security in power plants and manufacturing.
- **IoT device security** in smart grids and autonomous vehicles.
- **Medical device security** (pacemakers, infusion pumps) where breach = life loss.
- **Financial transaction integrity** in banking and e-commerce platforms.

### 2.5 Types of Security Controls

Security controls are categorized along three dimensions that KTU loves to test:

1. **By Function:**
   - *Preventive* — Stop an attack before it happens (firewalls, encryption, training).
   - *Detective* — Identify an attack in progress (IDS, logs, audits).
   - *Corrective* — Restore systems after an attack (backups, incident response).
   - *Deterrent* — Discourage attackers (warning banners, legal notices).
   - *Compensating* — Alternative controls when primary control is not feasible.

2. **By Form:**
   - *Physical* — Locks, fences, CCTV, biometric access.
   - *Technical (Logical)* — Firewalls, encryption, access control lists.
   - *Administrative* — Policies, procedures, security awareness training.

3. **By Strategy:**
   - *Defensive* — Protect from attack.
   - *Offensive* — Proactively hunt threats (red teaming, penetration testing).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example: Quantitative Risk Analysis (KTU Board Pattern)

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **canonical KTU Part B 14-mark question** on information security. Practice the exact format below.

**Problem Statement:**
A company has a customer database valued at ₹20,00,000. A ransomware attack would expose 40% of the database's value (Exposure Factor = 0.40). Based on industry data, such an attack is expected to occur once every 2 years. The company is considering purchasing an advanced endpoint detection system for ₹1,50,000 per year. After deployment, the attack frequency is expected to drop to once every 5 years, and the Exposure Factor is expected to reduce to 0.10. Calculate:
1. The **SLE** before controls.
2. The **ALE** before controls.
3. The **ALE** after controls.
4. The **Net Benefit** of the proposed control.
5. The **ROSI** of the proposed control.
6. Should the company purchase the control? Justify.

---

**Step 1: Single Loss Expectancy (SLE) Before Controls**

The Single Loss Expectancy is the monetary loss expected from a single occurrence of the threat.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SLE}_{\text{before}} &= \text{Asset Value} \times \text{Exposure Factor} \\
&= 20,00,000 \times 0.40 \\
&= 8,00,000 \text{ INR}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** If the database is worth ₹20 lakh and 40% of it is compromised per attack, then a single attack costs ₹8 lakh.

---

**Step 2: Annualized Loss Expectancy (ALE) Before Controls**

The ALE converts the per-incident loss into an annual expected loss.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ALE}_{\text{before}} &= \text{SLE}_{\text{before}} \times \text{ARO}_{\text{before}} \\
&= 8,00,000 \times \frac{1}{2} \\
&= 4,00,000 \text{ INR per year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** Since the attack is expected once every 2 years, ARO = 1/2 = 0.5.

---

**Step 3: SLE and ALE After Controls**

After installing the EDR system, the exposure factor drops to 0.10 and the attack frequency drops to once every 5 years.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SLE}_{\text{after}} &= 20,00,000 \times 0.10 = 2,00,000 \text{ INR} \\
\text{ARO}_{\text{after}} &= \frac{1}{5} = 0.2 \\
\text{ALE}_{\text{after}} &= 2,00,000 \times 0.2 = 40,000 \text{ INR per year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** Stronger controls reduce both the severity and frequency of incidents.

---

**Step 4: Net Benefit Calculation**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Net Benefit} &= \text{ALE}_{\text{before}} - \text{ALE}_{\text{after}} - \text{Annual Cost of Control} \\
&= 4,00,000 - 40,000 - 1,50,000 \\
&= 2,10,000 \text{ INR per year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** The company saves ₹2,10,000 per year net after paying for the control.

---

**Step 5: Return on Security Investment (ROSI)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ROSI} &= \frac{\text{ALE}_{\text{before}} - \text{ALE}_{\text{after}} - \text{Cost of Control}}{\text{Cost of Control}} \times 100\% \\
&= \frac{2,10,000}{1,50,000} \times 100\% \\
&= 140\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** For every ₹1 spent on the control, the company saves ₹1.40 in expected losses.

---

**Step 6: Decision & Justification**

Since the **Net Benefit is positive (₹2,10,000)** and **ROSI = 140%**, the company **should purchase the advanced endpoint detection system**. The investment yields a return 1.4× the cost, making it a financially sound security decision.

### 3.2 Analytical Threat–Control Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This matrix is a **high-scoring visual aid** for KTU Part B answers. It demonstrates comprehensive understanding.

| Threat Category | Specific Threat | Vulnerability Exploited | Primary CIA Pillar Affected | Recommended Controls |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Human** | Social engineering, phishing | Employee unawareness | Confidentiality | Security awareness training, MFA |
| **Software** | Buffer overflow, SQL injection | Unpatched applications | Integrity, Availability | Input validation, WAF, patch management |
| **Malware** | Ransomware, Trojans, spyware | Unpatched OS, no EDR | All three | Antivirus/EDR, least privilege, backups |
| **Network** | MITM, DoS/DDoS | Open ports, weak encryption | Confidentiality, Availability | Firewalls, IDS/IPS, TLS, rate limiting |
| **Physical** | Theft, hardware tampering | Unlocked server rooms | Confidentiality, Availability | Biometric access, CCTV, locked cabinets |
| **Environmental** | Fire, flood, power loss | Poor data center design | Availability | UPS, fire suppression, off-site backups |
| **Insider** | Data exfiltration, sabotage | Excessive privileges | Confidentiality, Integrity | RBAC, audit logging, separation of duties |

### 3.3 The Information Security Lifecycle (NIST-Aligned)

The KTU 2024 syllabus references the **NIST Cybersecurity Framework** core functions. Memorize this sequence as a 7-mark answer:

1. **Identify** — Asset inventory, risk assessment, governance.
2. **Protect** — Access control, encryption, training, firewalls.
3. **Detect** — IDS, SIEM, anomaly detection, log monitoring.
4. **Respond** — Incident response plan, forensics, communication.
5. **Recover** — Disaster recovery, business continuity, lessons learned.
6. *(Plus two cross-cutting functions: **Govern** and **Risk Management**.)*

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The CIA Triad — Foundational Security Model

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA["INFORMATION SECURITY"]:::core
    nodeB["CONFIDENTIALITY"]:::pillar1
    nodeC["INTEGRITY"]:::pillar2
    nodeD["AVAILABILITY"]:::pillar3
    nodeE["Encryption and Access Control"]:::ctrl1
    nodeF["Hashing and Digital Signatures"]:::ctrl2
    nodeG["Redundancy and Backups"]:::ctrl3
    nodeH["Privacy Laws and NDAs"]:::goal1
    nodeI["Data Accuracy and Audit Trails"]:::goal2
    nodeJ["Uptime SLAs and DR Plans"]:::goal3

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeA --> nodeC
    nodeA --> nodeD
    nodeB --> nodeE
    nodeB --> nodeH
    nodeC --> nodeF
    nodeC --> nodeI
    nodeD --> nodeG
    nodeD --> nodeJ

    classDef core fill:#1f2937,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px,color:#fff
    classDef pillar1 fill:#dc2626,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef pillar2 fill:#16a34a,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef pillar3 fill:#2563eb,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef ctrl1 fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#000
    classDef ctrl2 fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#000
    classDef ctrl3 fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#000
    classDef goal1 fill:#e0e7ff,stroke:#000
    classDef goal2 fill:#e0e7ff,stroke:#000
    classDef goal3 fill:#e0e7ff,stroke:#000
```

### 4.2 Risk Management Lifecycle — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1["STEP 1: ASSET IDENTIFICATION"]:::stage1
    node2["STEP 2: THREAT IDENTIFICATION"]:::stage2
    node3["STEP 3: VULNERABILITY ASSESSMENT"]:::stage3
    node4["STEP 4: RISK ANALYSIS"]:::stage4
    node5["STEP 5: CONTROL SELECTION"]:::stage5
    node6["STEP 6: IMPLEMENTATION"]:::stage6
    node7["STEP 7: MONITORING AND REVIEW"]:::stage7
    node1 --> node2 --> node3 --> node4 --> node5 --> node6 --> node7
    node7 -.feedback loop.-> node1

    classDef stage1 fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#000
    classDef stage2 fill:#fb923c,stroke:#000
    classDef stage3 fill:#f87171,stroke:#000
    classDef stage4 fill:#dc2626,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef stage5 fill:#a855f7,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef stage6 fill:#3b82f6,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef stage7 fill:#10b981,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

### 4.3 Layered Security Model (Defense in Depth)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeL1["LAYER 1: POLICIES AND GOVERNANCE"]:::layer1
    nodeL2["LAYER 2: PHYSICAL SECURITY"]:::layer2
    nodeL3["LAYER 3: PERIMETER NETWORK SECURITY"]:::layer3
    nodeL4["LAYER 4: INTERNAL NETWORK SECURITY"]:::layer4
    nodeL5["LAYER 5: HOST SECURITY"]:::layer5
    nodeL6["LAYER 6: APPLICATION SECURITY"]:::layer6
    nodeL7["LAYER 7: DATA SECURITY"]:::layer7
    nodeA["ASSET"]:::asset

    nodeA --> nodeL7 --> nodeL6 --> nodeL5 --> nodeL4 --> nodeL3 --> nodeL2 --> nodeL1

    classDef layer1 fill:#7c2d12,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef layer2 fill:#991b1b,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef layer3 fill:#b91c1c,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef layer4 fill:#dc2626,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef layer5 fill:#ef4444,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef layer6 fill:#f87171,stroke:#000
    classDef layer7 fill:#fca5a5,stroke:#000
    classDef asset fill:#fde68a,stroke:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Defense in Depth:** No single control is sufficient. Each layer acts as a barrier — if one fails, the next layer still protects the asset. This is a **favourite 7-mark KTU question**: *"Explain defense in depth with a diagram."*

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question]**

**Q1. Define the CIA Triad. Why is it considered the cornerstone of Information Security?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **CIA Triad** consists of three core principles:
1. **Confidentiality** — Ensuring information is accessible only to authorized users (achieved via encryption, access control).
2. **Integrity** — Ensuring information is accurate and unaltered (achieved via hashing, digital signatures).
3. **Availability** — Ensuring systems and data are accessible when needed (achieved via redundancy, backups).

It is the cornerstone because **every security control, policy, and framework maps back to protecting one or more of these three properties**. Without CIA, there is no foundation for measuring information security effectiveness. **(3 marks)**

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question]**

**Q2. Differentiate between a Threat, a Vulnerability, and a Risk. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Term | Definition | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Threat** | Any potential event that can cause harm | A hacker attempting SQL injection |
| **Vulnerability** | A weakness that can be exploited | An unpatched web application |
| **Risk** | The probability and impact of a threat exploiting a vulnerability | 30% chance of data breach costing ₹5 lakh per year |

The distinction is critical: a vulnerability *exists* independently, a threat *may or may not* act, and risk is the *quantified exposure* when both align. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, 14 Marks]**

#### **Question A (Choice 1)**

**(a)** Define Information Security. Explain the **CIA Triad** in detail with suitable examples for each pillar. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the concept of **Quantitative Risk Analysis**. A hospital's patient records system is valued at ₹50,00,000. A malware attack would compromise 25% of the data (Exposure Factor = 0.25). Such attacks are estimated to occur **twice a year**. Calculate the **SLE** and **ALE**. If a security control costing ₹2,00,000/year reduces the ARO to 0.5 and the Exposure Factor to 0.05, calculate the **new SLE, new ALE, Net Benefit, and ROSI**. Recommend whether the control should be implemented. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) Part (a) Solution — 7 marks:**

Information Security is the discipline of protecting information assets from unauthorized access, modification, disruption, or destruction. It encompasses people, processes, and technology.

**CIA Triad Detailed:**

- **Confidentiality (2 marks):** The property that information is not disclosed to unauthorized entities. Example: Aadhaar card data encrypted at rest and transmitted over TLS, accessible only via OTP-authenticated portals. Breaches include the 2018 Aadhaar data leak incident.

- **Integrity (2 marks):** The property that data remains accurate and unaltered. Example: A bank transfer of ₹5,000 — the receiving account must show exactly ₹5,000 credited, not ₹500 or ₹50,000. Achieved via SHA-256 hashing and digital signatures.

- **Availability (2 marks):** The property that systems are accessible when needed. Example: An e-commerce website during a festive sale handling 10 lakh concurrent users without crashing. Achieved via load balancers, CDNs, and disaster recovery sites.

- **Conclusion (1 mark):** The CIA Triad forms the universal framework; all security policies, whether ISO 27001 or NIST, derive from it.

**(b) Part (b) Solution — 7 marks:**

**Given Data:**
- Asset Value = ₹50,00,000
- $\text{EF}_{\text{before}} = 0.25$
- $\text{ARO}_{\text{before}} = 2$ per year
- $\text{EF}_{\text{after}} = 0.05$
- $\text{ARO}_{\text{after}} = 0.5$
- Annual Control Cost = ₹2,00,000

**Step 1: SLE Before**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SLE}_{\text{before}} &= 50,00,000 \times 0.25 \\
&= 12,50,000 \text{ INR}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: ALE Before**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ALE}_{\text{before}} &= 12,50,000 \times 2 \\
&= 25,00,000 \text{ INR per year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: SLE After and ALE After**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SLE}_{\text{after}} &= 50,00,000 \times 0.05 = 2,50,000 \text{ INR} \\
\text{ALE}_{\text{after}} &= 2,50,000 \times 0.5 = 1,25,000 \text{ INR per year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Net Benefit**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Net Benefit} &= 25,00,000 - 1,25,000 - 2,00,000 \\
&= 21,75,000 \text{ INR per year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: ROSI**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ROSI} &= \frac{21,75,000}{2,00,000} \times 100\% \\
&= 1087.5\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Recommendation:** The control **must be implemented**. With a Net Benefit of ₹21,75,000/year and a ROSI of 1087.5%, the investment is overwhelmingly justified. For every ₹1 spent, the hospital saves ₹10.87 in expected losses.

---

#### **Question B (Choice 2 — Internal Choice Alternative)**

**(a)** What is a **Vulnerability**? List and briefly explain any **four categories of security threats** with examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Defense in Depth** strategy with a layered architecture diagram. Why is it said that *"security is only as strong as the weakest link"*? **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) Part (a) Solution — 7 marks:**

A **Vulnerability (1 mark)** is a weakness or flaw in a system that can be exploited to compromise security.

**Four Threat Categories (1.5 marks each):**

1. **Malware Threats:** Malicious software like viruses, worms, Trojans, ransomware, and spyware. Example: The WannaCry ransomware (2017) exploited unpatched Windows systems, affecting 200,000+ computers across 150 countries.

2. **Network Threats:** Attacks targeting network infrastructure — DoS/DDoS, Man-in-the-Middle, packet sniffing, DNS spoofing. Example: A DDoS attack on a bank's website rendering it unavailable to customers.

3. **Human/Social Engineering Threats:** Manipulating people to bypass security — phishing, pretexting, baiting, tailgating. Example: A fake email impersonating the CEO asking finance to transfer funds (Business Email Compromise).

4. **Physical Threats:** Theft, vandalism, hardware tampering, natural disasters. Example: An unattended server room allowing an insider to plug in a USB keylogger.

**(b) Part (b) Solution — 7 marks:**

**Defense in Depth (4 marks):** A layered security strategy where multiple independent controls protect an asset. If one layer fails, subsequent layers continue to provide protection. Layers (from outer to inner) include: Policies, Physical Security, Perimeter (firewall), Network (IDS/IPS), Host (antivirus), Application (input validation), and Data (encryption).

**"Weakest Link" Argument (3 marks):** A chain breaks at its weakest link. In security, an organization may have excellent firewalls but a single careless employee clicking a phishing link can compromise the entire network. Therefore, security must be **holistic** — every layer, every person, every process must be secured. A single unpatched server, a single weak password, or a single untrained employee can undermine millions of rupees of security investment.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing SLE and ALE:** SLE = single incident; ALE = per year. Examiners specifically deduct marks for this.
> 2. **Forgetting units:** Always write "INR per year" or "\$" in ALE answers.
> 3. **Diagram is mandatory:** In 7-mark CIA Triad questions, a diagram adds 1–2 marks. Always include the Mermaid or hand-drawn triangle.
> 4. **One-sided answers:** KTU awards marks for **examples**. A CIA Triad answer without examples is incomplete.
> 5. **Calculation errors in ARO:** ARO = frequency per year. "Once every 4 years" means ARO = 0.25, not 4.
> 6. **Skipping the justification:** Always end risk analysis answers with a clear **YES/NO recommendation** backed by the numbers.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap — High-Density Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Bullet Summary for Last-Minute KTU Prep**

- **Information Security =** Protecting data's CIA (Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability).
- **CIA Triad** is the foundational model — memorize all three pillars with examples.
- **Threat** = potential harm; **Vulnerability** = weakness; **Risk** = probability × impact.
- **Key formulas** to memorize verbatim:
  - $\text{SLE} = \text{Asset Value} \times \text{Exposure Factor}$
  - $\text{ALE} = \text{SLE} \times \text{ARO}$
  - $\text{ROSI} = \dfrac{\text{ALE}_{\text{before}} - \text{ALE}_{\text{after}} - \text{Cost}}{\text{Cost}} \times 100\%$
- **Control types:** Preventive, Detective, Corrective, Deterrent, Compensating.
- **Control forms:** Physical, Technical (Logical), Administrative.
- **Threat categories:** Malware, Network, Human (social engineering), Physical, Insider.
- **NIST Functions:** Identify → Protect → Detect → Respond → Recover (plus Govern).
- **Defense in Depth** = layered security; no single control is sufficient.
- **Quantitative Risk Analysis** uses SLE, ALE, ARO, ROSI; **Qualitative** uses scales like Low/Medium/High.
- **Always justify recommendations** with calculated Net Benefit and ROSI.
- **Legal context:** IT Act 2000 (India), GDPR, HIPAA, PCI-DSS — mention these for full marks.
- **Diagram questions** appear in nearly every KTU board paper on this topic — practice CIA Triad + Defense in Depth diagrams.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Unintentional Threats to Information Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT523 - INFORMATION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Unintentional Threats to Information Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme** syllabus for Information Systems, an **Unintentional Threat** (also termed a *non-malicious threat*, *accidental threat*, or *inadvertent threat*) is defined as any potential danger to an information system's **Confidentiality, Integrity, and Availability (CIA Triad)** that arises **without deliberate malicious intent** from any human actor, natural process, or environmental condition.

According to the **NIST SP 800-30 Rev. 1** risk management framework, unintentional threats originate from:
- Inadvertent human actions (errors, omissions, mistakes)
- Natural and environmental causes (floods, earthquakes, lightning)
- Technical failures originating from system design flaws or component fatigue

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** Unintentional threats are distinguished from *intentional threats* (malware, hacking, insider attacks) solely by the **absence of malicious intent**. The damage, however, can be **identically catastrophic** in magnitude.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you own a beautiful aquarium in your home. The fish inside (your *data*) depend entirely on the tank's environment.

- **Intentional threat** = A person deliberately pouring poison into the tank.
- **Unintentional threat** = You accidentally leaving the room light on for 48 hours (causing the water temperature to rise and kill the fish), OR a sudden power cut stopping the oxygen pump, OR a small earthquake cracking the glass.

The tragedy is **real**, the **damage is permanent**, yet **nobody meant to harm the fish**. Unintentional threats to information systems operate on the exact same principle — they disrupt the delicate operational ecosystem of data, hardware, software, and networks without any human wanting to cause harm.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU Metric:** The industry-accepted benchmark statistic is that approximately **52% to 60% of all security incidents** in any given year are caused by unintentional human errors, making this the **single largest category** of security breaches globally.

### 1.3 The CIA Triad — The Damage Framework

Every unintentional threat targets one or more pillars of the **CIA Triad**:

| CIA Pillar | Definition | Unintentional Threat Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Confidentiality** | Data is accessible only to authorized entities | An employee mistakenly emailing salary data to the entire company |
| **Integrity** | Data is accurate and unaltered | A junior accountant typing `0` instead of `0.0` in a financial system |
| **Availability** | Systems are accessible when needed | A transformer explosion shutting down the data center |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Unintentional Threats

The KTU 2024 syllabus (Module 2) categorizes unintentional threats into **three primary classes**. Understanding this taxonomy is critical for both theory exams and the lab-based viva.

#### A. Human Errors (The Largest Sub-Class)
These are mistakes made by legitimate users, administrators, or developers acting in good faith.

**1. Operator Errors**
- Misconfiguration of firewalls, routers, or access control lists
- Entering wrong commands in production databases
- Forgetting to enable encryption on a new server

**2. Developer / Programmer Errors**
- Software bugs and unhandled exceptions
- Buffer overflows caused by poor input validation
- Hard-coded credentials in source code (a top cause of cloud breaches)

**3. End-User Errors**
- Accidental deletion of files or database records
- Sending sensitive data to the wrong recipient (misdirected email)
- Failing to apply critical security patches

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for KTU Exam:** The 2017 *Verizon Data Breach Investigations Report (DBIR)* revealed that **misdelivery** (sending information to the wrong person) was the **#1 cause of accidental data loss** in enterprises.

#### B. Environmental & Natural Threats
Forces of nature that physically destroy or disrupt information systems.

- **Natural Disasters:** Floods, earthquakes, hurricanes, tsunamis, tornadoes
- **Climatic Threats:** Extreme heat, excessive humidity, static electricity buildup
- **Biological Threats:** Mold, pests (rodents chewing on cables), dust accumulation

#### C. Technical / Infrastructure Failures
Failures of the underlying physical and logical infrastructure.

- **Hardware Failures:** Hard disk crashes, RAM bit-rot, CPU overheating, power supply burnout
- **Software Failures:** Operating system crashes, application freezes, firmware corruption
- **Network Failures:** ISP outages, DNS server failures, cable cuts, switch port failures
- **Power Anomalies:** Blackouts, brownouts, surges, spikes, and electromagnetic interference (EMI)

### 2.2 Deep Analytical Reasoning — The "Why" and "How"

**Why do unintentional threats dominate security statistics?**

1. **Human Cognitive Limitations:** Humans are biologically incapable of maintaining 100% vigilance. Fatigue, stress, and distraction are physiological inevitabilities.
2. **System Complexity:** Modern information systems contain millions of lines of code and thousands of configuration parameters. The probability of human error scales exponentially with complexity.
3. **Lack of Security Awareness:** Most employees are trained on *productivity tools*, not on *security hygiene*.
4. **Absence of Malicious Indicators:** Traditional signature-based security tools (antivirus, IDS) cannot detect accidental errors, because no "attack signature" exists.

**How do these threats propagate?**

$$
\text{Trigger Event} \rightarrow \text{Vulnerability Exposure} \rightarrow \text{System Compromise} \rightarrow \text{Impact on CIA}
$$

A single unintentional action can cascade across the entire stack — from physical layer to application layer — within seconds.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Risk Quantification for Unintentional Threats

Although unintentional threats lack intent, their **financial and operational impact is quantified using the same risk formulas** as intentional threats. The following table is **HIGH-YIELD** for KTU 14-mark questions.

| Formula Symbol | Name | Mathematical Expression | Unit / Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\text{SLE}$ | Single Loss Expectancy | $\text{SLE} = \text{Asset Value (AV)} \times \text{Exposure Factor (EF)}$ | Currency (₹ / $) |
| $\text{ARO}$ | Annualized Rate of Occurrence | Estimated frequency of threat per year | Dimensionless (events/year) |
| $\text{ALE}$ | Annualized Loss Expectancy | $\text{ALE} = \text{SLE} \times \text{ARO}$ | Currency per year |
| $\text{ROSI}$ | Return on Security Investment | $\text{ROSI} = \dfrac{\text{ALE}_{\text{before}} - \text{ALE}_{\text{after}} - \text{Control Cost}}{\text{Control Cost}}$ | Ratio (decimal) |
| $\text{MTBF}$ | Mean Time Between Failures | $\text{MTBF} = \dfrac{\text{Total Operational Time}}{\text{Number of Failures}}$ | Hours |
| $\text{MTTR}$ | Mean Time To Repair | $\text{MTTR} = \dfrac{\text{Total Downtime}}{\text{Number of Repairs}}$ | Hours |
| $\text{MTTF}$ | Mean Time To Failure | $\text{MTTF} = \dfrac{\text{Total Functional Time}}{\text{Number of Units Failed}}$ | Hours |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are used by **Risk Managers, CISOs, and IT Auditors** to justify security budgets to boards of directors. A KTU examiner loves questions that test whether you can apply ALE to real scenarios.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivations of Core Risk Formulas

> [!NOTE]
> The following derivations are exhaustive and aligned with the analytical depth expected in a KTU 14-mark Part B question. No steps are skipped.

#### Derivation 1: Single Loss Expectancy (SLE)

The **Single Loss Expectancy** represents the monetary loss expected from a *single occurrence* of a threat. It is derived from two observable quantities:

**Step 1: Define the Asset Value (AV)**

The Asset Value is the total replacement cost or business value of the information asset, including data, hardware, software, and the labor cost to recreate lost data.

$$
\text{AV} = \text{Hardware Cost} + \text{Software Cost} + \text{Data Reconstruction Cost} + \text{Productivity Loss}
$$

**Step 2: Define the Exposure Factor (EF)**

The Exposure Factor is the percentage (expressed as a decimal) of the asset that would be *destroyed, disclosed, or rendered unusable* in a single threat event.

$$
\text{EF} \in [0, 1] \quad \text{where} \quad 0\% \leq \text{EF} \leq 100\%
$$

**Step 3: Formulate the SLE Equation**

Multiplying the asset's total value by the fraction exposed gives the loss in a single event:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SLE} &= \text{Asset Value} \times \text{Exposure Factor} \\
\text{SLE} &= \text{AV} \times \text{EF}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** If a server worth ₹5,00,000 is exposed to a power surge that destroys **40%** of its components, the SLE is calculated by converting 40% to 0.40 and multiplying.

#### Derivation 2: Annualized Loss Expectancy (ALE)

**Step 1: Define the Annualized Rate of Occurrence (ARO)**

The ARO is the *estimated frequency* with which a threat is expected to occur in a 12-month period, based on historical data, actuarial tables, or industry benchmarks.

$$
\text{ARO} \in [0, \infty) \quad \text{(non-negative real number)}
$$

**Step 2: Conceptual Linkage**

If SLE is the cost of **one** event, and ARO is the **number** of events per year, then multiplying them yields the **expected annual loss**.

**Step 3: Formulate the ALE Equation**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ALE} &= \text{SLE} \times \text{ARO} \\
\text{ALE} &= (\text{AV} \times \text{EF}) \times \text{ARO}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** The ALE is the *insurance premium* equivalent. A company should ideally invest up to **ALE amount per year** in controls to mitigate the threat.

#### Derivation 3: Return on Security Investment (ROSI)

**Step 1: Define the Cost Components**
- $\text{ALE}_{\text{before}}$ = Annual loss expectancy *before* control implementation
- $\text{ALE}_{\text{after}}$ = Annual loss expectancy *after* control implementation
- $\text{Cost of Control (COC)}$ = Annualized cost of the security control (licenses, training, salaries)

**Step 2: Calculate Net Savings**

$$
\text{Net Savings} = \text{ALE}_{\text{before}} - \text{ALE}_{\text{after}} - \text{COC}
$$

**Step 3: Formulate the ROSI Equation**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ROSI} &= \dfrac{\text{Net Savings}}{\text{Cost of Control}} \\
\text{ROSI} &= \dfrac{\text{ALE}_{\text{before}} - \text{ALE}_{\text{after}} - \text{COC}}{\text{COC}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** A positive ROSI indicates the control is financially justified. A negative ROSI means the control costs more than the threat it prevents.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board-Standard)

> **Problem Statement:** A company hosts a database server in a coastal region. The server's total asset value is **₹10,00,000**. Historical records show that flooding occurs approximately **once every 4 years**, and a flood event would damage **70%** of the server's components. The company is considering installing flood barriers at an annual cost of **₹50,000**, which would reduce the Exposure Factor from 70% to 10% and the ARO to once every 20 years.
>
> **Calculate:** (i) SLE, (ii) ALE before controls, (iii) ALE after controls, (iv) ROSI of the flood barrier.

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Identify the Variables**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{AV} &= ₹10,00,000 \\
\text{EF}_{\text{before}} &= 0.70 \quad (70\%) \\
\text{EF}_{\text{after}} &= 0.10 \quad (10\%) \\
\text{ARO}_{\text{before}} &= \dfrac{1}{4} = 0.25 \text{ per year} \\
\text{ARO}_{\text{after}} &= \dfrac{1}{20} = 0.05 \text{ per year} \\
\text{COC} &= ₹50,000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate SLE (Before Control)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SLE}_{\text{before}} &= \text{AV} \times \text{EF}_{\text{before}} \\
\text{SLE}_{\text{before}} &= 10,00,000 \times 0.70 \\
\text{SLE}_{\text{before}} &= ₹7,00,000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate SLE (After Control)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SLE}_{\text{after}} &= \text{AV} \times \text{EF}_{\text{after}} \\
\text{SLE}_{\text{after}} &= 10,00,000 \times 0.10 \\
\text{SLE}_{\text{after}} &= ₹1,00,000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Calculate ALE (Before Control)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ALE}_{\text{before}} &= \text{SLE}_{\text{before}} \times \text{ARO}_{\text{before}} \\
\text{ALE}_{\text{before}} &= 7,00,000 \times 0.25 \\
\text{ALE}_{\text{before}} &= ₹1,75,000 \text{ per year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Calculate ALE (After Control)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ALE}_{\text{after}} &= \text{SLE}_{\text{after}} \times \text{ARO}_{\text{after}} \\
\text{ALE}_{\text{after}} &= 1,00,000 \times 0.05 \\
\text{ALE}_{\text{after}} &= ₹5,000 \text{ per year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Calculate ROSI**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ROSI} &= \dfrac{\text{ALE}_{\text{before}} - \text{ALE}_{\text{after}} - \text{COC}}{\text{COC}} \\
\text{ROSI} &= \dfrac{1,75,000 - 5,000 - 50,000}{50,000} \\
\text{ROSI} &= \dfrac{1,20,000}{50,000} \\
\text{ROSI} &= 2.4 \quad \text{or} \quad 240\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** A ROSI of **2.4 (240%)** means that for every ₹1 spent on flood barriers, the company saves ₹2.40 in expected losses. The control is **strongly justified** from a financial risk perspective.

### 3.3 Mitigation Strategy Matrix for Unintentional Threats

| Threat Category | Specific Threat | Recommended Control | KTU-Mapped CO |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Human Error** | Accidental file deletion | Automated backups, Recycle Bin policies, RBAC | CO2 |
| **Human Error** | Misdirected email | DLP (Data Loss Prevention) tools, email filters | CO2 |
| **Human Error** | Patch negligence | Automated patch management systems (e.g., WSUS) | CO3 |
| **Natural** | Flood / Earthquake | Geographic redundancy, offsite backups, flood sensors | CO4 |
| **Natural** | Fire | FM-200 suppression, fire-resistant server cabinets | CO4 |
| **Technical** | Power surge | UPS, surge protectors, diesel generators | CO3 |
| **Technical** | Hardware failure | RAID, hot-swappable components, MTBF tracking | CO3 |
| **Environmental** | Heat / Humidity | Precision AC (CRAC units), humidity sensors | CO4 |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Taxonomy of Unintentional Threats

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[UNINTENTIONAL THREATS TO IS] --> B[Human Errors]
    A --> C[Environmental & Natural Threats]
    A --> D[Technical Infrastructure Failures]

    B --> B1[Operator Errors]
    B --> B2[Developer Errors]
    B --> B3[End User Errors]
    B --> B4[Administrator Errors]

    B1 --> B1a[Misconfiguration]
    B1 --> B1b[Wrong Commands]
    B2 --> B2a[Buffer Overflows]
    B2 --> B2b[Hardcoded Credentials]
    B3 --> B3a[Accidental Deletion]
    B3 --> B3b[Misdirected Emails]
    B3 --> B3c[Lost Devices]
    B4 --> B4a[Patch Negligence]
    B4 --> B4b[Log Mishandling]

    C --> C1[Natural Disasters]
    C --> C2[Climatic Anomalies]
    C --> C3[Biological Threats]

    C1 --> C1a[Floods]
```
