```mermaid
    C1 --> C1b[Earthquakes]
    C1 --> C1c[Lightning Strikes]
    C2 --> C2a[Extreme Heat]
    C2 --> C2b[High Humidity]
    C2 --> C2c[Static Discharge]
    C3 --> C3a[Dust Accumulation]
    C3 --> C3b[Pest Damage]

    D --> D1[Hardware Failures]
    D --> D2[Software Failures]
    D --> D3[Power Anomalies]
    D --> D4[Network Failures]

    D1 --> D1a[Disk Crash]
    D1 --> D1b[Component Burnout]
    D2 --> D2a[OS Crash]
    D2 --> D2b[Firmware Corruption]
    D3 --> D3a[Blackout]
    D3 --> D3b[Brownout]
    D3 --> D3c[Surges and Spikes]
    D4 --> D4a[ISP Outage]
    D4 --> D4b[DNS Failure]
    D4 --> D4c[Cable Cut]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Flow Diagram: Unintentional Threat Impact Cascade

```mermaid
graph TD
    P1[Phase 1: Trigger Event] --> P2[Phase 2: Vulnerability Exposed]
    P2 --> P3[Phase 3: System Component Affected]
    P3 --> P4[Phase 4: CIA Triad Violation]
    P4 --> P5[Phase 5: Business Impact Realized]

    P1 --> P1a[Example: Power surge]
    P2 --> P2a[Unprotected PSU]
    P3 --> P3a[Server motherboard damaged]
    P4 --> P4a[Availability lost, Integrity at risk]
    P5 --> P5a[Downtime cost, Data loss, Reputation damage]

    P5 --> P6[Phase 6: Mitigation Response]
    P6 --> P6a[UPS Installation]
    P6 --> P6b[Disaster Recovery Activation]
    P6 --> P6c[Insurance Claim]
    P6 --> P6d[Post Incident Review]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: Risk Quantification Process Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Identify Information Asset] --> B[Determine Asset Value AV]
    B --> C[Identify Unintentional Threats]
    C --> D[Calculate Exposure Factor EF]
    D --> E[Compute SLE]
    E --> F[Estimate ARO from Historical Data]
    F --> G[Compute ALE]
    G --> H{ROSI Positive?}
    H -->|Yes| I[Implement Control]
    H -->|No| J[Accept Risk or Seek Cheaper Control]
    I --> K[Monitor and Reassess Annually]
    J --> K
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** Each block represents a discrete stage in the risk management lifecycle as defined by NIST SP 800-30. The branching at "ROSI Positive?" mirrors the decision logic a CISO uses to allocate security budgets.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Question 1, 3 Marks]**
> **Q: Differentiate between intentional and unintentional threats to information systems. Give two examples of unintentional threats.**

**Model Answer:**

Intentional threats are deliberate, malicious actions taken to compromise an information system, whereas unintentional threats occur **without any malicious intent** and result from human mistakes, natural events, or technical failures.

| Aspect | Intentional Threat | Unintentional Threat |
|---|---|---|
| Intent | Malicious | Absent |
| Examples | Malware, Phishing, DDoS | Accidental deletion, Power outage |
| Detection | Signature-based, behavioral | Anomaly-based, process audits |

**Two Examples of Unintentional Threats:**
1. An employee accidentally formats the production database.
2. A flood destroys the on-premise server room.

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Question 2, 3 Marks]**
> **Q: Define Single Loss Expectancy (SLE) and Annualized Loss Expectancy (ALE). State the relationship between them.**

**Model Answer:**

- **SLE (Single Loss Expectancy):** The expected monetary loss from a *single occurrence* of a threat. It is calculated as: $\text{SLE} = \text{AV} \times \text{EF}$.
- **ALE (Annualized Loss Expectancy):** The expected monetary loss from a threat over a *one-year period*. It is calculated as: $\text{ALE} = \text{SLE} \times \text{ARO}$.

**Relationship:** ALE is the *product* of SLE and ARO. It quantifies the yearly financial exposure and is used to justify security control investments.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Question, 14 Marks]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Classify unintentional threats to information systems in detail. Discuss human errors with suitable examples. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A laptop containing customer credit card data is valued at ₹80,000. The probability of it being lost in a year is 0.4, and a loss event exposes 60% of the data. Calculate the SLE and ALE. The company plans to implement full-disk encryption at an annual cost of ₹10,000, which will reduce the Exposure Factor to 5%. Compute the ROSI. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution for (a):**

Unintentional threats are categorized into three main classes:

**1. Human Errors** — The most prevalent class, arising from mistakes by users, operators, developers, and administrators.
- *Operator Errors:* A network admin types `shutdown -r now` on a production server instead of a test server, causing downtime.
- *Developer Errors:* A programmer forgets to sanitize user input, creating a SQL injection vulnerability.
- *End-User Errors:* An employee replies-all to an email containing salary information.
- *Administrator Errors:* Failure to apply a critical security patch leaves a system vulnerable to a known exploit.

**2. Environmental and Natural Threats** — Caused by nature and physical surroundings.
- Floods, earthquakes, lightning, hurricanes
- Extreme temperatures, humidity, static electricity
- Dust, pests, biological contamination

**3. Technical and Infrastructure Failures** — Malfunctions in hardware, software, or utilities.
- Hardware: disk crashes, PSU burnout, RAM failure
- Software: OS crashes, firmware corruption, application bugs
- Power: blackouts, brownouts, surges, spikes
- Network: cable cuts, switch failures, DNS outages

> Each class can compromise the **CIA Triad** — for instance, a power surge threatens **Availability**, while a misconfigured database may compromise **Confidentiality**.

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1: Identify the Variables**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{AV} &= ₹80,000 \\
\text{EF}_{\text{before}} &= 0.60 \\
\text{EF}_{\text{after}} &= 0.05 \\
\text{ARO} &= 0.4 \text{ per year} \\
\text{COC} &= ₹10,000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate SLE (Before Control)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SLE}_{\text{before}} &= \text{AV} \times \text{EF}_{\text{before}} \\
\text{SLE}_{\text{before}} &= 80,000 \times 0.60 = ₹48,000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate ALE (Before Control)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ALE}_{\text{before}} &= \text{SLE}_{\text{before}} \times \text{ARO} \\
\text{ALE}_{\text{before}} &= 48,000 \times 0.4 = ₹19,200
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Calculate SLE (After Control)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SLE}_{\text{after}} &= \text{AV} \times \text{EF}_{\text{after}} \\
\text{SLE}_{\text{after}} &= 80,000 \times 0.05 = ₹4,000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Calculate ALE (After Control)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ALE}_{\text{after}} &= 4,000 \times 0.4 = ₹1,600
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Calculate ROSI**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ROSI} &= \dfrac{\text{ALE}_{\text{before}} - \text{ALE}_{\text{after}} - \text{COC}}{\text{COC}} \\
\text{ROSI} &= \dfrac{19,200 - 1,600 - 10,000}{10,000} \\
\text{ROSI} &= \dfrac{7,600}{10,000} = 0.76 \text{ or } 76\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conclusion:** A ROSI of 76% indicates the encryption investment is financially justified, recovering ₹0.76 net for every ₹1 spent.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)**

**(a)** Explain in detail the various natural and environmental threats to information systems. Describe the control measures for each. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A data center in a seismic zone houses servers worth ₹50,00,000. Earthquakes occur on average once every 10 years, and a quake damages 80% of the assets. The proposed seismic retrofitting costs ₹1,50,000 annually and reduces both EF to 20% and ARO to once in 50 years. Calculate the SLE, ALE (before and after), and ROSI. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution for (a):**

Natural and environmental threats are physical events that can damage infrastructure, disrupt operations, and destroy data.

| Threat | Description | Control Measures |
|---|---|---|
| **Floods** | Water damage to hardware and short-circuits | Elevated server rooms, waterproof barriers, sump pumps, offsite backups |
| **Earthquakes** | Structural collapse, equipment displacement | Seismic racks, anchor bolts, structural retrofitting, geographic redundancy |
| **Hurricanes / Tornadoes** | High-velocity winds, flying debris | Reinforced buildings, storm shutters, redundant cloud infrastructure |
| **Lightning** | Power surges and electromagnetic interference | Lightning rods, surge protectors, isolated grounding systems |
| **Extreme Heat** | Component overheating, thermal shutdown | Precision air conditioning (CRAC), hot/cold aisle containment, temperature monitoring |
| **Excessive Humidity** | Condensation, corrosion, short circuits | Dehumidifiers, humidity sensors, sealed server rooms |
| **Static Electricity** | ESD damage to sensitive chips | Anti-static flooring, wrist straps, ionizers, humidity control (40-60% RH) |
| **Fire** | Total destruction of assets | FM-200 / Novec 1230 gas suppression, smoke detectors, fire-resistant cabinets |
| **Dust / Pests** | Clogging, short circuits, cable damage | Sealed server rooms, regular cleaning, pest control, positive air pressure |

> **Best Practice:** Adopt a **Defense in Depth (DiD)** strategy that combines physical, logical, and administrative controls to mitigate environmental risks comprehensively.

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1: Identify the Variables**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{AV} &= ₹50,00,000 \\
\text{EF}_{\text{before}} &= 0.80 \\
\text{EF}_{\text{after}} &= 0.20 \\
\text{ARO}_{\text{before}} &= 0.10 \text{ per year} \\
\text{ARO}_{\text{after}} &= 0.02 \text{ per year} \\
\text{COC} &= ₹1,50,000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: SLE (Before and After)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SLE}_{\text{before}} &= 50,00,000 \times 0.80 = ₹40,00,000 \\
\text{SLE}_{\text{after}} &= 50,00,000 \times 0.20 = ₹10,00,000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: ALE (Before and After)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ALE}_{\text{before}} &= 40,00,000 \times 0.10 = ₹4,00,000 \\
\text{ALE}_{\text{after}} &= 10,00,000 \times 0.02 = ₹20,000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: ROSI**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ROSI} &= \dfrac{4,00,000 - 20,000 - 1,50,000}{1,50,000} \\
\text{ROSI} &= \dfrac{2,30,000}{1,50,000} = 1.533 \text{ or } 153.3\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conclusion:** The seismic retrofitting has a strong positive ROSI of 153.3%, making it a highly recommended control.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common Mistake #1:** Students frequently forget to convert percentages to decimals in the EF (e.g., using 60 instead of 0.60). This single error loses 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Common Mistake #2:** The ARO must be expressed in *events per year*, not events per decade. Divide accordingly.
> 3. **Common Mistake #3:** ROSI must include the *Cost of Control* in the numerator subtraction. Many students forget to subtract the COC, which inflates the ROSI incorrectly.
> 4. **Common Mistake #4:** Do not confuse MTTR with MTBF. MTTR is *repair* time; MTBF is *between* failures. Examiners test this distinction.
> 5. **Common Mistake #5:** When classifying threats, students often list "hacking" or "malware" — these are **intentional** threats and will be marked wrong in an unintentional threat question.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Revision Checklist:**

- **Definition:** Unintentional threats are dangers to IS arising **without malicious intent** from humans, nature, or technology.
- **Three Primary Classes:** Human Errors, Environmental/Natural Threats, Technical Infrastructure Failures.
- **Human Errors Sub-Categories:** Operator, Developer, End-User, and Administrator errors.
- **Most Common Human Error:** Misdirected emails and misconfigurations.
- **Environmental Threats:** Floods, earthquakes, fire, heat, humidity, static electricity, dust.
- **Technical Failures:** Hardware crashes, software bugs, power anomalies, network outages.
- **Power Anomaly Types:** Blackout (complete loss), Brownout (voltage drop), Surge (voltage spike), Spike (transient).
- **Core Formulas (Must Memorize):**
  - $\text{SLE} = \text{AV} \times \text{EF}$
  - $\text{ALE} = \text{SLE} \times \text{ARO}$
  - $\text{ROSI} = \dfrac{\text{ALE}_{\text{before}} - \text{ALE}_{\text{after}} - \text{COC}}{\text{COC}}$
  - $\text{MTBF} = \dfrac{\text{Total Operational Time}}{\text{Number of Failures}}$
- **EF is a decimal** (0 to 1), not a percentage when substituted into formulas.
- **ARO unit:** Events per year (always normalize to annual basis).
- **CIA Triad Linkage:** Every unintentional threat violates at least one of **Confidentiality, Integrity, or Availability**.
- **Mitigation Principles:** Defense in Depth, Principle of Least Privilege, Regular Backups, Redundancy, UPS, RAID, and Security Awareness Training.
- **Industry Stat to Quote:** ~52–60% of all security incidents stem from unintentional human errors (cite DBIR or IBM Cost of a Data Breach Report).
- **Key Distinction:** Unintentional ≠ Harmless. Damage magnitude can equal intentional attacks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Deliberate Threats to Information Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT523 - INFORMATION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Deliberate Threats to Information Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Deliberate Threats to Information Systems** are intentional, human-driven malicious actions designed to compromise the **Confidentiality, Integrity, and Availability (CIA Triad)** of an information system. Unlike accidental threats (natural disasters, human error), deliberate threats originate from a conscious adversary — commonly termed a **Threat Actor** — operating with motive, means, and opportunity.

According to KTU 2024 Scheme terminology aligned with NIST SP 800-30 and ISO/IEC 27005, a deliberate threat is characterized by:

- **Intent**: A purposeful goal (financial gain, espionage, disruption, revenge, ideology).
- **Capability**: Technical skill level of the adversary.
- **Target Specificity**: Focused exploitation of a known system vulnerability.
- **Origin**: Internal (insider) or External (outsider).

### Threat Classification Vocabulary

| Term | Definition (KTU Standard) |
|---|---|
| **Threat** | Any potential cause of an unwanted incident that may result in harm to a system. |
| **Vulnerability** | A weakness in an IS that can be exploited by a threat. |
| **Attack** | The actual exploitation attempt against a vulnerability. |
| **Threat Actor** | An individual or group responsible for an incident impacting the security of an IS. |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Bank Vault"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Box:** Imagine a bank vault protecting gold bars. The vault is your *Information System*. The gold bars are your *Data*. An *accidental threat* is like an earthquake shaking the vault. A **deliberate threat** is a thief who studies the lock mechanism, picks it deliberately, and walks out with the gold. The thief chooses the vault, plans the method, and executes with intent.

This analogy helps visualize that every deliberate threat has three key elements:
1. **A motivated adversary** (the thief).
2. **A known or discovered weakness** (a faulty lock).
3. **An executed exploitation** (picking the lock).

## 1.3 The CIA Triad — The Target of Every Deliberate Threat

Every deliberate attack in KTU syllabus is mapped against one or more pillars:

- **C — Confidentiality**: Preventing unauthorized disclosure of data. *(Violation = Data Breach)*
- **I — Integrity**: Preventing unauthorized modification of data. *(Violation = Data Tampering)*
- **A — Availability**: Ensuring timely, reliable access to data. *(Violation = Denial of Service)*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** All categories of deliberate threats discussed in Module 2 must be analyzed in terms of which CIA pillar they primarily violate. Examiners frequently frame 3-mark questions around identifying the violated security property.

## 1.4 Categories of Threat Actors (KTU 2024 Taxonomy)

| Threat Actor Type | Description | Typical Motive |
|---|---|---|
| **Hackers (Black Hat)** | Unauthorized intruders with malicious intent. | Financial, fame, disruption |
| **Hackers (White Hat)** | Ethical security professionals. | Defense, auditing |
| **Hacktivists** | Politically/socially motivated attackers. | Ideology, protest |
| **Cybercriminals** | Organized criminal networks. | Money, fraud |
| **Insiders** | Disgruntled employees, contractors. | Revenge, financial gain |
| **Nation-State Actors** | Government-sponsored APT groups. | Espionage, sabotage |
| **Script Kiddies** | Unskilled attackers using pre-built tools. | Curiosity, minor mischief |
| **Competitors** | Corporate rivals conducting espionage. | Market advantage |

## 1.5 Why This Topic Matters in KTU

> [!NOTE]
> Information Systems managers must understand the threat landscape to design effective **Risk Management Frameworks (RMF)**. The KTU 2024 Scheme CO statements for this module require students to *identify, classify, and propose countermeasures* for deliberate threats — making this a guaranteed 14-mark question territory.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Major Categories of Deliberate Threats

### 2.1.1 Malware (Malicious Software)

Malware is the broadest and most persistent class of deliberate threats. KTU syllabus demands a deep understanding of each subtype.

**A. Viruses**
- A **computer virus** is a self-replicating program that attaches itself to a legitimate host file (executable, document, boot sector).
- Requires **user action** to execute (e.g., opening an infected file).
- Lifecycle: **Dormant → Propagation → Triggering → Execution**.
- Payload types: Data destruction, logic bombs, file corruption, backdoors.

**B. Worms**
- Self-contained programs that propagate **without human intervention** across networks.
- Exploit OS vulnerabilities and use network protocols (SMTP, SMB, RPC) for self-replication.
- Famous example: **ILOVEYOU, Code Red, Conficker, WannaCry**.

**C. Trojan Horses**
- Disguised as legitimate/benign software but carry hidden malicious payloads.
- **Do not self-replicate** (key difference from virus/worm).
- Types: **Backdoor Trojans, Banking Trojans, RATs (Remote Access Trojans), Downloader Trojans**.

**D. Ransomware**
- Encrypts victim data using strong cryptography (RSA-2048, AES-256) and demands ransom (typically cryptocurrency).
- **Doxware/Leakware**: Threatens to publish stolen data publicly.
- Famous examples: **WannaCry, NotPetya, REvil, LockBit, Conti**.

**E. Spyware & Adware**
- **Spyware** secretly monitors user activity and transmits data to a third party (keyloggers, screen scrapers).
- **Adware** displays unwanted advertisements; can be a delivery vector for spyware.

**F. Rootkits**
- Covert programs that gain **privileged (root/admin) access** while hiding their existence from the OS.
- Types: User-mode rootkits, Kernel-mode rootkits, Bootkits, Firmware rootkits.

**G. Botnets**
- Network of **compromised machines (bots/zombies)** controlled via a **Command & Control (C&C)** server.
- Used for **DDoS attacks, spam, cryptocurrency mining, click fraud**.

**H. Logic Bombs**
- Malicious code that triggers a payload only when specific conditions are met (date, event, file deletion).
- Often planted by **insiders** with authorized access.

**I. Zero-Day Exploits**
- Exploits targeting **unknown vulnerabilities** (no patch available).
- Highly valued in black markets; used in **Advanced Persistent Threats (APTs)**.

### 2.1.2 Hacking & Unauthorized Access

- **Password Cracking**: Brute force, dictionary attack, rainbow tables, credential stuffing.
- **SQL Injection (SQLi)**: Injects malicious SQL statements into input fields to manipulate databases.
- **Cross-Site Scripting (XSS)**: Injects client-side scripts into web pages viewed by other users.
- **Man-in-the-Middle (MITM)**: Attacker secretly relays/alters communication between two parties.
- **Session Hijacking**: Steals a valid session token to impersonate an authenticated user.
- **Privilege Escalation**: Exploiting bugs to gain elevated access (vertical/horizontal).
- **Buffer Overflow**: Overwriting memory of a process to execute arbitrary code.

### 2.1.3 Social Engineering

The **psychological manipulation of people** into performing security mistakes or divulging confidential information.

| Attack Type | Mechanism | Countermeasure |
|---|---|---|
| **Phishing** | Fraudulent emails mimicking trusted entities. | Email filtering, user training. |
| **Spear Phishing** | Targeted phishing aimed at specific individuals. | Multi-factor authentication (MFA). |
| **Whaling** | Phishing aimed at high-level executives (CEO, CFO). | Strict executive protocols. |
| **Vishing** | Voice phishing via telephone calls. | Caller verification, no remote access. |
| **Smishing** | SMS-based phishing. | SMS filtering, awareness. |
| **Pretexting** | Fabricated scenario to steal info. | Identity verification procedures. |
| **Baiting** | Leaving infected USB drives in public. | Disable autorun, security policies. |
| **Tailgating/Piggybacking** | Following authorized person into restricted area. | Mantraps, badge access. |
| **Quid Pro Quo** | Offering a service in exchange for info. | Awareness training. |

### 2.1.4 Denial of Service (DoS) & Distributed DoS (DDoS)

- **DoS**: Single source overwhelms target with traffic or resource requests.
- **DDoS**: Multiple compromised systems (botnet) flood target simultaneously.
- **Attack Vectors**:
  - **SYN Flood**: Exploits TCP three-way handshake; leaves half-open connections.
  - **UDP Flood**: Overwhelms with UDP packets to random ports.
  - **HTTP Flood**: Layer 7 attack sending massive GET/POST requests.
  - **Ping of Death**: Oversized ICMP packets.
  - **Smurf Attack**: ICMP echo requests with spoofed victim IP to amplifiers.
  - **DNS Amplification**: Small DNS queries producing huge responses aimed at victim.
  - **Slowloris**: Holds HTTP connections open to exhaust server resources.

### 2.1.5 Insider Threats

- **Malicious Insiders**: Employees, contractors, or business partners who intentionally cause harm.
- **Negligent Insiders**: Unintentionally cause breaches through carelessness.
- **Compromised Insiders**: Legitimate credentials stolen and misused by external attackers.
- **Privilege Abuse**: Misuse of legitimate access rights.

### 2.1.6 Cyber Terrorism & Information Warfare

- **Cyber Terrorism**: Politically motivated attacks against information systems to cause fear, disruption, or destruction.
- **Information Warfare**: Strategic use of information to gain advantage over an adversary (e.g., propaganda, disruption of enemy C4 systems).

### 2.1.7 Identity Theft & Data Exfiltration

- **Identity Theft**: Stealing personal identifying information (PII) to impersonate victims.
- **Data Exfiltration**: Unauthorized transfer of data from an organization. May use **steganography, DNS tunneling, HTTPS covert channels**.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula / Metrics Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> KTU questions on this topic may require the application of risk formulas. Use LaTeX rendering carefully — no vertical pipes inside tables.

| Metric / Formula | Equation | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Annualized Loss Expectancy | $ALE = SLE \times ARO$ | Expected yearly loss from a threat. |
| Single Loss Expectancy | $SLE = Asset\ Value \times Exposure\ Factor$ | Loss from a single occurrence. |
| Annualized Rate of Occurrence | $ARO$ (dimensionless number) | Estimated frequency per year. |
| Risk | $Risk = Threat \times Vulnerability \times Impact$ | Composite risk score. |
| DDoS Impact | $Impact \propto Botnet\ Size \times Request\ Rate$ | Amplification factor. |
| Encryption Strength | $Strength \approx 2^n / MTTF$ | Where $n$ is key bits and $MTTF$ is mean time to break. |
| Shannon Entropy (Information Content) | $H(X) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p(x_i) \log_2 p(x_i)$ | Bits needed to encode data (used in stego analysis). |
| RSA Key Strength | $n = p \times q$ | Security depends on $n$ bit length ($n \geq 2048$ bits recommended). |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Understanding deliberate threats is not academic — it directly enables:

- **Enterprise Risk Management (ERM)**: Quantifying financial exposure.
- **Incident Response Planning (IRP)**: Predefined procedures for breach handling.
- **Business Continuity Planning (BCP)**: Maintaining operations during attacks.
- **Cyber Insurance Underwriting**: Premium calculation based on threat exposure.
- **Compliance Mapping**: GDPR, HIPAA, PCI-DSS, India's **Digital Personal Data Protection Act 2023**, and the **IT Act 2000** all reference deliberate threat mitigation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Worked Examples

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Calculating ALE for a Ransomware Attack

> [!NOTE]
> This is a frequently asked 7-mark analytical question in KTU ESE. Understanding the step-by-step risk computation is essential.

**Problem Statement:**
An organization has a customer database valued at **₹50,00,000 (50 lakhs)**. A risk assessment estimates that a ransomware attack would compromise **60%** of the database (i.e., the *Exposure Factor* $EF = 0.6$). Historical data indicates that such attacks occur approximately **2 times per year** ($ARO = 2$).

Compute the **Single Loss Expectancy (SLE)** and **Annualized Loss Expectancy (ALE)**.

### Step 1: Identify the Given Values

We extract the input parameters from the problem statement:

$$Asset\ Value\ (AV) = \text{₹}50{,}00{,}000$$

$$Exposure\ Factor\ (EF) = 0.60$$

$$Annualized\ Rate\ of\ Occurrence\ (ARO) = 2$$

### Step 2: Apply the SLE Formula

The Single Loss Expectancy represents the financial impact of a single occurrence of the threat. We substitute the values into the KTU standard formula:

$$
\begin{aligned}
SLE &= AV \times EF \\
&= \text{₹}50{,}00{,}000 \times 0.60 \\
&= \text{₹}30{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** A single successful ransomware attack would cause a direct loss of **₹30 lakhs** to the organization.

### Step 3: Apply the ALE Formula

The Annualized Loss Expectancy aggregates the expected loss over a one-year horizon. We multiply the SLE by the frequency of occurrence:

$$
\begin{aligned}
ALE &= SLE \times ARO \\
&= \text{₹}30{,}00{,}000 \times 2 \\
&= \text{₹}60{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** The organization should expect an average annual loss of **₹60 lakhs** to ransomware threats.

### Step 4: Decision Criterion

> [!IMPORTANT]
> If the cost of a proposed countermeasure (e.g., endpoint detection & response system) is **less than ₹60 lakhs/year**, the control is **justified economically** based on ALE savings — this is the foundational *Cost-Benefit Analysis (CBA)* principle in information security management.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Mapping Threats to the CIA Triad

**Problem Statement:** Classify the following attacks under the CIA Triad and justify your answer.

1. A phishing attack that captures an employee's login credentials.
2. A DDoS attack against a bank's online portal.
3. A disgruntled employee alters salary records in the HR database.

### Step 1: Analyze Attack #1 — Phishing

The attacker's goal is to **read** or **steal** sensitive information. The data is not modified, and the system remains online.

- Confidentiality: **VIOLATED** (credentials are exposed to the attacker).
- Integrity: Not violated.
- Availability: Not violated.

**Primary Violation: Confidentiality**

### Step 2: Analyze Attack #2 — DDoS

The attacker's goal is to make the banking portal **unreachable** by legitimate customers. Data is not stolen or modified, but the service is denied.

- Confidentiality: Not violated.
- Integrity: Not violated.
- Availability: **VIOLATED** (service is down).

**Primary Violation: Availability**

### Step 3: Analyze Attack #3 — Salary Tampering

The attacker **modifies** the integrity of the HR records. The data's trustworthiness is destroyed.

- Confidentiality: Not violated (data is not disclosed).
- Integrity: **VIOLATED** (records are altered).
- Availability: Not violated.

**Primary Violation: Integrity**

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Botnet Strength Estimation

**Problem Statement:** A botnet consists of 10,000 infected machines. Each machine generates 50 HTTP requests per second towards a target server. The target server can handle 200,000 requests per second. Determine whether the DDoS attack will be successful.

### Step 1: Compute the Aggregate Attack Rate

We sum the request generation rates of all bots in the network:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Attack\ Rate &= Number\ of\ Bots \times Request\ Rate\ per\ Bot \\
&= 10{,}000 \times 50 \\
&= 500{,}000\ requests\ per\ second
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2: Compare Against Server Capacity

The target server can sustain 200,000 RPS. Since the attack rate exceeds this capacity:

$$
500{,}000 > 200{,}000
$$

### Step 3: Determine Overload Ratio

$$
\begin{aligned}
Overload\ Ratio &= \frac{Attack\ Rate}{Server\ Capacity} \\
&= \frac{500{,}000}{200{,}000} \\
&= 2.5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** The server is flooded with **2.5× its capacity**, ensuring a successful DoS. Real defenders would need to add scrubbing centers, Anycast networks, or deploy **Web Application Firewalls (WAFs)** to absorb this traffic.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Shannon Entropy in Steganalysis

**Problem Statement:** A security analyst suspects steganography in a 1 MB image file. Compute the entropy of the byte distribution if the byte values follow the probability table below.

| Byte Value | $p(x_i)$ |
|---|---|
| 0 | 0.5 |
| 1 | 0.25 |
| 2 | 0.125 |
| 3 | 0.125 |

### Step 1: Verify Probability Closure

$$
\sum_{i} p(x_i) = 0.5 + 0.25 + 0.125 + 0.125 = 1.000 \quad \checkmark
$$

### Step 2: Apply Shannon's Formula

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(X) &= -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p(x_i) \log_2 p(x_i) \\
&= -[0.5 \log_2 0.5 + 0.25 \log_2 0.25 + 0.125 \log_2 0.125 + 0.125 \log_2 0.125] \\
&= -[0.5 \times (-1) + 0.25 \times (-2) + 0.125 \times (-3) + 0.125 \times (-3)] \\
&= -[-0.5 - 0.5 - 0.375 - 0.375] \\
&= 1.75\ \text{bits/byte}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** A stego-image with embedded encrypted data typically shows entropy approaching **8 bits/byte** (maximum randomness). The current 1.75 bits/byte suggests the data is structured or compressed — not yet a confirmed stego signal, but warrants deeper analysis.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
