# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> The following questions are aligned with the KTU 2024 Scheme Bloom's Taxonomy distribution (L1–L6) and follow the standard ESE pattern: **Part A (3 marks)** and **Part B (14 marks with internal choice)**.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between a virus and a worm with two key technical distinctions.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Virus | Worm |
|---|---|---|
| **Replication Trigger** | Requires user action (opening a file, running an executable). | Self-propagating; no user interaction required. |
| **Scope of Spread** | Spreads via file sharing, removable media, email attachments within a single host or via user-mediated transfer. | Spreads autonomously across networks using protocols (TCP/IP, SMB). |
| **Carrier** | Needs a host file (executable, document, boot sector). | Self-contained executable; no host file required. |
| **Network Dependency** | Not strictly required. | Requires network connectivity. |
| **Example** | Melissa, CIH (Chernobyl). | ILOVEYOU, Conficker, WannaCry worm module. |

**Marks Allocation (3 marks):**
- Correct identification of the **trigger** mechanism (1 mark).
- Correct identification of the **scope of spread** (1 mark).
- One valid example for each (1 mark).

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is phishing? List three types of phishing and one countermeasure for each.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Phishing is a social engineering attack where the attacker impersonates a trusted entity (e.g., bank, IT department, government) via digital communication to trick victims into revealing sensitive information such as credentials, OTPs, or financial data.

| Phishing Type | Description | Countermeasure |
|---|---|---|
| **Email Phishing** | Mass-mailed fraudulent emails with malicious links. | DMARC/DKIM/SPF email authentication, user awareness training. |
| **Spear Phishing** | Targeted phishing aimed at specific individuals based on gathered OSINT. | Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA), strict email filtering, sandboxing of URLs. |
| **Smishing** | SMS-based phishing with malicious links or OTP theft requests. | SMS filtering, user awareness, never share OTP via SMS. |
| **Vishing** | Voice-call phishing (fake bank, fake IT support). | Caller-ID verification, callback to official numbers, no remote access. |
| **Whaling** | Phishing aimed at C-level executives. | Dedicated executive security training, hardware security keys, segregated devices. |

**Marks Allocation (3 marks):**
- Correct definition (1 mark).
- Three valid types with examples (1.5 marks — 0.5 each).
- Three corresponding countermeasures (0.5 marks combined).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**"Deliberate threats targeting an Information System can be classified into multiple categories. Discuss the major categories, illustrate each with a real-world example, and outline the corresponding defense strategy."**

#### **Part (a) — Classify the major categories of deliberate threats with real-world examples. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Deliberate threats to Information Systems are intentional attacks orchestrated by threat actors to compromise the **CIA Triad**. The major categories are:

**1. Malware-Based Threats**

Malicious software designed to infiltrate, damage, or gain unauthorized access. Subcategories:

- **Viruses** — Example: *Melissa Virus (1999)* propagated via Microsoft Word email attachments, causing $80 million in damages by overwhelming email servers. Defense: Install reputable antivirus, disable macros by default, patch OS regularly.
- **Worms** — Example: *Conficker (2008)* infected millions of Windows machines by exploiting the MS08-067 vulnerability. Defense: Network segmentation, patch management, host-based firewalls.
- **Trojan Horses** — Example: *Emotet* disguised as a legitimate document, delivering additional malware like TrickBot and Ryuk. Defense: Application whitelisting, user awareness training, behavioral analysis tools.
- **Ransomware** — Example: *WannaCry (2017)* exploited the EternalBlue vulnerability to encrypt data on 230,000+ computers worldwide, demanding Bitcoin ransom. Defense: Regular offline backups, patch SMB protocol, EDR solutions, network segmentation.
- **Spyware** — Example: *Pegasus* by NSO Group, used for surveillance of journalists and activists. Defense: Endpoint detection, mobile device management, app vetting.
- **Rootkits** — Example: *Sony BMG Rootkit (2005)* hidden in DRM music CDs. Defense: TPM-based boot integrity, kernel-level anti-rootkit tools, secure boot.

**2. Network-Based Threats**

- **DoS / DDoS** — Example: *Mirai Botnet (2016)* used 100,000+ IoT devices to disrupt DNS provider Dyn, taking down Twitter, Netflix, Reddit. Defense: Anycast routing, scrubbing centers, rate limiting, ISP-level filtering.
- **MITM Attacks** — Example: Public Wi-Fi session hijacking in cafes. Defense: TLS/HTTPS, certificate pinning, VPN.
- **SQL Injection** — Example: *2019 Fortnite vulnerability* exposed 200M+ user accounts. Defense: Parameterized queries, WAF, input validation.

**3. Social Engineering Threats**

- **Phishing** — Example: *2016 John Podesta email leak* that influenced the U.S. election. Defense: MFA, user training, anti-phishing gateways.
- **Pretexting / Vishing** — Example: *Twitter Bitcoin Scam (2020)* used vishing to compromise high-profile accounts. Defense: Strict verification protocols for high-value accounts.

**4. Insider Threats**

- Example: *Edward Snowden (2013)* exfiltrated classified NSA documents. Defense: Principle of least privilege, DLP tools, behavioral analytics, exit interviews.

**5. Advanced Persistent Threats (APTs)**

- Example: *Stuxnet (2010)* — A U.S.–Israeli joint operation that destroyed Iranian uranium centrifuges. Defense: Zero Trust Architecture, network segmentation, threat hunting.

**Marks Allocation (7 marks):**
- Five major categories identified correctly (5 × 1 = 5 marks).
- One real-world example for each (5 × 0.4 = 2 marks — awarded collectively).

---

#### **Part (b) — Design a comprehensive defense strategy mapping each threat category to layered countermeasures. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A robust defense strategy follows the **Defense-in-Depth (DiD)** principle with multiple overlapping controls:

**Layer 1 — Perimeter Defense**
- Deploy **Next-Generation Firewalls (NGFW)** with deep packet inspection.
- Implement **Intrusion Detection/Prevention Systems (IDS/IPS)**.
- Use **Anti-DDoS scrubbing** services (e.g., Cloudflare, Akamai).
- Deploy **DMARC, DKIM, SPF** to combat email phishing.

**Layer 2 — Network Defense**
- **VLAN segmentation** to isolate sensitive systems.
- **Network Access Control (NAC)** to enforce device authentication.
- Use **VPN with strong encryption (AES-256)** for remote access.
- Apply **802.1X** for port-based authentication.

**Layer 3 — Host Defense**
- Install and update **Endpoint Detection & Response (EDR)** tools.
- Enable **Application Whitelisting** to block unauthorized executables.
- Enforce **Disk Encryption (BitLocker, FileVault)**.
- Apply **OS hardening** baselines (CIS Benchmarks).

**Layer 4 — Application Defense**
- Use **Web Application Firewalls (WAF)** against SQLi/XSS.
- Enforce **Input Validation** in all user-facing fields.
- Mandate **Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA)** for all critical systems.
- Conduct **Secure SDLC** with code reviews and SAST/DAST scans.

**Layer 5 — Data Defense**
- Implement **Encryption at Rest and in Transit** (AES-256, TLS 1.3).
- Use **Data Loss Prevention (DLP)** tools to detect exfiltration.
- Maintain **air-gapped, immutable backups** following the 3-2-1 rule.
- Apply **Tokenization** for sensitive PII data.

**Layer 6 — Human/Process Defense**
- Conduct **quarterly security awareness training**.
- Run **phishing simulation campaigns**.
- Establish **Incident Response (IR) playbooks** aligned with NIST SP 800-61.
- Adopt **Zero Trust Architecture (ZTA)** — verify explicitly, least privilege, assume breach.

**Governance & Compliance Layer**
- Perform annual **risk assessments** using ISO 27005 / NIST RMF.
- Conduct **penetration testing** and **red team exercises**.
- Maintain a **Security Operations Center (SOC)** with 24/7 monitoring.
- Align with **India's DPDP Act 2023, IT Act 2000, CERT-In directives 2022**.

**Marks Allocation (7 marks):**
- Six defense layers identified (6 × 0.5 = 3 marks).
- Two specific controls per layer explained (6 × 0.5 = 3 marks).
- Governance/compliance framework reference (1 mark).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**"Risk in an Information System is a function of threats, vulnerabilities, and impact. As a security manager of a fintech startup, perform a quantitative risk assessment for a potential data breach and propose a justified set of controls."**

#### **Part (a) — Perform a quantitative risk assessment for a data breach scenario. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Asset Identification**

The fintech startup's primary assets are:
- Customer PII database: Value = **₹2,00,00,000 (2 Crore)**.
- Transaction processing system: Value = **₹1,50,00,000 (1.5 Crore)**.
- Source code repository: Value = **₹75,00,000 (75 Lakh)**.

**Step 2 — Threat Identification**

Primary deliberate threats:
- SQL injection by external attacker.
- Phishing targeting finance staff.
- Insider threat from a disgruntled DBA.

**Step 3 — Vulnerability Assessment**

A penetration test reveals:
- Web application lacks input validation (vulnerability to SQLi).
- No MFA on internal admin tools (vulnerability to phishing).
- Excessive DB admin privileges across 12 accounts (vulnerability to insider threat).

**Step 4 — Impact Estimation (Exposure Factor)**

| Asset | Threat | EF |
|---|---|---|
| PII Database | SQLi data exfiltration | 0.80 |
| PII Database | Insider theft | 0.50 |
| Transaction System | Phishing-induced fraud | 0.40 |

**Step 5 — Frequency Estimation (ARO)**

| Threat | ARO |
|---|---|
| SQLi | 1 per year |
| Phishing | 4 per year |
| Insider | 0.5 per year |

**Step 6 — SLE and ALE Calculations**

For **SQLi on PII Database**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
SLE_{SQLi} &= AV \times EF = \text{₹}2{,}00{,}00{,}000 \times 0.80 = \text{₹}1{,}60{,}00{,}000 \\
ALE_{SQLi} &= SLE \times ARO = \text{₹}1{,}60{,}00{,}000 \times 1 = \text{₹}1{,}60{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

For **Phishing on Transaction System**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
SLE_{Phish} &= \text{₹}1{,}50{,}00{,}000 \times 0.40 = \text{₹}60{,}00{,}000 \\
ALE_{Phish} &= \text{₹}60{,}00{,}000 \times 4 = \text{₹}2{,}40{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

For **Insider on PII Database**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
SLE_{Insider} &= \text{₹}2{,}00{,}00{,}000 \times 0.50 = \text{₹}1{,}00{,}00{,}000 \\
ALE_{Insider} &= \text{₹}1{,}00{,}00{,}000 \times 0.5 = \text{₹}50{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Total Annual Loss Exposure**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Total\ ALE &= \text{₹}1{,}60{,}00{,}000 + \text{₹}2{,}40{,}00{,}000 + \text{₹}50{,}00{,}000 \\
&= \text{₹}4{,}50{,}00{,}000\ (4.5\ \text{Crore per year})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Marks Allocation (7 marks):**
- Asset identification (1 mark).
- Threat and vulnerability mapping (1.5 marks).
- Exposure Factor estimation (1 mark).
- ARO estimation (0.5 mark).
- SLE and ALE calculation for at least two threats (2.5 marks).
- Total ALE aggregation (0.5 mark).

---

#### **Part (b) — Propose a justified set of security controls. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A control is **economically justified** when its annualized cost is **less than the reduction in ALE** it provides.

**Proposed Control Set:**

| Control | Target Threat | Annual Cost | Estimated ALE Reduction | Justified? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **WAF + Input Validation Refactor** | SQLi | ₹40,00,000 | ₹1,40,00,000 | ✅ Yes |
| **MFA (FIDO2 Hardware Keys)** | Phishing | ₹15,00,000 | ₹2,00,00,000 | ✅ Yes |
| **DLP + Privileged Access Mgmt (PAM)** | Insider | ₹30,00,000 | ₹40,00,000 | ✅ Yes |
| **SIEM + 24/7 SOC** | All threats | ₹50,00,000 | ₹60,00,000 | ✅ Yes |
| **Annual Pen Test + Red Team** | All threats | ₹20,00,000 | ₹40,00,000 | ✅ Yes |

**Total Annual Security Investment:**

$$
Total\ Cost = 40 + 15 + 30 + 50 + 20 = \text{₹}1{,}55{,}00{,}000\ (1.55\ \text{Crore})
$$

**Total ALE Reduction:**

$$
Total\ Reduction = 1.40 + 2.00 + 0.40 + 0.60 + 0.40 = \text{₹}4{,}80{,}00{,}000\ (4.80\ \text{Crore})
$$

**Return on Security Investment (ROSI):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
ROSI &= \frac{ALE\ Reduction - Control\ Cost}{Control\ Cost} \\
&= \frac{\text{₹}4{,}80{,}00{,}000 - \text{₹}1{,}55{,}00{,}000}{\text{₹}1{,}55{,}00{,}000} \\
&= \frac{\text{₹}3{,}25{,}00{,}000}{\text{₹}1{,}55{,}00{,}000} \\
&\approx 2.10\ (or\ 210\%)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** Every ₹1 invested in security returns approximately ₹2.10 in avoided losses — a strong business case for the board.

**Additional Justifications (qualitative):**
- **Regulatory Compliance**: Aligns with RBI Cyber Security Framework, SEBI guidelines, and DPDP Act 2023.
- **Reputation Protection**: A breach could result in loss of customer trust worth multiples of the direct financial loss.
- **Customer Trust & Retention**: Demonstrating a SOC 2 / ISO 27001 posture wins enterprise customers.

**Marks Allocation (7 marks):**
- Five controls proposed with cost and benefit (5 × 1 = 5 marks).
- ROSI calculation correct (1.5 marks).
- Qualitative justifications (0.5 mark).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in Deliberate Threats Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Threat with Vulnerability**: Students often write *"Weak passwords are a threat"* — this is **wrong**. A weak password is a *vulnerability*; the *threat* is the attacker exploiting it. Examiners deduct 1 mark per such confusion.
>
> 2. **Mislocating CIA Triad**: DDoS attacks violate *Availability*, not *Integrity*. SQL Injection violates *Confidentiality* AND *Integrity*, not just one. Always state the **primary** violation first, then secondary.
>
> 3. **Skipping the Example**: KTU examiners award 1–2 marks specifically for real-world examples. Writing "virus infects files" without naming *Melissa* or *Conficker* will cost you.
>
> 4. **Forgetting the Countermeasure Mapping**: When asked to discuss threats, students often list the attack and stop. KTU rubric requires **Threat → Countermeasure** linkage. Always close the loop.
>
> 5. **ALE Calculation Errors**: Mixing up SLE and ALE formulas is the #1 reason students lose marks in risk-assessment questions. Remember: **ALE = SLE × ARO**, and **SLE = AV × EF**. Write both formulas before substituting.
>
> 6. **Outdated Terminology**: Do not write "computer worm spreads via email" without clarifying that modern worms use **SMB, RPC, or zero-day exploits** as primary vectors. Examiners value updated, syllabus-aligned vocabulary.
>
> 7. **No Diagrams in 14-Mark Answers**: A well-labeled CIA Triad, attack lifecycle, or defense-in-depth architecture diagram in a 14-mark answer can earn an **extra 1–2 marks** as presentation reward.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Deliberate Threats to IS (KTU 2024)**

### 🔑 Must-Know Definitions
- Deliberate Threat = Intentional, human-driven attack on CIA.
- Threat Actor = Person/group responsible for attack.
- Vulnerability = Weakness exploited by threat.
- CIA Triad = Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability.

### 🦠 Malware Family (Know Each with Example + Countermeasure)
- **Virus** — Melissa; needs host file + user action. (Counter: AV + disable macros)
- **Worm** — Conficker; self-propagates over network. (Counter: Patch + segmentation)
- **Trojan** — Emotet; disguised as legitimate software. (Counter: Whitelisting + user training)
- **Ransomware** — WannaCry; encrypts data, demands ransom. (Counter: Backups + patching)
- **Spyware** — Pegasus; covertly monitors users. (Counter: EDR + MDM)
- **Rootkit** — Sony BMG; gains privileged covert access. (Counter: Secure Boot + TPM)
- **Botnet** — Mirai; network of compromised zombies. (Counter: ISP filtering + IoT hygiene)
- **Logic Bomb** — Insider-planted; triggers on condition. (Counter: Code review + audits)

### 🎭 Social Engineering (Map Each to Countermeasure)
- Phishing / Spear Phishing / Whaling → MFA + email gateway.
- Vishing / Smishing → Awareness + callback verification.
- Pretexting / Baiting / Tailgating → Identity verification + mantraps.

### 💣 DDoS Attack Types
- SYN Flood, UDP Flood, HTTP Flood, Smurf, Slowloris, DNS Amplification.
- Counter: Anycast, scrubbing, WAF, rate limiting.

### 📊 Risk Formulas (Master These)
- $SLE = AV \times EF$
- $ALE = SLE \times ARO$
- $ROSI = (ALE_{reduction} - Control_{cost}) / Control_{cost}$
- Justify a control when its cost < ALE reduction.

### 🛡️ Defense-in-Depth Layers
- Perimeter → Network → Host → Application → Data → Human/Process → Governance.

### 📜 Compliance References to Mention
- ISO/IEC 27001, 27005
- NIST SP 800-30, 800-61
- India's IT Act 2000, DPDP Act 2023
- CERT-In Directions 2022 (6-hour breach reporting)
- RBI Cyber Security Framework for banks
- GDPR, HIPAA, PCI-DSS (international)

### 🧠 Examiner Triggers for Full Marks
- Always **name** a real-world attack.
- Always **map** threats to the CIA Triad.
- Always **close the loop** with a countermeasure.
- Always **draw a diagram** in 14-mark answers.
- Always **show formulas** before substituting values in risk calculations.

> **Final Note for KTU 2024:** Module 2 deliberately tests *threat identification + classification + countermeasure mapping*. Memorize the malware table, the social engineering tree, and the ALE/ROSI formulas — these three clusters account for **70%+ of marks** in this module.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: What Organizations Are Doing to Protect Information Resources
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT523 - INFORMATION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** What Organizations Are Doing to Protect Information Resources

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of KTU 2024 Scheme (PEADT523 - Information Systems), **Information Resource Protection (IRP)** refers to the systematic, organization-wide set of policies, procedures, technical controls, physical safeguards, and human-resource practices that an enterprise deploys to ensure the **Confidentiality, Integrity, and Availability (CIA Triad)** of its data assets, information systems, and supporting infrastructure against internal and external threats.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Organizations treat information as a *strategic resource* (alongside people, capital, and raw materials). Protecting it is therefore a **board-level governance concern**, not merely an IT department responsibility.

The discipline is formally governed by international standards such as **ISO/IEC 27001**, the **NIST Cybersecurity Framework (CSF)**, and **COBIT 2019**, which provide structured approaches to identify, assess, mitigate, and monitor information-security risks.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Digital Bank Vault"

Imagine an organization as a **bank that deals in information currency** instead of money.

| Bank Component | Organizational Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Vault Door | Firewall / Access Control |
| Security Guards | Authentication & Authorization Systems |
| CCTV Cameras | Intrusion Detection Systems (IDS) |
| Insurance Policy | Risk Management & Business Continuity |
| Vault Construction | Network Segmentation & Encryption |
| Staff Training Drills | Security Awareness Programs |
| Audit Inspections | Compliance & Internal Audits |

Just as a bank cannot rely on a single locked door to protect millions in cash, an organization cannot rely on a single firewall to protect its information assets. It needs **layered defense** — what security professionals call **Defense in Depth**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Term — Defense in Depth:**
> A security strategy that employs multiple overlapping layers of controls (physical, administrative/managerial, and technical/logical) so that the failure of one layer does not result in the compromise of the entire system.

## 1.3 The CIA Triad — The Foundational Mandate

Every protective measure an organization implements maps back to one of these three pillars:

1. **Confidentiality** — Ensuring information is accessible *only* to authorized parties (e.g., encryption, access control lists).
2. **Integrity** — Ensuring information is *accurate and unaltered* during storage, processing, or transit (e.g., hashing, digital signatures).
3. **Availability** — Ensuring information and systems are *accessible when needed* by legitimate users (e.g., redundancy, backups, DDoS mitigation).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Compliance Anchor:** Regulatory frameworks like the **IT Act 2000 (India)**, **GDPR (EU)**, and **HIPAA (USA)** all codify the CIA Triad into legally enforceable obligations.

## 1.4 The Three Categories of Protective Controls

> [!NOTE]
> **Mandatory Classification — KTU Board-Exam Favorite:**

- **Physical Controls:** Locks, biometric entry, CCTV, security guards, fire suppression.
- **Administrative (Managerial) Controls:** Security policies, procedures, background checks, training, incident-response plans.
- **Technical (Logical) Controls:** Firewalls, encryption, antivirus, intrusion detection/prevention systems (IDS/IPS), multi-factor authentication (MFA).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five-Domain Framework Organizations Use

Modern organizations structure their information-protection activities around **five interlocking domains**. A KTU answer that lists all five earns full structural marks.

### Domain 1: Security Governance & Risk Management
- Establishes the **Chief Information Security Officer (CISO)** role.
- Defines the organization's risk appetite.
- Aligns security spending with business objectives.
- Documents the **Information Security Policy (ISP)** as the master document.

### Domain 2: Access Control & Identity Management
- Enforces *least privilege* — users get only the access necessary to perform their job.
- Implements the **AAA Model**: **Authentication, Authorization, Accounting**.
- Deploys Role-Based Access Control (**RBAC**) or Attribute-Based Access Control (**ABAC**).

### Domain 3: Security Operations & Technology Infrastructure
- Operates the **Security Operations Center (SOC)** for 24/7 monitoring.
- Deploys firewalls, IDS/IPS, SIEM (Security Information & Event Management) platforms.
- Conducts regular **vulnerability assessments** and **penetration testing**.

### Domain 4: Incident Response & Business Continuity
- Maintains an **Incident Response Plan (IRP)** with phases: *Preparation → Identification → Containment → Eradication → Recovery → Lessons Learned*.
- Implements **Business Continuity Planning (BCP)** and **Disaster Recovery (DR)**.
- Maintains offline, geographically separated **3-2-1 backup rule** copies.

### Domain 5: Compliance, Training & Awareness
- Conducts mandatory annual security awareness training.
- Performs internal/external audits.
- Ensures adherence to **GDPR, PCI-DSS, ISO 27001, NIST CSF**.

## 2.2 The Risk Management Equation

The single quantitative relationship that drives every protection decision is the **Annual Loss Expectancy (ALE)** calculation:

$$
\text{ALE} = \text{SLE} \times \text{ARO}
$$

Where:

- $\text{SLE}$ = Single Loss Expectancy (financial loss from *one* occurrence) = Asset Value $\times$ Exposure Factor.
- $\text{ARO}$ = Annualized Rate of Occurrence (estimated number of times the threat will occur per year).
- $\text{ALE}$ = Annual Loss Expectancy (expected yearly loss to the organization).

> [!NOTE]
> **Decision Rule:** An organization should invest in a control only if the *cost of the control* is **less than** the *reduction in ALE* it provides. This is called the **Cost-Benefit Analysis (CBA)** in security spending.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet — Frameworks & Metrics

| Framework / Metric | Full Name | Primary Use | Key Stage in KTU 2024 |
|---|---|---|---|
| **CIA Triad** | Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability | Foundation of all security goals | Module 2 Core |
| **AAA Model** | Authentication, Authorization, Accounting | Access control design | Module 2 Core |
| **ALE** | Annual Loss Expectancy | Quantify risk in monetary terms | Module 2 Calculations |
| **SLE** | Single Loss Expectancy | Loss from one incident | Module 2 Calculations |
| **ARO** | Annualized Rate of Occurrence | Frequency of threat | Module 2 Calculations |
| **NIST CSF** | NIST Cybersecurity Framework | Identify-Protect-Detect-Respond-Recover | Module 2 Governance |
| **ISO 27001** | International Standard for ISMS | Certifiable ISMS framework | Module 2 Governance |
| **SOC** | Security Operations Center | 24/7 monitoring | Module 2 Operations |
| **SIEM** | Security Info & Event Management | Log correlation, threat detection | Module 2 Operations |
| **BCP/DRP** | Business Continuity / Disaster Recovery Plan | Continuity assurance | Module 2 Continuity |
| **MFA** | Multi-Factor Authentication | Strong authentication | Module 2 Access |
| **RBAC** | Role-Based Access Control | Scalable authorization | Module 2 Access |
| **3-2-1 Rule** | 3 copies, 2 media types, 1 offsite | Backup strategy | Module 2 Continuity |
| **IDS/IPS** | Intrusion Detection / Prevention System | Network threat response | Module 2 Operations |
| **COBIT** | Control Objectives for Info & Related Tech | IT governance | Module 2 Governance |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Valuation Tip:** Always cite the framework by its *full name first*, then the acronym in parentheses. Examiners award method marks for correct expansion.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Banking/FinTech:** Uses encryption + tokenization to protect card data under **PCI-DSS**.
- **Healthcare:** Deploys HIPAA-compliant access logs to protect Electronic Health Records (EHR).
- **Manufacturing (Industry 4.0):** Segregates **OT (Operational Technology)** networks from **IT** networks to prevent ransomware spread to factory floors.
- **Government/Defence:** Uses air-gapped networks, classified data labelling, and zero-trust architectures.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Methodology & Worked Risk Computations

Because this is a management-focused topic, the "derivations" here are **structured analytical methodologies** and **worked numerical risk-calculations** that KTU commonly tests.

## 3.1 Methodology: The Six-Phase Risk Management Lifecycle

### Step 1 — Asset Identification
Catalogue every information asset:
- Hardware (servers, laptops, IoT devices)
- Software (applications, OS)
- Data (customer records, intellectual property)
- Personnel (employees, contractors)
- Facilities (datacenters, offices)

### Step 2 — Threat Identification
Identify potential threats: malware, insider threats, natural disasters, supply-chain attacks, phishing, ransomware.

### Step 3 — Vulnerability Assessment
Find weaknesses: unpatched software, weak passwords, misconfigured firewalls, missing encryption.

### Step 4 — Impact & Likelihood Analysis
Assign values:
- $\text{Exposure Factor (EF)}$: Percentage of asset value lost in one incident.
- $\text{ARO}$: How many times per year the threat is expected to occur.

### Step 5 — Risk Calculation
Apply the **ALE = SLE × ARO** formula to obtain monetary exposure.

### Step 6 — Control Selection & Cost-Benefit Justification
Select controls where Control Cost $<$ ALE Reduction. Re-assess residual risk.

> [!NOTE]
> **Residual Risk** is the risk that remains *after* controls are applied. It must be formally accepted by management — this is called the **Risk Acceptance** step.

## 3.2 Worked Calculation 1 — Basic ALE Computation

**Problem Statement:**
A company has a customer database valued at ₹50,00,000. A risk assessment estimates that a breach would expose 40% of the data, and such breaches are expected to occur 2 times per year. Find the **SLE, ARO, and ALE**.

### Step-by-Step Solution

$$
\text{Asset Value} = ₹\ 50{,}00{,}000
$$

$$
\text{Exposure Factor (EF)} = 40\% = 0.40
$$

$$
\text{SLE} = \text{Asset Value} \times \text{EF}
$$

$$
\text{SLE} = ₹\ 50{,}00{,}000 \times 0.40 = ₹\ 20{,}00{,}000
$$

$$
\text{ARO} = 2\ \text{incidents per year}
$$

$$
\text{ALE} = \text{SLE} \times \text{ARO}
$$

$$
\text{ALE} = ₹\ 20{,}00{,}000 \times 2 = ₹\ 40{,}00{,}000\ \text{per year}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** The organization stands to lose approximately ₹40 lakh *annually* from this single risk. Any control costing less than ₹40 lakh/year that reduces this risk is economically justified.

## 3.3 Worked Calculation 2 — Cost-Benefit Analysis of a Control

**Problem Statement:**
The same company is evaluating an encryption control costing ₹12,00,000 per year that will reduce the ARO from 2 to 0.5 incidents/year. Is the control justified?

### Step-by-Step Solution

$$
\text{ALE}_{\text{before}} = ₹\ 20{,}00{,}000 \times 2 = ₹\ 40{,}00{,}000
$$

$$
\text{ALE}_{\text{after}} = ₹\ 20{,}00{,}000 \times 0.5 = ₹\ 10{,}00{,}000
$$

$$
\text{ALE Reduction} = ₹\ 40{,}00{,}000 - ₹\ 10{,}00{,}000 = ₹\ 30{,}00{,}000
$$

$$
\text{Net Benefit} = \text{ALE Reduction} - \text{Control Cost}
$$

$$
\text{Net Benefit} = ₹\ 30{,}00{,}000 - ₹\ 12{,}00{,}000 = ₹\ 18{,}00{,}000\ \text{per year}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Decision:** Since Net Benefit is **positive (₹18 lakh/year)**, the encryption control is **economically justified** and should be implemented.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Threats to Controls

| Threat | Likelihood | Impact | Primary Control | Control Type |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Phishing Attack | High | Medium | Email filtering + User training | Technical + Administrative |
| Ransomware | Medium | High | Offline backups + EDR + Patch mgmt | Technical + Administrative |
| Insider Data Theft | Medium | High | RBAC + DLP + Audit logs | Technical + Administrative |
| DDoS Attack | Low | High | CDN + Rate limiting + ISP scrubbing | Technical |
| Physical Theft | Low | Medium | CCTV + Biometric + Asset tagging | Physical |
| Natural Disaster | Very Low | Very High | Offsite backups + DR site | Administrative + Technical |

## 3.5 Analytical Insight — The Three Tiers of Defense

Organizations apply protection in three concentric tiers:

- **Tier 1 — Perimeter Defense:** Firewalls, DMZ, anti-malware gateways.
- **Tier 2 — Internal Network Defense:** Network segmentation, IDS/IPS, NAC (Network Access Control).
- **Tier 3 — Host & Data Defense:** Endpoint Detection & Response (EDR), encryption, file integrity monitoring.

> [!NOTE]
> **Zero Trust Extension:** Modern organizations increasingly adopt the **Zero Trust Architecture (ZTA)** model, which assumes *no implicit trust* and verifies *every access request* regardless of whether it originates inside or outside the network perimeter.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Schematic — Organizational Information Protection Framework

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Reading Order:** Start at node `topA` (Governance), flow outward to the three Control Families, then converge into the central Monitoring Hub `coreM`, and finally terminate at the Outcome `goalZ`.

```mermaid
graph TD
    topA[Information Security Governance Board] --> stratB[Define ISP and Risk Appetite]
    stratB --> polC[Master Information Security Policy]

    polC --> fam1[Administrative Controls Family]
    polC --> fam2[Technical Controls Family]
    polC --> fam3[Physical Controls Family]

    fam1 --> adm1A[Security Awareness Training]
    fam1 --> adm1B[Background Verification]
    fam1 --> adm1C[Incident Response Plan]
    fam1 --> adm1D[BCP and DRP Documentation]

    fam2 --> tec2A[Firewall and IDS IPS]
    fam2 --> tec2B[Multi Factor Authentication]
    fam2 --> tec2C[Encryption at Rest and In Transit]
    fam2 --> tec2D[SIEM Platform]

    fam3 --> phy3A[Biometric Door Access]
    fam3 --> phy3B[CCTV and Security Patrols]
    fam3 --> phy3C[Fire Suppression and UPS]

    tec2D --> coreM[Central SOC Monitoring Hub]
    adm1C --> coreM
    phy3B --> coreM

    coreM --> aud1[Internal and External Audits]
    coreM --> aud2[Compliance Reporting GDPR ISO27001]
    coreM --> aud3[Continuous Vulnerability Scanning]

    aud1 --> goalZ[Protected Information Resources CIA Maintained]
    aud2 --> goalZ
    aud3 --> goalZ
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Schematic — Incident Response Lifecycle (NIST SP 800-61)

```mermaid
graph LR
    ph1[Phase 1 Preparation] --> ph2[Phase 2 Detection and Analysis]
    ph2 --> ph3[Phase 3 Containment]
    ph3 --> ph4[Phase 4 Eradication]
    ph4 --> ph5[Phase 5 Recovery]
    ph5 --> ph6[Phase 6 Post Incident Lessons Learned]
    ph6 -.feedback loop.-> ph1
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Defense-in-Depth Layers

```mermaid
graph TB
    L1[Layer 1 Physical Security Gates CCTV Locks] --> L2[Layer 2 Perimeter Network Firewall DMZ IDS]
    L2 --> L3[Layer 3 Internal Network Segmentation NAC VLANs]
    L3 --> L4[Layer 4 Host Security EDR Antivirus Patch Management]
    L4 --> L5[Layer 5 Application Security Secure Coding WAF Input Validation]
    L5 --> L6[Layer 6 Data Security Encryption Hashing Tokenization DLP]
    L6 --> L7[Layer 7 Monitoring and Audit SIEM SOC Continuous Logging]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway from Diagrams:** Every protection layer *overlaps* with the next. The failure of Layer 3 does not lead to total compromise because Layer 4, 5, 6, and 7 still exist as independent barriers. This redundancy is the essence of **Defense in Depth**.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. Writing *only* "firewall" or *only* "antivirus" when asked how organizations protect information — KTU expects a **layered, multi-control answer** spanning people, process, and technology.
> 2. Confusing **ALE** (annual) with **SLE** (single incident) in numerical problems — examiners deduct 2 marks per wrong substitution.
> 3. Citing acronyms without expansion (e.g., writing "SOC" without spelling out "Security Operations Center") — costs the *method mark*.
> 4. Treating the CIA Triad as just three words without defining each — KTU 2024 Scheme rubric allocates 1 mark per correct definition.
> 5. Forgetting to state the **residual risk acceptance** step — a frequent 2-mark deduction in governance questions.

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]
**List and briefly explain the three components of the CIA Triad that organizations strive to protect.**

**Model Answer:**

The CIA Triad is the foundational model of information security.

1. **Confidentiality:** Ensures that information is disclosed *only* to authorized individuals, systems, or processes. Example controls include encryption, access control lists, and MFA. (1 mark)
2. **Integrity:** Ensures that information remains *accurate, complete, and unaltered* during storage, processing, or transmission. Example controls include hashing (e.g., SHA-256), digital signatures, and version control. (1 mark)
3. **Availability:** Ensures that information and information systems are *accessible to authorized users* whenever required. Example controls include redundant servers, UPS systems, DDoS mitigation, and the **3-2-1 backup rule**. (1 mark)

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]
**What is Defense in Depth? Why do organizations adopt it?**

**Model Answer:**

**Defense in Depth** is a security strategy in which an organization deploys *multiple, overlapping layers of controls* — physical, administrative, and technical — so that the failure of any single control does not lead to total compromise of information assets. (2 marks)

**Why organizations adopt it:**
- No single control is 100% effective; layered controls compensate for individual weaknesses.
- It addresses diverse threat types (physical theft, cyber attacks, insider misuse) simultaneously.
- It satisfies regulatory and audit requirements (e.g., ISO 27001 control families). (1 mark)

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model — 14 Marks]

**(a)** Explain in detail the **five functional domains** through which organizations protect their information resources. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An organization has identified a server asset worth ₹80,00,000. A risk assessment shows an Exposure Factor of 25% and an Annualized Rate of Occurrence of 3. The organization is evaluating a firewall control costing ₹10,00,000/year that will reduce the ARO to 1. Calculate the SLE, the ALE before and after the control, and determine whether the control should be implemented. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

**(a) The Five Functional Domains — 7 Marks**

Organizations structure their information-protection activities into the following five domains:

**1. Security Governance and Risk Management (1.5 marks)**
Headed by the **CISO** (Chief Information Security Officer), this domain establishes the organization's security strategy, defines its **risk appetite**, and produces the master **Information Security Policy (ISP)**. It aligns security investments with business objectives and ensures board-level oversight.

**2. Access Control and Identity Management (1.5 marks)**
Implements the **AAA Model — Authentication, Authorization, and Accounting**. Uses **Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA)**, **Role-Based Access Control (RBAC)**, and the *principle of least privilege* so users receive only the minimum access required to perform their duties.

**3. Security Operations and Technology Infrastructure (1.5 marks)**
Operates a **Security Operations Center (SOC)** for 24/7 monitoring. Deploys firewalls, **IDS/IPS**, **SIEM** platforms, and conducts regular **vulnerability assessments** and **penetration testing**.

**4. Incident Response and Business Continuity (1.5 marks)**
Maintains a documented **Incident Response Plan (IRP)** following the NIST six-phase model: *Preparation → Detection → Containment → Eradication → Recovery → Lessons Learned*. Also maintains **BCP/DRP** and follows the **3-2-1 backup rule**.

**5. Compliance, Training and Awareness (1 mark)**
Conducts mandatory annual security awareness training, performs internal/external audits, and ensures adherence to standards like **ISO 27001, GDPR, PCI-DSS, and the NIST Cybersecurity Framework**.

**(b) Numerical Solution — 7 Marks**

$$
\text{Asset Value} = ₹\ 80{,}00{,}000
$$

$$
\text{Exposure Factor (EF)} = 25\% = 0.25
$$

$$
\text{SLE} = \text{Asset Value} \times \text{EF}
$$

$$
\text{SLE} = ₹\ 80{,}00{,}000 \times 0.25 = ₹\ 20{,}00{,}000 \quad \text{(2 marks)}
$$

$$
\text{ARO}_{\text{before}} = 3
$$

$$
\text{ARO}_{\text{after}} = 1
$$

$$
\text{ALE}_{\text{before}} = \text{SLE} \times \text{ARO}_{\text{before}}
$$

$$
\text{ALE}_{\text{before}} = ₹\ 20{,}00{,}000 \times 3 = ₹\ 60{,}00{,}000 \quad \text{(2 marks)}
$$

$$
\text{ALE}_{\text{after}} = \text{SLE} \times \text{ARO}_{\text{after}}
$$

$$
\text{ALE}_{\text{after}} = ₹\ 20{,}00{,}000 \times 1 = ₹\ 20{,}00{,}000 \quad \text{(1.5 marks)}
$$

$$
\text{ALE Reduction} = ₹\ 60{,}00{,}000 - ₹\ 20{,}00{,}000 = ₹\ 40{,}00{,}000
$$

$$
\text{Net Benefit} = ₹\ 40{,}00{,}000 - ₹\ 10{,}00{,}000 = ₹\ 30{,}00{,}000 \quad \text{(1 mark)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** Since Net Benefit is **positive (₹30 lakh/year)**, the firewall control is **economically justified** and the organization should implement it. Any *residual risk* (₹20 lakh/year) must be formally accepted by management.

---

### Question B. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model — 14 Marks]

**(a)** Describe the **three categories of security controls** (Physical, Administrative, Technical) with two examples in each category. Explain how together they form a Defense-in-Depth strategy. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **NIST six-phase Incident Response Lifecycle** and state one activity performed in each phase. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

**(a) Three Categories of Security Controls — 7 Marks**

**1. Physical Controls (2 marks):** These protect the physical premises and hardware.
Examples: Biometric door access for server rooms; CCTV surveillance with 24×7 monitoring.
Other examples: Cable locks, fire suppression systems, security guard patrols.

**2. Administrative (Managerial) Controls (2 marks):** These are policies, procedures, and people-based safeguards.
Examples: Mandatory annual security awareness training; background verification before employment.
Other examples: Incident Response Plan (IRP), Business Continuity Plan (BCP), acceptable-use policies.

**3. Technical (Logical) Controls (2 marks):** These are software and hardware-based safeguards embedded in systems.
Examples: Firewall rules blocking unauthorized traffic; Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) for system logins.
Other examples: Encryption (AES-256), Intrusion Detection Systems (IDS), antivirus/EDR.

**Forming Defense-in-Depth (1 mark):**
When an organization deploys all three categories *in combination*, an attacker must defeat multiple independent layers. For example, a stolen laptop (failure of physical control) is still protected by disk encryption and MFA (technical controls), and the incident is escalated per the IRP (administrative control). The redundancy among categories is the essence of **Defense in Depth**.

**(b) NIST Six-Phase Incident Response Lifecycle — 7 Marks**

**1. Preparation (1 mark):** Develop and document the IRP, train the response team, deploy monitoring tools, and establish communication channels *before* any incident occurs.

**2. Detection and Analysis (1.5 marks):** Identify potential security incidents through SIEM alerts, user reports, or anomaly detection. Determine the *scope, type, and severity* of the incident.

**3. Containment (1.5 marks):** Limit the impact of the incident — isolate affected systems, block malicious IPs, disconnect infected hosts from the network (short-term and long-term containment).

**4. Eradication (1 mark):** Remove the threat completely — delete malware, close exploited vulnerabilities, reset compromised credentials, and patch affected systems.

**5. Recovery (1 mark):** Restore systems to normal operation from clean backups, validate system integrity, and monitor for signs of residual compromise.

**6. Post-Incident / Lessons Learned (1 mark):** Conduct a post-mortem meeting, document findings, update the IRP, and feed improvements back into the *Preparation* phase — completing the feedback loop.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Tip:** Phases 1–6 form a *cyclical feedback loop*. Many students list them linearly; mentioning the feedback loop from Phase 6 to Phase 1 earns the final method mark.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **CIA Triad** = Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability — the foundational mandate of all protection.
- **Defense in Depth** = layered physical + administrative + technical controls.
- **AAA Model** = Authentication, Authorization, Accounting — governs access control.
- **Five Functional Domains** = Governance, Access Control, Operations, Incident Response, Compliance.
- **Three Control Categories** = Physical, Administrative, Technical.
- **ALE = SLE × ARO** — the single most-tested formula; always expand acronyms first.
- **Control is justified if:** Control Cost $<$ ALE Reduction (positive net benefit).
- **Residual Risk** must be formally accepted by management — never ignored.
- **Zero Trust Architecture (ZTA)** = no implicit trust, verify every request.
- **3-2-1 Backup Rule** = 3 copies, 2 different media, 1 stored offsite.
- **NIST IR Lifecycle** = 6 phases: Preparation → Detection → Containment → Eradication → Recovery → Lessons Learned (with feedback loop).
- **Key Standards:** ISO 27001, NIST CSF, COBIT 2019, GDPR, PCI-DSS, IT Act 2000.
- **Governance Anchor:** Information security is a *board-level* concern, not merely an IT issue.
- **Always cite frameworks by full name** before using the acronym in KTU answers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Information Security Controls
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT523 - INFORMATION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Information Security Controls

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Information Security Controls** are the safeguards, countermeasures, and protective measures (administrative, technical/logical, and physical) deliberately selected, designed, and implemented to preserve the **CIA triad** — *Confidentiality, Integrity, and Availability* — of an organization's information assets against identified threats, vulnerabilities, and risks, while ensuring compliance with applicable policies, standards, regulations, and contractual obligations.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology (mapped to NIST SP 800-53 and ISO/IEC 27001:2022), a *control* is **anything that modifies or reduces risk**. Controls are the operational response to the risk-management lifecycle: they are selected after a risk assessment identifies threats and vulnerabilities, and they are continuously monitored and re-evaluated.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Fortified Bank Vault

Imagine a bank protecting its gold reserves:

- **The Vault Door + Alarm + Guards + Audit Logs** = *Layered Security Controls*
- **Hiring trustworthy staff and screening them** = *Administrative Controls*
- **The combination lock on the vault door** = *Technical/Logical Controls*
- **The thick concrete walls, fences, and locked building** = *Physical Controls*
- **Daily reconciliation of gold stock** = *Detective Controls*
- **A backup vault in another city** = *Recovery Controls*

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Examiners:** No single control can protect information alone. The strength of an information security program lies in **defense-in-depth** — overlapping layers of controls so that the failure of one layer does not compromise the entire system.

## 1.3 The CIA Triad — Foundation of Every Control

| Pillar | Definition | Threat Example | Control Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Confidentiality** | Ensuring information is accessible only to authorized entities | Eavesdropping, shoulder surfing, data leakage | Encryption, access control lists, NDAs |
| **Integrity** | Ensuring information is accurate, complete, and unaltered | Man-in-the-middle, SQL injection, unauthorized modification | Hashing, digital signatures, audit trails |
| **Availability** | Ensuring information is accessible when needed by authorized users | DDoS attacks, hardware failure, ransomware | Redundancy, backups, failover clusters |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners frequently award marks to students who explicitly map a control back to one (or more) pillars of the **CIA triad**. Always do this in your 14-mark answers.

## 1.4 Why Information Security Controls Matter — KTU Syllabus Context

- They convert **abstract risk** into **measurable, manageable actions**.
- They satisfy **legal and regulatory compliance** (e.g., IT Act 2000, GDPR, DPDP Act 2023).
- They protect the three pillars of *Trust*: customer trust, partner trust, and brand trust.
- They are the **auditable proof** that an organization is managing its information responsibly.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Primary Classification Schemes

Information security controls are classified along **four orthogonal axes**. A KTU 14-mark answer typically expects you to discuss at least two of these axes.

### Axis A — By Function (What the Control Does)

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **most-tested classification** in KTU university exams.

1. **Preventive Controls** — *Stop a security incident before it occurs.*
   Examples: Access control lists, encryption, firewalls, security awareness training, biometric authentication.
2. **Detective Controls** — *Identify that an incident has occurred or is occurring.*
   Examples: Intrusion Detection Systems (IDS), log monitoring, SIEM dashboards, CCTV, audit trails.
3. **Corrective Controls** — *Restore systems to normal after an incident.*
   Examples: Incident response plans, patching, antivirus quarantine, system restoration from backups.
4. **Deterrent Controls** — *Discourage attackers from attempting an attack.*
   Examples: Warning banners, legal notices ("Authorized access only"), visible CCTV, security guard patrols.
5. **Recovery Controls** — *Restore lost data, services, and capabilities after a disaster.*
   Examples: Off-site backups, Disaster Recovery (DR) sites, Business Continuity Plans (BCP), hot/warm/cold sites.
6. **Compensating Controls** — *Alternative controls used when a primary control cannot be implemented.*
   Examples: Using a manual sign-in register when a digital access card system fails; using one-time passwords when smart cards are unavailable.

### Axis B — By Implementation Type (How the Control Is Built)

| Type | Description | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Administrative / Managerial Controls** | Policies, procedures, guidelines, and human-resource practices | Security policy, hiring screening, separation of duties, security awareness training |
| **Technical / Logical Controls** | Hardware/software mechanisms enforcing security | Firewalls, encryption, ACLs, IDS/IPS, anti-malware |
| **Physical Controls** | Tangible mechanisms preventing unauthorized physical access | Fences, locks, CCTV, mantraps, biometric door readers, Faraday cages |

### Axis C — By Layer (NIST Cybersecurity Framework View)

> Identify → Protect → Detect → Respond → Recover

Each function in the **NIST CSF 2.0 (2024)** maps to specific control categories:
- **Identify** → Asset inventory, risk assessment, governance.
- **Protect** → Access control, awareness training, data security, protective technology.
- **Detect** → Anomalies & events, security continuous monitoring, detection processes.
- **Respond** → Response planning, communications, analysis, mitigation.
- **Recover** → Recovery planning, improvements, communications.

### Axis D — By Control Modality

- **Manual Controls** — Operated by humans (e.g., sign-in registers, manual log reviews).
- **Automated Controls** — Operated by systems (e.g., automatic patching, real-time SIEM alerts).
- **Hybrid Controls** — Combination of both (e.g., automated alerting + human investigation).

## 2.2 Defense in Depth — Layered Security Model

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Defense in Depth** is the foundational design principle: an attacker must defeat **multiple independent layers** to compromise the asset. The failure of one layer triggers the next layer's detection/prevention.

The classic layers, from outermost to innermost:

1. **Policies, Procedures & Awareness** (Administrative)
2. **Physical Security** (Perimeter, building, room)
3. **Perimeter Network Security** (Firewalls, DMZ, VPNs)
4. **Internal Network Security** (NAC, segmentation, IDS/IPS)
5. **Host Security** (OS hardening, anti-malware, patching)
6. **Application Security** (Input validation, secure coding, WAF)
7. **Data Security** (Encryption at rest, DLP, tokenization)

## 2.3 Access Control Models — The Most Tested Sub-Topic

| Model | Full Form | Who Decides Access | Best Suited For | Key Weakness |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **DAC** | Discretionary Access Control | The **data owner** | Collaborative file-sharing environments | Trojan-horse attacks; owner may grant excessive rights |
| **MAC** | Mandatory Access Control | The system / **security policy** (clearance labels) | Military, government, classified systems | Inflexible; high administrative overhead |
| **RBAC** | Role-Based Access Control | The **role definition** (e.g., HR Manager, Auditor) | Enterprises with clear job functions | Role explosion in large organizations |
| **Rule-Based** | Rule-Based Access Control | **Pre-defined rules** (e.g., firewall ACL rules) | Network devices, firewalls | Rules may conflict; ordering matters |
| **ABAC** | Attribute-Based Access Control | **Multiple attributes** (user, resource, action, environment) | Cloud, dynamic, large-scale environments | Complex attribute management; performance overhead |

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of thumb for KTU answers:** *DAC = owner decides, MAC = system decides, RBAC = role decides, ABAC = attributes decide.*

## 2.4 Cryptographic Controls — KTU Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Standard | Purpose | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Symmetric Encryption** | $C = E_K(P)$, $P = D_K(C)$ | Confidentiality | Same key K for encrypt & decrypt |
| 2 | **Asymmetric Encryption** | $C = E_{K_{pub}}(P)$, $P = D_{K_{priv}}(C)$ | Confidentiality, non-repudiation | Public key encrypts, private key decrypts |
| 3 | **Digital Signature** | $S = \text{Sign}_{K_{priv}}(H(M))$ | Integrity, authentication, non-repudiation | Sign with private, verify with public |
| 4 | **Hash Function** | $h = H(M)$ | Integrity verification | One-way, collision-resistant |
| 5 | **RSA Key Generation** | $n = p \cdot q$, $\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)$ | Foundation of asymmetric crypto | p, q = large primes |
| 6 | **HMAC** | $\text{HMAC}(K, M) = H((K \oplus opad) \Vert H((K \oplus ipad) \Vert M))$ | Message authentication with shared key | Combines hash + symmetric key |

## 2.5 Network Security Controls

| Control | Layer | Function | Type |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Firewall (Packet Filter)** | Network (L3/L4) | Filters traffic by IP/port | Preventive |
| **Stateful Inspection Firewall** | Network (L3/L4) | Tracks connection state | Preventive + Detective |
| **Application-Layer Firewall / WAF** | Application (L7) | Inspects HTTP/SQL payloads | Preventive |
| **IDS (Intrusion Detection System)** | Network/Host | Detects suspicious activity | Detective |
| **IPS (Intrusion Prevention System)** | Network/Host | Detects AND blocks | Preventive + Detective |
| **VPN (Virtual Private Network)** | Network | Encrypted tunnel over public network | Preventive (Confidentiality) |
| **DMZ** | Network | Isolated subnet for public-facing servers | Preventive |
| **NAC (Network Access Control)** | Network | Authenticates & authorizes devices before granting access | Preventive |

## 2.6 Risk Management — The Mathematical Foundation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Risk formula is **the single most-tested formula** under "Information Security Controls" in KTU exams.

$$\text{Risk} = \text{Threat} \times \text{Vulnerability} \times \text{Impact (Asset Value)}$$

Equivalently:

$$R = T \times V \times I$$

Where each component is typically scored on a qualitative scale (1–5) or quantitative scale (in monetary loss).

**Risk Treatment Options (mapped to controls):**
1. **Risk Mitigation / Reduction** → Implement controls to reduce likelihood or impact.
2. **Risk Transfer** → Buy insurance, outsource, use SLA penalties.
3. **Risk Acceptance** → Formally acknowledge and accept the risk (when cost of control > loss).
4. **Risk Avoidance** → Eliminate the activity causing the risk (e.g., discontinue a service).
5. **Risk Exploitation / Enhancement** *(opportunity-side risk)* → In some frameworks, take action to *increase* the probability of positive outcomes.

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula & Standards Cheat Sheet

| # | Item | Standard / Formula | Exam Weightage |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | CIA Triad | Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability | High |
| 2 | Risk Equation | $R = T \times V \times I$ | **Very High** |
| 3 | Control Categories | Preventive, Detective, Corrective, Deterrent, Recovery, Compensating | **Very High** |
| 4 | Control Types | Administrative, Technical, Physical | High |
| 5 | Access Control Models | DAC, MAC, RBAC, Rule-Based, ABAC | **Very High** |
| 6 | Cryptographic Functions | Hash, Symmetric, Asymmetric, Digital Signature | High |
| 7 | Defense in Depth Layers | 7 layers (Policy → Data) | Medium |
| 8 | NIST CSF | Identify-Protect-Detect-Respond-Recover | Medium |
| 9 | ISO 27001 Annex A Domains | 4 themes — Organizational, People, Physical, Technological (93 controls) | Medium |
| 10 | COBIT | Governance & management of enterprise IT | Low-Medium |

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Control

| If the Risk is... | Primary Control Function | Secondary Control Function | Rationale |
|---|---|---|---|
| Data theft from server | Preventive (encryption, ACL) | Detective (DLP, audit logs) | Stop the theft AND detect if it occurs |
| Unauthorized physical entry | Preventive (locks, biometrics) | Deterrent (signage, CCTV) | Block entry AND discourage attempts |
| Insider data exfiltration | Preventive (least privilege, segregation of duties) | Detective (UBA, SIEM) | Reduce insider ability AND detect anomalies |
| Ransomware attack | Preventive (anti-malware, patching) | Recovery (offsite backups) | Reduce likelihood AND recover if successful |
| Compliance violation | Administrative (policy, training) | Detective (compliance audits) | Inform AND verify compliance |
| DDoS attack | Detective (traffic monitoring) | Recovery (CDN, scrubbing center) | Detect attack AND maintain availability |

## 3.2 Worked-Out Numerical Problem — Quantitative Risk Assessment

> [!EXAMPLE]
> **Problem (KTU-style 14-mark adapted to 7-mark):**
> An e-commerce company has identified an asset (customer database) valued at **₹50,00,000**. The estimated annual probability of a breach due to weak passwords is **0.4 (40%)**, and the vulnerability rating (on a 1–5 scale) is **3**. The control currently in place reduces impact by 30%. Calculate:
> 1. The Annual Loss Expectancy (ALE) **before** controls.
> 2. The Residual Risk **after** controls.
> 3. Recommend whether a control costing ₹6,00,000/year is justified.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the Asset Value (AV):**

$$AV = 50{,}00{,}000 \text{ INR}$$

This is the total monetary value of the customer database.

**Step 2 — Determine the Exposure Factor (EF):**

The impact when the threat materializes. The problem states the control reduces impact by 30%, so:
- Without control: $EF_{before} = 1.0$ (100% loss of asset value).
- With control: $EF_{after} = 1.0 - 0.30 = 0.70$.

**Step 3 — Compute the Single Loss Expectancy (SLE):**

$$SLE = AV \times EF$$

$$SLE_{before} = 50{,}00{,}000 \times 1.0 = 50{,}00{,}000$$

$$SLE_{after} = 50{,}00{,}000 \times 0.70 = 35{,}00{,}000$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Annual Rate of Occurrence (ARO):**

The annual probability of the breach:

$$ARO = 0.4$$

**Step 5 — Compute the Annual Loss Expectancy (ALE):**

$$ALE = SLE \times ARO$$

$$ALE_{before} = 50{,}00{,}000 \times 0.4 = 20{,}00{,}000 \text{ INR}$$

$$ALE_{after} = 35{,}00{,}000 \times 0.4 = 14{,}00{,}000 \text{ INR}$$

**Step 6 — Compute the Annual Savings from the Control:**

$$\text{Savings} = ALE_{before} - ALE_{after}$$

$$\text{Savings} = 20{,}00{,}000 - 14{,}00{,}000 = 6{,}00{,}000 \text{ INR}$$

**Step 7 — Compute the Net Value of the Control:**

$$\text{Net Value} = \text{Savings} - \text{Cost of Control}$$

$$\text{Net Value} = 6{,}00{,}000 - 6{,}00{,}000 = 0 \text{ INR}$$

**Step 8 — Decision:**

> The control is **marginally justified** — Net Value is zero, meaning the company simply **breaks even** on the financial control. However, this calculation does not include *intangible benefits* (brand reputation, regulatory penalties avoided, customer trust). Therefore, **the control is recommended** when considering qualitative factors.

**Step 9 — Residual Risk:**

$$\text{Residual Risk} = ALE_{after} = 14{,}00{,}000 \text{ INR}$$

Or expressed as:

$$\text{Risk Reduction} = \frac{ALE_{before} - ALE_{after}}{ALE_{before}} \times 100\%$$

$$\text{Risk Reduction} = \frac{20{,}00{,}000 - 14{,}00{,}000}{20{,}00{,}000} \times 100\% = 30\%$$

**Conclusion:** The control reduces risk by exactly the impact reduction percentage (as expected, since ARO is unchanged in this simplified model).

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Logic — Selecting Access Controls in an Organization

**Step 1 — Identify information assets and owners.**
**Step 2 — Classify data by sensitivity** (Public, Internal, Confidential, Restricted).
**Step 3 — Define roles** based on job functions (RBAC) or attribute policies (ABAC).
**Step 4 — Apply least privilege** — grant minimum permissions necessary.
**Step 5 — Apply need-to-know** — even authorized users see only what they need.
**Step 6 — Implement separation of duties** — no single person should control an entire critical process.
**Step 7 — Deploy authentication mechanisms** (passwords, MFA, biometrics).
**Step 8 — Enable auditing and logging** of all access attempts.
**Step 9 — Periodically review access rights** (User Access Review / UAR).
**Step 10 — Update controls** when roles, personnel, or systems change.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Logic Check:** If your answer mentions *only* technical controls (e.g., firewalls, encryption), you lose marks. Always include **administrative + technical + physical** in a balanced way.

## 3.4 Logical Flow — Cryptographic Control Selection

```
STEP 1: What must be protected?
        ├── Confidentiality  → Encryption (symmetric or asymmetric)
        ├── Integrity        → Hashing (SHA-256) or HMAC
        ├── Authentication   → Digital certificates, passwords, MFA
        └── Non-Repudiation  → Digital signatures
```

$$ \text{Hash Function Properties} = \{ \text{Deterministic, One-way, Collision-resistant, Avalanche-effect} \} $$

- **Deterministic:** Same input $M$ always yields same hash $h$.
- **One-way:** Given $h$, computing $M$ is computationally infeasible.
- **Collision-resistant:** Finding $M_1 \neq M_2$ such that $H(M_1) = H(M_2)$ is infeasible.
- **Avalanche-effect:** A tiny change in $M$ drastically changes $h$.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define Information Security Controls. List the three control types with one example each. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

> **Information Security Controls** are the safeguards and countermeasures (administrative, technical, and physical) implemented to protect the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of information assets.

**The three control types:**

| Type | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Administrative** | Policies, procedures, training | Security Awareness Training Program |
| **Technical / Logical** | Hardware/software enforcement | Firewall, encryption, access control lists |
| **Physical** | Tangible barriers | Biometric door access, CCTV cameras |

*(3 marks — 1 mark definition + 2 marks list with examples)*

---

### Q2. Differentiate between Preventive and Detective Controls. Give one example of each. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Preventive Control | Detective Control |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Stop incident before it occurs | Identify incident during/after occurrence |
| **Timing** | Pre-incident | During or post-incident |
| **Example** | Firewall blocking unauthorized traffic | Intrusion Detection System raising an alert |

**Examples:**
- **Preventive:** Encryption of sensitive data at rest.
- **Detective:** SIEM dashboard correlating logs to detect anomalies.

*(3 marks — differentiation 2 marks + examples 1 mark)*

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks

#### (a) Explain in detail the various classifications of information security controls. Illustrate with examples. `[7 Marks]`

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Information Security Controls can be classified along four major axes: by function, by implementation type, by layer (NIST CSF), and by modality.

**Classification by Function (3 marks):**
- **Preventive** — e.g., firewall, encryption, MFA.
- **Detective** — e.g., IDS, audit logs, CCTV.
- **Corrective** — e.g., patching, system restoration.
- **Deterrent** — e.g., warning banners, security guards.
- **Recovery** — e.g., offsite backups, hot sites.
- **Compensating** — e.g., manual sign-in when the digital system fails.

**Classification by Implementation Type (2 marks):**
- **Administrative** — security policies, background checks, separation of duties.
- **Technical/Logical** — antivirus, encryption, firewalls, DLP.
- **Physical** — locks, fences, mantraps, Faraday cages.

**Conclusion (1 mark):**
A robust information security program uses controls from **all three implementation types** and across **all six function categories** to achieve defense-in-depth.

---

#### (b) Describe the access control models DAC, MAC, and RBAC with suitable examples. Compare them in a table. `[7 Marks]`

**Model Answer:**

**DAC — Discretionary Access Control (2 marks):**
- The **data owner** decides who can access the resource.
- Permissions are stored with the resource (e.g., NTFS file permissions).
- Example: A project manager grants read access to a folder for specific team members in a collaborative environment.

**MAC — Mandatory Access Control (2 marks):**
- Access decisions are made by the **system based on labels** (e.g., Top Secret, Secret, Confidential, Unclassified).
- Users are assigned clearance levels; resources are assigned sensitivity labels.
- Example: Military systems where a user with "Secret" clearance cannot access "Top Secret" files regardless of ownership.

**RBAC — Role-Based Access Control (2 marks):**
- Access is based on the **role** assigned to a user.
- Roles correspond to job functions (e.g., HR Manager, Finance Officer, Auditor).
- Example: An "HR Executive" role automatically gets permissions to view employee records, generate payslips, but not to approve loans.

**Comparison Table (1 mark):**

| Aspect | DAC | MAC | RBAC |
|---|---|---|---|
| Decided by | Owner | System policy | Role definition |
| Flexibility | High | Low | Medium |
| Best for | Collaborative systems | Military / Government | Enterprises |
| Weakness | Trojan-horse risks | Inflexible | Role explosion |
| Granularity | Per-user | Label-based | Per-role |

---

### Question B — 14 Marks

#### (a) Explain the Risk Management process. Calculate ALE for an asset worth ₹40,00,000 with EF=0.6 and ARO=0.25. If a control reduces EF to 0.2 and costs ₹4,00,000/year, is it justified? `[7 Marks]`

**Model Answer:**

**Risk Management Process (3 marks):**
1. **Asset Identification** — list all information assets and their values.
2. **Threat Identification** — identify potential threats (natural, human, environmental).
3. **Vulnerability Assessment** — find weaknesses that threats could exploit.
4. **Risk Assessment** — calculate risk as $R = T \times V \times I$.
5. **Risk Treatment** — Mitigate, Transfer, Accept, or Avoid.
6. **Control Selection & Implementation.**
7. **Monitoring & Review.**

**Calculation (3 marks):**

**Before control:**
$$SLE = AV \times EF = 40{,}00{,}000 \times 0.6 = 24{,}00{,}000$$

$$ALE_{before} = SLE \times ARO = 24{,}00{,}000 \times 0.25 = 6{,}00{,}000$$

**After control (EF = 0.2, ARO unchanged):**
$$SLE_{after} = 40{,}00{,}000 \times 0.2 = 8{,}00{,}000$$

$$ALE_{after} = 8{,}00{,}000 \times 0.25 = 2{,}00{,}000$$

**Annual Savings:**
$$\text{Savings} = 6{,}00{,}000 - 2{,}00{,}000 = 4{,}00{,}000$$

**Net Value:**
$$\text{Net Value} = \text{Savings} - \text{Cost of Control} = 4{,}00{,}000 - 4{,}00{,}000 = 0$$

**Decision (1 mark):**
The control is **marginally justified** — it breaks even financially. Considering qualitative benefits (compliance, reputation, customer trust), **the control is recommended**.

---

#### (b) Describe the cryptographic controls used to ensure confidentiality, integrity, and authentication. Mention relevant algorithms. `[7 Marks]`

**Model Answer:**

**Confidentiality via Encryption (2.5 marks):**
- **Symmetric encryption** — same key for encryption and decryption.
  - Algorithms: AES (128/192/256-bit), DES (legacy), 3DES, ChaCha20.
  - Use case: bulk data encryption (databases, file systems).
  - Formula: $C = E_K(P)$.
- **Asymmetric encryption** — public-private keypair.
  - Algorithms: RSA, ECC (Elliptic Curve Cryptography).
  - Use case: secure key exchange, digital envelopes.
  - Formula: $C = E_{K_{pub}}(P)$.

**Integrity via Hashing (2 marks):**
- One-way hash functions map data of any size to a fixed-size digest.
- Algorithms: **SHA-256, SHA-3, MD5 (legacy/weak)**.
- Properties: deterministic, one-way, collision-resistant, avalanche-effect.
- **HMAC** combines a hash with a shared secret key for message authentication.

**Authentication via Digital Signatures & Certificates (2.5 marks):**
- **Digital Signature** — Sender signs with **private key**; receiver verifies with **public key**.
  - Formula: $S = \text{Sign}_{K_{priv}}(H(M))$.
  - Provides integrity + authentication + non-repudiation.
- **Digital Certificates** — issued by trusted Certificate Authorities (CAs), bind a public key to an identity.
- **Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA)** — combines something you know (password), have (token), and are (biometric).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> 1. **Common mistake:** Students list "firewall" as both preventive *and* detective. While *stateful inspection* firewalls can be both, a *pure packet filter* is only preventive. **Read the question carefully.**
> 2. **Common mistake:** Writing "RBAC = role-based" without examples. **Always provide one example role** (e.g., HR Executive, Auditor).
> 3. **Common mistake:** Confusing **ALE with SLE**. SLE = single incident loss; ALE = annual expected loss. Examiners deduct full marks if these are swapped.
> 4. **Common mistake:** Writing $C = E_K(P)$ as a definition **without explaining what $K$, $P$, $C$, $E$, $D$ stand for**. Always define your variables.
> 5. **Common mistake:** Treating *detective* and *corrective* controls as the same. Detective = *finds* the incident; Corrective = *fixes* it.
> 6. **Common mistake:** Forgetting to mention **compensating controls** when listing categories — this is a frequently-tested item and missing it costs marks.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Information Security Controls**

- **Definition:** Controls are safeguards that modify/reduce risk to CIA triad.
- **Three Control Types:** Administrative, Technical, Physical — **all three needed**.
- **Six Control Functions:** Preventive, Detective, Corrective, Deterrent, Recovery, Compensating.
- **CIA Triad:** Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability — always map your answer back to these.
- **Risk Formula:** $R = T \times V \times I$; **ALE = SLE × ARO**.
- **Decision Rule:** Implement a control if **Savings ≥ Cost**; otherwise, consider accepting, transferring, or avoiding the risk.
- **Access Control Models:** DAC (owner), MAC (system/labels), RBAC (role), ABAC (attributes), Rule-Based (rules).
- **Defense in Depth:** Seven layers — policy → data; no single layer is sufficient.
- **NIST CSF 2.0 (2024):** Identify → Protect → Detect → Respond → Recover.
- **Cryptographic Controls:** Symmetric (AES), Asymmetric (RSA/ECC), Hash (SHA-256), Digital Signature (private sign / public verify), HMAC.
- **Network Controls:** Firewall, IDS/IPS, VPN, DMZ, NAC.
- **Standards to mention in exams:** ISO 27001:2022, NIST SP 800-53, COBIT, IT Act 2000, DPDP Act 2023.
- **Mnemonic for control functions:** **"PeeDeeCoDeReCo"** → **P**reventive, **De**tective, **Co**rrective, **De**terrent, **Re**covery, **Co**mpensating.
- **Mnemonic for access models:** **"D-M-R-R-A"** → **D**AC, **M**AC, **R**BAC, **R**ule-based, **A**BAC.
- **Always cite real-world examples** in 14-mark answers to fetch full marks.
- **Always end a 14-mark answer** with a conclusion linking controls to CIA triad or to a recognized framework (ISO 27001 / NIST CSF).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Personal Information Asset Protection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEADT523 - INFORMATION SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Personal Information Asset Protection ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview > [!NOTE] > **Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)** > **Personal Information Asset Protection (PIAP)** refers to the systematic framework of policies, procedures, technologies, and human behavioral practices designed to identify, classify, safeguard, and manage Personally Identifiable Information (PII) and other sensitive personal data against unauthorized access, disclosure, alteration, or destruction. It is a subset of **Information Security Governance** that treats personal data as a *valuable corporate and individual asset* requiring life-cycle management. ### 1.1 What Qualifies as a "Personal Information Asset"? In the KTU 2024 scheme, personal information is broadly classified into three categories: | Category | Definition | KTU 2024 Examples | |---|---|---| | **PII (Personally Identifiable Information)** | Data that can directly identify an individual | Aadhaar Number, Passport Number, Phone Number | | **SPI (Sensitive Personal Information)** | A subset of PII that causes harm if breached | Biometric data, Financial credentials, Health records | | **Behavioral / Inferred Data** | Data derived from user actions and preferences | Browsing history, location trails, purchase patterns | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 High-Yield Definition Box** > The **"Asset"** metaphor is critical in KTU examinations. Treat personal data exactly like a physical asset (e.g., gold or a house) — it has a **monetary value on the dark web**, requires **insurance (backups)**, needs a **lock (encryption)**, and demands **surveillance (monitoring)**. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Digital House" Model > [!TIP] > **Intuitive Analogy: Your Data is a House** > Imagine your personal information is a **house** in a city: > - **The Land (Data Subject):** You are the owner of the house. > - **The Walls (Firewall & Access Control):** Stop intruders from entering. > - **The Lock (Encryption):** Even if someone breaks in, they cannot read your valuables. > - **The CCTV (Monitoring & Logging):** Records who came, when, and what they did. > - **The Safe (Tokenization & Masking):** Hides the most precious items (passwords, biometrics). > - **The Will & Legal Papers (Privacy Policy & Consent):** Defines who inherits your data and under what conditions it can be shared. > - **The Insurance (Backup & Recovery):** Ensures you can rebuild if the house is destroyed (ransomware attack). This analogy makes it immediately clear that **PIAP is not a single tool, but a layered defense-in-depth strategy**. ### 1.3 Why is Personal Information an "Asset"? The KTU curriculum emphasizes the **Asset-Based View of Information**, which states: > Information is a **tangible, measurable corporate asset** that appears on the balance sheet under intangible assets and must be **accounted for, depreciated, and protected** like any other resource. **Three economic reasons why personal information is treated as an asset:** 1. **Direct Market Value:** Credit card data sells for **\$5–\$110**, medical records for **\$250–\$1000**, and full identity profiles for **\$1000+** on dark web marketplaces. 2. **Regulatory Liability:** Under **GDPR fines reach €20 million or 4% of global turnover**; under India's **Digital Personal Data Protection Act (DPDPA) 2023**, fines reach **₹250 crore per breach**. 3. **Reputational Capital:** Loss of customer trust causes long-term brand erosion, quantified as a **30–50% drop in customer retention** post-breach (per IBM Cost of a Data Breach Report benchmarks). ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Personal Information Protection Life-Cycle (PIPLC) The KTU 2024 module structures PIAP around a **five-stage life-cycle model**. Each stage demands specific controls: | Stage | Core Activity | KTU-Standard Control | |---|---|---| | **1. Identification** | Inventory of all PII across systems | Data Discovery & Classification Tools | | **2. Classification** | Label data (Public, Internal, Confidential, Restricted) | Metadata tagging, DLP (Data Loss Prevention) rules | | **3. Protection** | Apply cryptographic & access controls | AES-256 encryption, RBAC, MFA | | **4. Monitoring** | Continuous surveillance of data flows | SIEM (Security Information & Event Management) | | **5. Disposal / Retention** | Secure deletion at end of life | Crypto-shredding, NIST 800-88 media sanitization | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Definition: CIA Triad Extension for PII** > Personal Information Asset Protection extends the traditional **CIA Triad** (Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability) with two additional pillars: **Privacy** (controlling *how* data is used) and **Consent** (the data subject's right to authorize processing). ### 2.2 The Layered Defense Model (Defense in Depth) Personal information must be protected through **six concentric layers**. A failure in one layer is absorbed by the next, ensuring no single point of failure exists. **Layer 1 — Perimeter Security:** - Function: Blocks unauthorized external access. - Tools: **Firewalls**, **DMZ (Demilitarized Zone)**, **WAF (Web Application Firewall)**. - Why it matters: Stops ~80% of automated bot attacks at the network edge. **Layer 2 — Network Security:** - Function: Secures data *in transit* between systems. - Tools: **TLS 1.3**, **IPSec VPN**, **802.1X port authentication**. - Why it matters: Prevents eavesdropping on Wi-Fi and LAN segments. **Layer 3 — System Security:** - Function: Hardens endpoints and servers. - Tools: **OS patching**, **antivirus**, **EDR (Endpoint Detection & Response)**, **HIDS**. - Why it matters: Closes vulnerabilities like **CVE-2024-XXXX** that ransomware exploits. **Layer 4 — Application Security:** - Function: Secures the software layer handling PII. - Tools: **Input validation**, **parameterized queries** (anti-SQLi), **SAST/DAST scanning**. - Why it matters: Most data breaches (per **OWASP Top 10**) occur at the application layer. **Layer 5 — Data Security (The Core Asset Layer):** - Function: Protects the data *itself* — at rest, in use, and in motion. - Tools: **AES-256 encryption**, **TDE (Transparent Data Encryption)**, **tokenization**, **hashing (bcrypt/Argon2)**. - Why it matters: Even if all outer layers fail, the data remains unreadable ciphertext. **Layer 6 — Human & Policy Layer:** -
