<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> These are model Part A questions for the KTU 2024 End Semester Examination (ESE). Each is calibrated for a precise 3-mark response of 60–90 words.

### Question 1: Define CRM. Differentiate between Operational and Analytical CRM.
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Similar Pattern]**

**Model Answer:**
*Customer Relationship Management (CRM) is a strategic approach that uses people, processes, and technology to manage and analyze customer interactions, with the goal of building long-term, profitable customer relationships.*

*Operational CRM* focuses on automating and streamlining customer-facing business processes such as sales force automation, marketing automation, and service automation. *Analytical CRM* focuses on analyzing customer data to extract patterns, segment customers, and predict future behavior (e.g., churn, lifetime value). In short, operational CRM **executes**; analytical CRM **thinks**.

*(Examiner's expected: definition + 2 distinct differences. 60–80 words.)*

---

### Question 2: What is the Bullwhip Effect in Supply Chain Management? State any two of its causes.
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Similar Pattern]**

**Model Answer:**
*The Bullwhip Effect is the phenomenon of progressive amplification of demand variability as one moves upstream in a supply chain — from retailer to wholesaler to manufacturer to supplier.*

Two major causes are:
1. **Demand forecast updating** — each tier updates its forecast based on the order it receives, compounding errors at each level.
2. **Order batching** — firms place orders periodically (e.g., monthly) rather than continuously, causing lumpy orders upstream.

*(Examiner's expected: definition + 2 causes. 50–70 words.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not write generic essays.** Tie every paragraph to a specific concept, model, or framework.
> 2. **Diagrams are mandatory** for full marks in 14-mark questions — at least one Mermaid-style / block diagram in your answer sheet.
> 3. **Real-world examples** (Amazon, Flipkart, Zomato) earn extra marks under the 2024 scheme's "Application Level" CO mapping.
> 4. **Avoid bullet-point-only answers** for 14-mark questions; examiners expect at least 2 paragraphs of prose per sub-part.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the major components and architecture of Customer Relationship Management (CRM). Discuss the role of analytical CRM in customer segmentation with a suitable example. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the four processes of the SCOR (Supply Chain Operations Reference) model. How does information sharing mitigate the bullwhip effect? **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

**(a) CRM Components, Architecture, and Analytical CRM Role (7 Marks)**

CRM has three broad components:
- **People** – the sales, marketing, and service teams who interact with customers.
- **Process** – the standardized workflows for lead nurturing, onboarding, support, and loyalty.
- **Technology** – the CRM software platform (e.g., Salesforce, Zoho) that unifies customer data.

The **architecture** typically follows a layered model:
1. **Operational Layer** – automates front-office functions (sales, marketing, service).
2. **Analytical Layer** – mines customer data, applies OLAP and data mining to discover patterns.
3. **Collaborative Layer** – enables interaction with partners and customers via portals and communities.

**Role of Analytical CRM in Customer Segmentation:**
Analytical CRM uses historical transaction data, demographic attributes, and behavioral signals to segment customers. A popular technique is **RFM (Recency, Frequency, Monetary)** analysis, which classifies customers into tiers such as *Champions*, *Loyal Customers*, *At-Risk*, and *Hibernating*.

*Example:* An online fashion retailer like Myntra uses analytical CRM to identify that customers who purchased in the last 30 days, with more than 5 transactions, and total spend exceeding ₹10,000, are *Champions*. These customers receive early access to sales, premium loyalty points, and personalized style recommendations — driving both retention and revenue.

**(b) SCOR Model and Bullwhip Mitigation (7 Marks)**

The **SCOR model** defines five core management processes (often called the four-plus-one):
1. **Plan** – Demand and supply planning, inventory strategy, S&OP (Sales and Operations Planning).
2. **Source** – Procurement of goods and services, supplier evaluation, contract management.
3. **Make** – Transformation of products through manufacturing, assembly, packaging.
4. **Deliver** – Order management, warehousing, transportation, last-mile delivery.
5. **Return** – Reverse logistics for defective, excess, or end-of-life products.

*(Insert diagram: five blocks connected sequentially, with information flow looping back to Plan.)*

**How Information Sharing Mitigates the Bullwhip Effect:**

The bullwhip effect arises primarily from a *lack of visibility* across supply chain tiers. When end-customer demand data is shared in real time with upstream partners through techniques such as:
- **VMI (Vendor Managed Inventory)** – the supplier directly monitors and replenishes the retailer's stock.
- **CPFR (Collaborative Planning, Forecasting, and Replenishment)** – joint forecasting between retailer and supplier.
- **EDI (Electronic Data Interchange)** and **RFID-based real-time tracking** – instant information exchange.

…the amplification of demand variance is dramatically reduced. Each tier can base its orders on *actual* end demand rather than inflated retailer orders, breaking the bullwhip pattern. The **central insight**: information asymmetry is the root cause; information symmetry is the cure.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Internal Choice Alternative)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Pattern]**

**(a)** Define Supply Chain Management. Explain the upstream, internal, and downstream components of a supply chain with a real-world example. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the role of CRM in enhancing customer lifetime value. Illustrate with the formula for CLV and explain the strategic significance of reducing CAC relative to CLV. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

**(a) Supply Chain Management and its Components (7 Marks)**

SCM is the integrated management of all activities involved in the flow of goods, services, information, and finances from raw material suppliers to the end consumer, with the goal of enhancing customer value and achieving competitive advantage.

**Components:**

- **Upstream Supply Chain**: Activities related to sourcing raw materials and components from suppliers. Example: Apple sourcing lithium and rare earth minerals from Australia and Chile for iPhone batteries.
- **Internal Supply Chain**: Operations within the firm — production scheduling, quality control, inventory management. Example: Foxconn assembling iPhones in its Shenzhen and Tamil Nadu plants.
- **Downstream Supply Chain**: Distribution, warehousing, retail, and delivery to the end customer. Example: Apple Store retail network and third-party logistics partners like DHL and FedEx handling iPhone distribution globally.

A fourth, increasingly important, component is **Reverse Logistics** — handling returns, recycling, and refurbishment. Apple's recycling program "Daisy" that disassembles old iPhones to recover valuable materials is a leading example.

**(b) CRM's Role in CLV and the CLV-CAC Relationship (7 Marks)**

**Customer Lifetime Value (CLV)** is the total revenue a customer is expected to generate over the entire duration of their relationship with the firm, minus the costs of serving them. The formula is:

$$
\text{CLV} = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \dfrac{(R_{t} - C_{t})}{(1+d)^{t}}
$$

where $R_t$ is the revenue in year $t$, $C_t$ is the cost of serving the customer in year $t$, $d$ is the discount rate (to account for the time value of money), and $n$ is the time horizon.

**Role of CRM in Enhancing CLV:**
CRM enhances CLV through three levers:
1. **Increasing revenue per customer** via cross-selling, up-selling, and personalized offers.
2. **Extending customer lifespan** through loyalty programs, proactive service, and relationship nurturing.
3. **Reducing service cost** by automating support, predicting churn, and self-service portals.

**Strategic Significance of the CLV : CAC Ratio:**

**Customer Acquisition Cost (CAC)** is the total marketing and sales spend divided by the number of new customers acquired. The ratio:

$$
\text{CLV : CAC} = \dfrac{\text{CLV}}{\text{CAC}}
$$

is a critical health metric. A ratio of **3 : 1 or higher** is considered healthy; below **1 : 1** indicates the firm is losing money on each customer. CRM systems enable precise tracking of both numerator and denominator, allowing the firm to optimize marketing spend, target high-CLV segments, and discard unprofitable channels. For SaaS companies like Salesforce or HubSpot, this ratio is the single most-watched KPI by investors and boards.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **CRM = People + Process + Technology**, not just software. It is a business *strategy*.
- ✅ Three flavors of CRM: **Operational** (executes), **Analytical** (analyzes), **Collaborative** (communicates).
- ✅ The **CRM value chain**: Capture → Store → Analyze → Act → Measure → Refine.
- ✅ **RFM analysis** is the most commonly tested analytical CRM technique.
- ✅ **Acquiring a new customer is 5–7× costlier** than retaining an existing one.
- ✅ **SCM = Upstream + Internal + Downstream + Reverse Logistics**.
- ✅ The **five flows in SCM**: Product, Information, Financial, Value, Reverse.
- ✅ The **SCOR model** has five processes: Plan, Source, Make, Deliver, Return.
- ✅ The **Bullwhip Effect** amplifies demand variance upstream; the cure is **information sharing**.
- ✅ **EOQ = √(2DS / H)** — minimizes the sum of ordering and holding costs.
- ✅ **ROP = d × L** — when inventory falls to ROP, place a new order of size Q*.
- ✅ **CLV : CAC ≥ 3 : 1** is the golden rule for sustainable customer economics.
- ✅ **Inventory Turnover Ratio = COGS / Average Inventory** — higher is better.
- ✅ **Perfect Order Rate (POR)** measures order delivery quality end-to-end.
- ✅ **CRM and SCM are complementary**, not substitutes — integrate them through ERP.
- ✅ Always draw at least **one block diagram** in a 14-mark question; the 2024 scheme rewards visual answers.
- ✅ **Real-world examples** (Amazon, Flipkart, Zoho, Myntra, Apple) significantly boost examiner perception scores.
- ✅ The **3 Vs of modern SCM**: Velocity, Variety, Visibility — memorize and quote in essay-type questions.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT523 - INFORMATION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Acquiring Information Systems and Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Acquisition of Information Systems (IS)** is the formal organizational process of identifying, evaluating, procuring, and implementing hardware, software, services, and supporting infrastructure required to build, replace, or upgrade an organization's information system. It encompasses strategic decisions regarding whether the system should be **built in-house**, **purchased as a package (COTS)**, **leased**, **outsourced**, or delivered through **cloud/SaaS** subscription models.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, *acquisition* is positioned within the **Systems Development Life Cycle (SDLC)** as the transition phase from **System Analysis & Design** to **Implementation**. It is governed by organizational policies, financial viability models, and strategic alignment with enterprise goals.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you need transportation for daily office commute. You essentially have **four options**:

1. **Buy a car outright** → Equivalent to **Build** (in-house development). High upfront cost, full ownership, complete customization.
2. **Buy a pre-owned certified car** → Equivalent to **Buy COTS (Commercial Off-The-Shelf)** software. Lower risk, faster deployment, limited customization.
3. **Take a car on rent (monthly)** → Equivalent to **Lease** or **Subscription-based acquisition**. Predictable OpEx, no ownership.
4. **Use Uber/Ola daily** → Equivalent to **Outsource or SaaS (Software as a Service)**. Zero capital, pay-per-use, minimal control.

> [!NOTE]
> Just as no single transport option suits everyone, **no single IS acquisition method fits all organizations**. The choice depends on cost, time, risk, customization, and strategic alignment.

### 1.3 Why Acquisition Matters in IS Management

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Organizations typically spend **30% to 50%** of their total IS budget on **acquisition and implementation**. Poor acquisition decisions can lead to project failure rates exceeding **70%** (Standish Group CHAOS Report), making this a *high-stakes strategic activity*.

**Key Drivers of IS Acquisition:**
- **Business Need:** New functionality, regulatory compliance, competitive pressure.
- **Technology Obsolescence:** Legacy system replacement.
- **Scalability Requirements:** Handling increased transaction volume.
- **Cost Optimization:** Reducing Total Cost of Ownership (TCO).
- **Innovation Enablement:** Adopting AI, Cloud, Big Data capabilities.

### 1.4 Overview of Acquisition Approaches

| Approach | Description | Typical Use Case |
|----------|-------------|------------------|
| **Build (In-House)** | Custom development by internal IT team | Strategic, differentiating systems |
| **Buy (COTS Package)** | Purchase pre-built vendor software | Standard business processes (HR, Finance) |
| **Lease** | Time-bound usage with periodic payments | Short-term or transitional needs |
| **Outsource** | Third-party vendor handles development/maintenance | Non-core competency areas |
| **SaaS / Cloud** | Subscription-based delivery over the Internet | Scalable, distributed applications |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Acquisition Process – Stepwise Logical Flow

The acquisition of an Information System is **not a single event** but a structured, multi-stage process. Below is the rigorous breakdown:

1. **Identify the Need / Trigger Event**
   * Business problem, opportunity, or compliance mandate surfaces.
   * Documented in a *Business Case* or *System Request*.

2. **Feasibility Analysis**
   * Three pillars: **Technical**, **Economic**, and **Operational (Schedule/Organizational)** feasibility.
   * Determines whether the project is worth pursuing at all.

3. **Make-or-Buy Decision**
   * The pivotal strategic choice: build internally, acquire externally, or hybrid.
   * Driven by cost-benefit analysis, in-house capability, time-to-market, and strategic importance.

4. **Define Acquisition Strategy**
   * Decide between RFP (Request for Proposal), RFI (Request for Information), RFQ (Request for Quotation), or direct negotiation.

5. **Vendor Evaluation & Shortlisting**
   * Evaluate candidates against weighted criteria (cost, functionality, support, reputation).

6. **Negotiation & Contracting**
   * Finalize SLAs (Service Level Agreements), pricing, IP rights, and exit clauses.

7. **Procurement & Implementation**
   * Software installation, hardware deployment, configuration, testing, and go-live.

8. **Post-Implementation Review**
   * Measure actual benefits vs. projected benefits; capture lessons learned.

### 2.2 Build vs. Buy Decision Framework

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Make-or-Buy decision** is the most frequently examined concept in KTU examinations on this topic. Master both the *qualitative criteria* and the *quantitative financial models*.

#### 2.2.1 Qualitative Criteria for Make-or-Buy

| Factor | Favors **MAKE (Build)** | Favors **BUY (Acquire)** |
|--------|--------------------------|---------------------------|
| Strategic Importance | Core to competitive advantage | Supports non-core function |
| Customization Needs | Highly unique requirements | Standard process fit |
| Time Pressure | Long timeline acceptable | Rapid deployment required |
| In-House Expertise | Skilled IT team available | Skill gap exists |
| Cost Structure | High recurring customization cost | High one-time license cost |
| Risk Tolerance | Organization can absorb risk | Vendor assumes implementation risk |

#### 2.2.2 Quantitative Financial Models (The "Why" Behind the Numbers)

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following formulas are the **highest-yield quantitative tools** for the KTU University Exam. Memorize the formula, the meaning of each variable, and the decision rule.

| Formula | Expression | Decision Rule / Interpretation |
|---------|------------|--------------------------------|
| **Net Present Value (NPV)** | $NPV = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \dfrac{CF_{t}}{(1+r)^{t}}$ | If $NPV > 0$, accept the project |
| **Internal Rate of Return (IRR)** | $NPV = 0$ solved for $r$ | If $IRR > r$ (hurdle rate), accept |
| **Payback Period** | $PP = \dfrac{\text{Initial Investment}}{\text{Annual Cash Inflow}}$ | Shorter is better (subject to threshold) |
| **Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)** | $TCO = DC + IC$ | Lower TCO is preferred |
| **Return on Investment (ROI)** | $ROI = \dfrac{(\text{Benefits} - \text{Costs})}{\text{Costs}} \times 100\%$ | Higher % indicates better investment |
| **Break-Even Point (units)** | $BEP = \dfrac{\text{Fixed Costs}}{\text{Price per Unit} - \text{Variable Cost per Unit}}$ | Point where revenue equals total cost |

**Variable Glossary (used across all formulas):**

- $CF_{t}$ = Cash flow in period $t$
- $r$ = Discount rate (cost of capital, typically **8% to 12%**)
- $n$ = Project lifespan in years
- $DC$ = Direct Costs (acquisition, licensing, hardware)
- $IC$ = Indirect Costs (training, downtime, support, maintenance)

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In large-scale **enterprise engineering projects** (e.g., smart grid deployment, ERP rollout in a manufacturing plant, hospital management system), the acquisition decision determines:

- **Capital Expenditure (CapEx)** vs. **Operational Expenditure (OpEx)** classification on the balance sheet.
- Long-term **vendor lock-in** and switching costs.
- Compliance posture against **ISO 27001**, **GDPR**, and industry-specific regulations.
- Scalability ceilings when the organization doubles in size.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **In KTU valuation**, when a question asks "Justify the Make-or-Buy decision," you must present **at least three qualitative factors AND one quantitative metric (NPV or Payback)**. Partial answers receive partial credit.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Detailed Worked Example: NPV-Based Acquisition Decision

> [!NOTE]
> The following exhaustive example is the **type of 14-mark question** KTU examiners frequently set. Every algebraic transition is explicitly shown — no steps are skipped.

**Problem Statement (KTU-Style):**
> A manufacturing company is evaluating two options to acquire a new inventory management system:
>
> **Option A – Build In-House:** Initial investment = **₹25,00,000**. Expected cash inflows: Year 1 = ₹6,00,000; Year 2 = ₹8,00,000; Year 3 = ₹10,00,000; Year 4 = ₹12,00,000. Salvage value at end of Year 4 = ₹2,00,000.
>
> **Option B – Buy COTS Package:** Initial investment = ₹15,00,000. Expected cash inflows: Year 1 = ₹5,00,000; Year 2 = ₹5,00,000; Year 3 = ₹5,00,000; Year 4 = ₹5,00,000. Annual maintenance cost = ₹1,00,000 from Year 1 onwards. Salvage value = ₹1,00,000.
>
> The cost of capital (discount rate) is **10%**. Using NPV, recommend the better acquisition option.

---

#### 3.1.1 Step-by-Step NPV Computation – Option A (Build)

**Step 1:** Identify the discount rate and time horizon.

$$
r = 0.10, \quad n = 4 \text{ years}
$$

**Step 2:** Write the NPV formula.

$$
NPV = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \frac{CF_{t}}{(1+r)^{t}}
$$

**Step 3:** Expand the summation for $t = 0$ to $t = 4$.

$$
NPV_{A} = \frac{CF_{0}}{(1.10)^{0}} + \frac{CF_{1}}{(1.10)^{1}} + \frac{CF_{2}}{(1.10)^{2}} + \frac{CF_{3}}{(1.10)^{3}} + \frac{CF_{4}}{(1.10)^{4}}
$$

**Step 4:** Substitute actual values. Note that $CF_0$ is the initial investment (negative outflow).

$$
NPV_{A} = \frac{-25{,}00{,}000}{1.0000} + \frac{6{,}00{,}000}{1.1000} + \frac{8{,}00{,}000}{1.2100} + \frac{10{,}00{,}000}{1.3310} + \frac{12{,}00{,}000}{1.4641}
$$

**Step 5:** Calculate each term individually for clarity.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Term } t=0 &: \quad -25{,}00{,}000.00 \\
\text{Term } t=1 &: \quad \frac{6{,}00{,}000}{1.10} = 5{,}45{,}454.55 \\
\text{Term } t=2 &: \quad \frac{8{,}00{,}000}{1.21} = 6{,}61{,}157.02 \\
\text{Term } t=3 &: \quad \frac{10{,}00{,}000}{1.331} = 7{,}51{,}314.80 \\
\text{Term } t=4 &: \quad \frac{(12{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}00{,}000)}{1.4641} = \frac{14{,}00{,}000}{1.4641} = 9{,}56{,}151.36
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6:** Sum all the discounted cash flows.

$$
\begin{aligned}
NPV_{A} &= -25{,}00{,}000.00 + 5{,}45{,}454.55 + 6{,}61{,}157.02 + 7{,}51{,}314.80 + 9{,}56{,}151.36 \\
NPV_{A} &= -25{,}00{,}000.00 + 29{,}14{,}077.73 \\
NPV_{A} &= +4{,}14{,}077.73
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion for Option A:** $NPV_{A} = +₹4,14,077.73$ (Positive → Project is financially viable)

---

#### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step NPV Computation – Option B (Buy COTS)

**Step 1:** Identify net cash flow per year (inflows minus maintenance outflows).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Net Cash Flow (Year 1)} &= 5{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}00{,}000 = 4{,}00{,}000 \\
\text{Net Cash Flow (Year 2)} &= 5{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}00{,}000 = 4{,}00{,}000 \\
\text{Net Cash Flow (Year 3)} &= 5{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}00{,}000 = 4{,}00{,}000 \\
\text{Net Cash Flow (Year 4)} &= 5{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}00{,}000 = 4{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Add salvage value to the Year 4 cash flow.

$$
\text{Year 4 total} = 4{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}00{,}000 = 5{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 3:** Apply the NPV formula.

$$
NPV_{B} = \frac{-15{,}00{,}000}{1.0000} + \frac{4{,}00{,}000}{1.10} + \frac{4{,}00{,}000}{1.21} + \frac{4{,}00{,}000}{1.331} + \frac{5{,}00{,}000}{1.4641}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute each discounted term.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Term } t=0 &: \quad -15{,}00{,}000.00 \\
\text{Term } t=1 &: \quad \frac{4{,}00{,}000}{1.10} = 3{,}63{,}636.36 \\
\text{Term } t=2 &: \quad \frac{4{,}00{,}000}{1.21} = 3{,}30{,}578.51 \\
\text{Term } t=3 &: \quad \frac{4{,}00{,}000}{1.331} = 3{,}00{,}525.92 \\
\text{Term } t=4 &: \quad \frac{5{,}00{,}000}{1.4641} = 3{,}41{,}482.63
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** Sum all terms.

$$
\begin{aligned}
NPV_{B} &= -15{,}00{,}000.00 + 3{,}63{,}636.36 + 3{,}30{,}578.51 + 3{,}00{,}525.92 + 3{,}41{,}482.63 \\
NPV_{B} &= -15{,}00{,}000.00 + 13{,}36{,}223.42 \\
NPV_{B} &= -1{,}63{,}776.58
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion for Option B:** $NPV_{B} = -₹1,63,776.58$ (Negative → Project is NOT financially viable at 10% discount rate)

---

#### 3.1.3 Comparative Decision Matrix

| Metric | Option A: Build | Option B: Buy |
|--------|-----------------|---------------|
| Initial Investment | ₹25,00,000 | ₹15,00,000 |
| Total Cash Inflows (undiscounted) | ₹36,00,000 | ₹21,00,000 |
| NPV @ 10% | **+₹4,14,077.73** | **-₹1,63,776.58** |
| Decision Recommendation | **ACCEPT** | **REJECT** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Recommendation:** Despite the higher initial outlay, the company should **build the system in-house** (Option A) because it yields a positive NPV, whereas buying the COTS package results in a negative NPV. The qualitative factors (customization, strategic fit) reinforce this quantitative conclusion.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example: Payback Period for Quick Screening

Suppose an alternative COTS proposal has:
- Initial Investment = **₹12,00,000**
- Annual Cash Inflow = **₹3,00,000**

$$
PP = \frac{\text{Initial Investment}}{\text{Annual Cash Inflow}} = \frac{12{,}00{,}000}{3{,}00{,}000} = 4 \text{ years}
$$

If the organization's acceptable payback threshold is **3 years**, this proposal is **rejected on screening grounds**, even before deeper NPV analysis.

---

### 3.3 Total Cost of Ownership (TCO) Decomposition

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners often include a "hidden costs" sub-question. Students who only quote the license fee lose 2–3 marks. Use this exhaustive TCO breakdown.

$$
TCO = (C_{HW} + C_{SW} + C_{Imp}) + (C_{Train} + C_{Supp} + C_{Maint} + C_{Down})
$$

**Where:**

- $C_{HW}$ = Hardware costs (servers, networking, end-user devices)
- $C_{SW}$ = Software license fees
- $C_{Imp}$ = Implementation & consulting costs
- $C_{Train}$ = End-user and administrator training
- $C_{Supp}$ = Annual vendor support contracts
- $C_{Maint}$ = Ongoing maintenance and upgrades
- $C_{Down}$ = Cost of downtime and productivity loss during outages

> [!WARNING]
> Industry benchmarks show that **indirect costs ($IC$) often exceed 50% of $TCO$** in the first three years. A KTU answer that ignores $C_{Train}$ and $C_{Down}$ is considered **incomplete**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Complete IS Acquisition Process (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify Business Need] --> B[Feasibility Study]
    B --> C{Make or Buy Decision}
    C -->|Build| D[In-House Development]
    C -->|Buy| E[Issue RFP / RFI]
    C -->|Outsource| F[Vendor Engagement]
    D --> G[Testing and QA]
    E --> H[Vendor Evaluation]
    F --> H
    H --> I[Contract Negotiation]
    G --> J[Implementation and Go-Live]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Post Implementation Review]
    K --> L[System Operational]
    L --> M{End of Life Cycle}
    M -->|Yes| A
    M -->|No| L

    style A fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
    style B fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
    style C fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
    style J fill:#548235,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
    style L fill:#7030a0,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
```

### 4.2 Make-or-Buy Decision Tree (Strategic Filter)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start: New System Need] --> Q1{Is the system strategically unique to our firm?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| R1{Do we have skilled in-house IT staff?}
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{Is rapid deployment critical within 6 months?}
    R1 -->|Yes| R2{High customisation required?}
    R1 -->|No| OUT[Outsource to specialist vendor]
    R2 -->|Yes| BUILD[Choose MAKE In-House Build]
    R2 -->|No| R3{Budget allows long term ROI?}
    R3 -->|Yes| BUILD
    R3 -->|No| COTS[Choose BUY COTS Package]
    Q2 -->|Yes| SAAS[Choose SaaS or Cloud Subscription]
    Q2 -->|No| Q3{Available budget is CapEx heavy?}
    Q3 -->|Yes| LEASE[Choose LEASE Model]
    Q3 -->|No| COTS

    style S fill:#002060,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
    style BUILD fill:#548235,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
    style COTS fill:#bf8f00,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
    style SAAS fill:#7030a0,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
    style LEASE fill:#c55a11,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
    style OUT fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
```

### 4.3 Functional Block Architecture: SaaS-Based Acquisition

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    USER[End Users] -->|HTTPS| EDGE[Edge Network and CDN]
    EDGE --> LB[Load Balancer]
    LB --> APP1[App Server Instance 1]
    LB --> APP2[App Server Instance 2]
    APP1 --> DB[Shared Database Cluster]
    APP2 --> DB
    APP1 --> MQ[Message Queue]
    APP2 --> MQ
    MQ --> WORKER[Background Worker Nodes]
    WORKER --> DB
    DB --> BACKUP[Encrypted Backup Vault]
    ADMIN[Cloud Administrator] -->|IAM Auth| APP1
    ADMIN -->|IAM Auth| APP2

    style USER fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
    style DB fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
    style ADMIN fill:#548235,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
```

### 4.4 Vendor Evaluation Weighted Scoring Model (Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Vendor Shortlist] --> B[Define Evaluation Criteria]
    B --> C1[Functionality 30 percent]
    B --> C2[Cost 25 percent]
    B --> C3[Vendor Reputation 15 percent]
    B --> C4[Technical Support 15 percent]
    B --> C5[Implementation Time 10 percent]
    B --> C6[Scalability 5 percent]
    C1 --> SC[Score Each Vendor 1 to 10]
    C2 --> SC
    C3 --> SC
    C4 --> SC
    C5 --> SC
    C6 --> SC
    SC --> WS[Weighted Sum Computation]
    WS --> R[Select Highest Scoring Vendor]

    style A fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
    style WS fill:#bf8f00,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
    style R fill:#548235,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define the term "Make-or-Buy decision" in the context of information system acquisition. List any four factors that influence this decision.*

**Model Answer:**

> The **Make-or-Buy decision** is the strategic choice an organization makes regarding whether to develop an information system using internal resources (MAKE) or to procure it from an external vendor (BUY) as a pre-built package or service.

**Four influencing factors:**

1. **Strategic Importance** – Whether the system provides competitive differentiation.
2. **Cost Considerations** – Total cost of in-house development vs. vendor pricing.
3. **Time-to-Market** – Urgency of deployment.
4. **In-House Expertise** – Availability of skilled IT personnel.

*(Any 4 valid factors accepted. Full 3 marks require definition + listing with brief justification.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Differentiate between COTS software and custom-built software. Which one carries higher long-term maintenance risk and why?*

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | COTS Software | Custom-Built Software |
|--------|---------------|----------------------|
| Origin | Vendor-developed, generic | Organization-specific |
| Cost | Lower initial cost | Higher initial cost |
| Customization | Limited | Fully customizable |
| Implementation Time | Short (weeks–months) | Long (months–years) |
| Vendor Dependency | High | Low |

**Higher long-term maintenance risk:** **COTS software**, because:
- The organization depends on the vendor for upgrades, patches, and bug fixes.
- If the vendor discontinues the product, the organization faces forced migration.
- License renewal costs may escalate unpredictably.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> Following the **KTU 2024 Scheme pattern**, each Part B question has two sub-parts of 7 marks each. Two complete alternative questions (A and B) are provided below; the student answers **either A or B**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – July 2024]*

**(a)** Explain in detail the **five major methods of acquiring information systems and applications**. Compare them on the basis of cost, time, risk, and customization. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A retail company is evaluating two ERP acquisition options. Use the **NPV method** to recommend the better one. Cost of capital = **12%**. **(7 Marks)**

**Option X – Cloud SaaS Subscription:**
- Yearly subscription cost = ₹8,00,000 (payable at start of each year, 4 years)
- Expected annual revenue benefit = ₹18,00,000
- Migration cost at the end of Year 4 = ₹2,00,000

**Option Y – On-Premise Licensed Purchase:**
- Initial license + hardware = ₹30,00,000
- Expected annual revenue benefit = ₹20,00,000
- Annual maintenance = ₹4,00,000
- Salvage value after Year 4 = ₹5,00,000

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.A(a)**

**Five Major Methods of Acquiring IS:**

1. **In-House Development (Build)**
   * Internal IT team designs and codes the system from scratch.
   * *Cost:* High initial; *Time:* Long; *Risk:* High; *Customization:* Maximum.

2. **Commercial Off-The-Shelf (COTS) Purchase**
   * Pre-packaged software licensed from a vendor (e.g., SAP, Oracle, Tally).
   * *Cost:* Moderate; *Time:* Short; *Risk:* Moderate; *Customization:* Limited.

3. **Outsourcing**
   * External vendor (often offshore) develops and/or maintains the system under contract.
   * *Cost:* Variable; *Time:* Medium; *Risk:* Vendor management; *Customization:* Moderate.

4. **Leasing / Rental**
   * Time-bound usage rights; periodic payment; no ownership transfer.
   * *Cost:* Predictable OpEx; *Time:* Immediate; *Risk:* Low; *Customization:* Minimal.

5. **SaaS / Cloud Subscription**
   * Software delivered over the Internet; pay-per-use subscription (e.g., AWS, Microsoft 365).
   * *Cost:* Subscription-based; *Time:* Immediate; *Risk:* Low; *Customization:* Moderate.

**Comparison Table:**

| Method | Cost | Time | Risk | Customization |
|--------|------|------|------|---------------|
| Build | High | Long | High | Maximum |
| COTS | Medium | Short | Medium | Limited |
| Outsource | Variable | Medium | Vendor Dependency | Moderate |
| Lease | Predictable | Immediate | Low | Minimal |
| SaaS | Subscription | Immediate | Low | Moderate |

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.A(b)** — NPV at 12% Discount Rate

**Step 1:** Discount factor for 12% is computed as $\dfrac{1}{(1.12)^t}$.

**Step 2 – Option X (Cloud SaaS):**
Subscription is paid at the start of each year, so Year 0 includes ₹8,00,000. Cash inflow is ₹18,00,000 at end of each year. Migration cost ₹2,00,000 at end of Year 4 is a deduction.

$$
NPV_{X} = -8{,}00{,}000 + \frac{(18{,}00{,}000 - 8{,}00{,}000)}{1.12} + \frac{(18{,}00{,}000 - 8{,}00{,}000)}{1.12^{2}} + \frac{(18{,}00{,}000 - 8{,}00{,}000)}{1.12^{3}} + \frac{(18{,}00{,}000 - 8{,}00{,}000 - 2{,}00{,}000)}{1.12^{4}}
$$

$$
NPV_{X} = -8{,}00{,}000 + \frac{10{,}00{,}000}{1.12} + \frac{10{,}00{,}000}{1.2544} + \frac{10{,}00{,}000}{1.4049} + \frac{8{,}00{,}000}{1.5735}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
NPV_{X} &= -8{,}00{,}000 + 8{,}92{,}857 + 7{,}97{,}194 + 7{,}11{,}781 + 5{,}08{,}404 \\
NPV_{X} &= ₹21{,}10{,}236 \text{ (approximately)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 – Option Y (On-Premise):**

$$
NPV_{Y} = -30{,}00{,}000 + \frac{(20{,}00{,}000 - 4{,}00{,}000)}{1.12} + \frac{(20{,}00{,}000 - 4{,}00{,}000)}{1.12^{2}} + \frac{(20{,}00{,}000 - 4{,}00{,}000)}{1.12^{3}} + \frac{(20{,}00{,}000 - 4{,}00{,}000 + 5{,}00{,}000)}{1.12^{4}}
$$

$$
NPV_{Y} = -30{,}00{,}000 + \frac{16{,}00{,}000}{1.12} + \frac{16{,}00{,}000}{1.2544} + \frac{16{,}00{,}000}{1.4049} + \frac{21{,}00{,}000}{1.5735}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
NPV_{Y} &= -30{,}00{,}000 + 14{,}28{,}571 + 12{,}75{,}510 + 11{,}38{,}849 + 13{,}34{,}604 \\
NPV_{Y} &= ₹11{,}77{,}534 \text{ (approximately)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 – Comparative Decision:**

| Metric | Option X (SaaS) | Option Y (On-Premise) |
|--------|-----------------|------------------------|
| Initial Outlay | ₹8,00,000 | ₹30,00,000 |
| NPV @ 12% | **₹21,10,236** | **₹11,77,534** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Recommendation:** **Option X (Cloud SaaS)** yields a substantially higher NPV. Additionally, it offers lower initial capital burden, automatic upgrades, and better scalability. **Choose Option X.**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]*

**(a)** Discuss the **role of a Request for Proposal (RFP)** in IS acquisition. What are the essential sections an effective RFP must contain? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compute the **Payback Period** and **ROI** for the following acquisition options and recommend the best one. **(7 Marks)**

| Option | Initial Cost (₹) | Annual Benefit (₹) | Project Life (Years) |
|--------|-------------------|---------------------|----------------------|
| P1 | 15,00,000 | 4,00,000 | 5 |
| P2 | 22,00,000 | 6,50,000 | 5 |
| P3 | 30,00,000 | 7,00,000 | 5 |

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.B(a)**

**Role of RFP in IS Acquisition:**

> [!NOTE]
> An **RFP (Request for Proposal)** is a formal document issued by the buyer inviting vendors to submit detailed proposals for supplying an information system. It standardizes evaluation, ensures fair competition, and forms the legal basis for contract negotiation.

**Key Roles:**
1. **Standardization** – All vendors respond to identical requirements.
2. **Comparability** – Facilitates apples-to-apples vendor comparison.
3. **Risk Reduction** – Documents expectations, SLAs, and acceptance criteria.
4. **Legal Protection** – Forms part of the contractual agreement.

**Essential Sections of an RFP:**

1. **Background and Introduction** – Organizational context, current state, project vision.
2. **Scope of Work** – Detailed functional and non-functional requirements.
3. **Technical Specifications** – Hardware, software, platform, integration needs.
4. **Vendor Qualifications** – Experience, certifications, financial stability.
5. **Project Timeline** – Milestones, deliverables, deadlines.
6. **Pricing Structure** – Licensing, support, training, and hidden cost expectations.
7. **Service Level Agreements (SLAs)** – Uptime, response time, escalation.
8. **Evaluation Criteria** – Weighted scoring methodology.
9. **Legal and Contractual Terms** – IP rights, confidentiality, termination clauses.
10. **Submission Instructions** – Format, deadlines, point of contact.

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.B(b)** — Payback Period and ROI

**Step 1 – Payback Period Formula:**

$$
PP = \frac{\text{Initial Cost}}{\text{Annual Benefit}}
$$

**Step 2 – Compute Payback for Each Option:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
PP_{P1} &= \frac{15{,}00{,}000}{4{,}00{,}000} = 3.75 \text{ years} \\
PP_{P2} &= \frac{22{,}00{,}000}{6{,}50{,}000} = 3.38 \text{ years} \\
PP_{P3} &= \frac{30{,}00{,}000}{7{,}00{,}000} = 4.29 \text{ years}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 – Compute Total Benefit Over 5 Years and ROI:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Benefit}_{P1} &= 4{,}00{,}000 \times 5 = 20{,}00{,}000 \\
\text{Net Gain}_{P1} &= 20{,}00{,}000 - 15{,}00{,}000 = 5{,}00{,}000 \\
ROI_{P1} &= \frac{5{,}00{,}000}{15{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = 33.33\%
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Benefit}_{P2} &= 6{,}50{,}000 \times 5 = 32{,}50{,}000 \\
\text{Net Gain}_{P2} &= 32{,}50{,}000 - 22{,}00{,}000 = 10{,}50{,}000 \\
ROI_{P2} &= \frac{10{,}50{,}000}{22{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = 47.73\%
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Benefit}_{P3} &= 7{,}00{,}000 \times 5 = 35{,}00{,}000 \\
\text{Net Gain}_{P3} &= 35{,}00{,}000 - 30{,}00{,}000 = 5{,}00{,}000 \\
ROI_{P3} &= \frac{5{,}00{,}000}{30{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = 16.67\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 – Comparative Summary:**

| Option | Payback (Years) | ROI (%) | Rank (Payback) | Rank (ROI) |
|--------|------------------|---------|----------------|------------|
| P1 | 3.75 | 33.33 | 2 | 2 |
| P2 | **3.38** | **47.73** | **1** | **1** |
| P3 | 4.29 | 16.67 | 3 | 3 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Recommendation:** **Option P2** is the best acquisition choice — it has the **shortest payback period (3.38 years)** AND the **highest ROI (47.73%)**, making it superior on both financial screening metrics.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU Valuation:**
>
> 1. **Stating only qualitative factors in Make-or-Buy answers** — Examiners expect *at least one quantitative model* (NPV, Payback, or ROI). Qualitative-only answers cap at 4 of 7 marks.
> 2. **Ignoring salvage value in NPV calculations** — Many students add cash inflows but forget to include the terminal salvage in the final year. **Always add it to $CF_n$.**
> 3. **Treating subscription costs as one-time outflows** — SaaS payments are recurring; they must appear in **every period's cash flow**, not just $t=0$.
> 4. **Confusing TCO with purchase price** — TCO includes training, support, and downtime. A COTS package with low license fee can have a *higher* TCO than a custom build.
> 5. **Not labeling diagrams** — In 14-mark questions where you draw a flow diagram (e.g., acquisition process), every box and arrow **must be labeled** or the diagram gets 0 marks.
> 6. **Forgetting the discount rate unit consistency** — If $r = 10\%$, use $0.10$ in formulas, not $10$. Students frequently write $\dfrac{CF}{(1+10)^t}$ instead of $\dfrac{CF}{(1+0.10)^t}$.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for the KTU 2024 Exam:**

- **Definition Ready:** Acquisition = process of identifying, evaluating, and procuring IS solutions (Build, Buy, Lease, Outsource, SaaS).
- **Five Major Methods Memorized:** In-House Build, COTS Purchase, Outsourcing, Leasing, SaaS/Cloud.
- **Make-or-Buy Qualitative Factors:** Strategic importance, customization, time, cost, expertise, risk.
- **Make-or-Buy Quantitative Tools:** NPV, IRR, Payback Period, ROI, TCO.
- **NPV Formula Mastered:** $NPV = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \dfrac{CF_{t}}{(1+r)^{t}}$ — Decision rule: $NPV > 0$ means **Accept**.
- **Payback Formula Mastered:** $PP = \dfrac{\text{Initial Investment}}{\text{Annual Cash Inflow}}$ — Shorter is better.
- **ROI Formula Mastered:** $ROI = \dfrac{(\text{Benefits} - \text{Costs})}{\text{Costs}} \times 100\%$.
- **TCO Includes:** Direct costs (hardware, software) **AND** indirect costs (training, support, downtime).
- **RFP Must Contain:** Scope, technical specs, vendor qualifications, SLAs, pricing, legal terms, evaluation criteria.
- **Vendor Evaluation:** Use weighted scoring model — never rely on price alone.
- **Always state assumptions** (discount rate, project life) before computing NPV.
- **Diagrams must be labeled** — boxes and arrows both.
- **Pitfall to avoid:** Including only license cost, ignoring training/support/maintenance.
- **Real-world link:** Cloud/SaaS is now the *dominant* acquisition mode for SMEs; prepare a 2-line justification.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEADT523 - INFORMATION SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Introduction # 📘 Module 4 — Introduction to Enterprise Systems ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 📌 Formal Academic Definition In the context of **PEADT523 – Information Systems (KTU 2024 Scheme)**, Module 4 introduces the student to **Enterprise Systems (ES)** — a unified, organization-wide class of information systems that integrate and automate core business processes across functional areas, enabling the seamless flow of data, decisions, and operations between internal departments and external stakeholders. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim Syllabus Framing):** > *Enterprise Systems are cross-functional, process-oriented, integrated software platforms that capture and share data across all functional areas of a business (Finance, HR, Operations, Supply Chain, Sales, CRM) through a common, centralized database, thereby supporting enterprise-wide process automation, real-time decision-making, and strategic coordination.* The "Introduction" topic specifically establishes the **conceptual foundation** required before diving into individual enterprise systems such as **ERP (Enterprise Resource Planning), SCM (Supply Chain Management Systems), and CRM (Customer Relationship Management Systems)**. --- ### 🧠 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > **Think of a multinational hospital chain.** > > Imagine a hospital group with three wings — **Out-Patient (Sales/CRM)**, **In-Patient Pharmacy (Operations/SCM)**, and **Accounts & Billing (Finance/ERP)**. Before enterprise systems, the receptionist's register (in Out-Patient) had no link to the pharmacy's stock register (In-Patient) and the cashier's ledger (Accounts). A patient could buy medicine, but no one would know — the stock wouldn't update, the bill wouldn't generate, and the patient's history wouldn't be recorded. > > **Enterprise Systems act like a single, central nervous system** that wires all three wings together. When a prescription is entered, the system: > 1. Deducts stock from the pharmacy > 2. Generates an automatic bill > 3. Updates the patient's lifelong medical record > 4. Sends an SMS reminder for follow-up > > **This is exactly what an ERP/SCM/CRM integration does in a business** — replaces isolated "silos" with one unified digital brain. --- ### 🔑 The Four Pillars of an Enterprise System (Core Building Blocks) | Pillar | What It Means | Real-World Role | |---|---|---| | **Integrated Database** | One single source of truth for the entire firm | Replaces dozens of disconnected spreadsheets | | **Process Automation** | Business workflows (procure-to-pay, order-to-cash) are pre-built | A purchase order auto-triggers a goods receipt | | **Real-Time Reporting** | Dashboards reflect current business state in seconds | Manager sees live inventory vs static weekly reports | | **Cross-Functional Scope** | Spans every department, not just one | HR data, finance data, supply data all linked | > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Highlight Box:** > The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes that the value of an enterprise system is **NOT in the software itself**, but in the **business process re-engineering (BPR)** and the **standardization of data** that the software forces upon the organization. ### 🏗️ Why Module 4 Matters in the KTU Curriculum By the end of this module, a B.Tech student is expected to (as per Course Outcomes CO4 / CO5): - Identify the components of an enterprise system. - Differentiate between **ERP, SCM, CRM, and KMS**. - Analyze how information systems provide **strategic, tactical, and operational** value. - Evaluate the role of enterprise systems in **digital transformation**. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet ## 2.1 The Conceptual Foundation — "Why Enterprise Systems?" Traditional organizations historically operated using **functional silos** (also called *islands of automation*). Each department owned its own software: - **Finance** → Tally / Legacy GL systems - **Inventory** → Standalone warehouse software - **Sales** → Excel sheets or local CRM - **HR** → Separate payroll software This created three well-documented problems: > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Three Pain Points of Siloed Systems (High-Yield for KTU 14-Mark Answers):** > 1. **Data Inconsistency** — The same product had different prices in different modules. > 2. **Process Latency** — A sales order to a delivery required manual re-keying across 4 systems. > 3. **No Enterprise-Wide Visibility** — The CEO had no consolidated view; reports took weeks. Enterprise systems **eliminate these three pain points** by enforcing a single shared database and pre-defined cross-functional processes. --- ## 2.2 The Three Layers of an Information System (Laudon & Laudon Framework) Every Information System (whether enterprise or departmental) operates on three layers. This is a **frequently tested concept** in KTU exams: | Layer | Function | Examples in Enterprise Context | |---|---|---| | **Operational Level** (Transaction Processing) | Handles day-to-day repetitive tasks | Sales order entry, payroll processing | | **Knowledge Level** (Knowledge Management) | Supports workers in creating / sharing knowledge | CAD tools, office suites, KMS | | **Management Level** (Decision Support) | Supports monitoring, control, decision-making | MIS reports, dashboards, DSS | | **Strategic Level** (Executive Information) | Long-range planning, "what-if" analysis | EIS, executive dashboards, strategic EIS | > **Mnemonic for KTU:** **"OK-MS"** → **O**perational → **K**nowledge → **M**anagement → **S**trategic (bottom-up). --- ## 2.3 Classification of Enterprise Information Systems (The Big Picture) Enterprise Systems broadly fall into **four families**, which KTU tests heavily: | System | Full Form | Core Purpose | KTU Buzzword | |---|---|---|---| | **ERP** | Enterprise Resource Planning | Internal process integration (Finance + HR + Operations) | "Backbone" | | **SCM** | Supply Chain Management | Upstream/downstream supplier & logistics integration | "Extended enterprise" | | **CRM** | Customer Relationship Management | Front-end customer & sales process automation | "Customer-facing" | | **KMS** | Knowledge Management System | Captures, stores, shares organizational knowledge | "Intellectual capital" | --- ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield for Numerical / Analytical Questions) > [!NOTE] > Although Module 4 is largely conceptual, KTU frequently asks **analytical / numerical** questions on enterprise system performance. The following are the standard, mathematically rigorous metrics you must memorize. $$ \begin{aligned} \textbf{1. Return on Investment (ROI) of an Enterprise System:} \quad & \text{ROI}_{ES} = \frac{\big( \text{Quantifiable Benefits} - \text{Total Cost of Ownership} \big)}{\text{Total Cost of Ownership}} \times 100 \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \textbf{2. Total Cost of Ownership (TCO):} \quad & \text{TCO} = C_{\text{HW}} + C_{\text{SW}} + C_{\text{Impl}} + C_{\text{Training}} + C_{\text{Maint}} + C_{\text{Downtime}} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \textbf{3. Payback Period (PP):} \quad & \text{PP} = \frac{\text{Total Implementation Cost}}{\text{Annual Net Cash Benefit}} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \textbf{4. Process Efficiency Gain (PEG):} \quad & \text{PEG} = \frac{T_{\text{Before}} - T_{\text{After}}}{T_{\text{Before}}} \times 100 \quad (\text{expressed as } \%) \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \textbf{5. Data Integration Index (DII):} \quad & \text{DII} = \frac{N_{\text{Integrated Data Fields}}}{N_{\text{Total Data Fields}}} \times 100 \end{aligned} $$ > **Units & Conventions Used in KTU 2024 Board Exams:** > - All monetary values in **₹ Crore** or **$ Million** unless specified. > - All time-based efficiency metrics expressed in **percentage (%)**. > - All ROI values > **0%** indicate profitable investment; < **0%** indicates a loss-making enterprise system. --- ## 2.5 Real-World Utility of This Module | Industry | Enterprise System Role | |---|---| | **Manufacturing** | ERP + SCM coordinate the entire production line, from raw material procurement to finished goods dispatch | | **Banking** | CRM tracks customer portfolios, KMS captures regulatory knowledge, ERP handles compliance reporting | | **Healthcare** | Integrated HIS (Hospital Information System) unifies patient records, billing, pharmacy, and lab | | **Retail** | SCM optimizes inventory across stores, CRM personalizes offers, ERP handles accounting | | **E-Governance** | Enterprise systems integrate citizen services, grievance redressal, and inter-departmental data sharing | --- # 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Framework & Worked Example Since Module 4 (Introduction) is primarily conceptual, KTU examiners typically pose **analytical / comparison-based 14-mark questions** rather than pure derivations. Below is a fully worked-out analytical problem that mirrors actual KTU Board paper patterns. --- ## 📊 Worked Example 1: TCO and ROI Calculation of an Enterprise System (KTU Board Style) ### **Problem Statement:** > A KTU-affiliated engineering college plans to implement an integrated Enterprise Resource Planning (ERP) system for automating admissions, fee collection, attendance, payroll, and examination workflows. The following cost data is provided: > > - Hardware Cost: **₹ 50 Lakh** > - Software Licensing (ERP suite): **₹ 80 Lakh** > - Implementation & Customization: **₹ 40 Lakh** > - Training Cost (Faculty + Staff): **₹ 20 Lakh** > - Annual Maintenance (per year): **₹ 15 Lakh** > - Estimated Annual Downtime Cost: **₹ 5 Lakh** > > The projected annual tangible benefits are: > - Reduction in manual labor: **₹ 60 Lakh/year** > - Error reduction & faster reporting: **₹ 25 Lakh/year** > > Calculate the **Total Cost of Ownership (TCO) over 5 years**, the **5-year ROI**, and the **Payback Period**. --- ### **Step-by-Step Solution:** #### **Step 1: Identify all cost components clearly.** The KTU standard formula for TCO over **N** years is: $$ \begin{aligned} & \text{TCO} = C_{\text{HW}} + C_{\text{SW}} + C_{\text{Impl}} + C_{\text{Training}} + N \times C_{\text{Annual Maint}} + N \times C_{\text{Downtime}} \end{aligned} $$ **Substitution of Given Values:** | Component | Value (₹ Lakh) | |---|---| | $C_{\text{HW}}$ | 50 | | $C_{\text{SW}}$ | 80 | | $C_{\text{Impl}}$ | 40 | | $C_{\text{Training}}$ | 20 | | $C_{\text{Annual Maint}}$ | 15 | | $C_{\text{Downtime}}$ | 5 | | $N$ (years) | 5 | #### **Step 2: Compute the one-time (upfront) costs.** $$ \begin{aligned} C_{\text{One-Time}} = C_{\text{HW}} + C_{\text{SW}} + C_{\text{Impl}} + C_{\text{Training}} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} C_{\text{One-Time}} = 50 + 80 + 40 + 20 = \mathbf{190 \text{ Lakh}} \end{aligned} $$ #### **Step 3: Compute the recurring costs over 5 years.** $$ \begin{aligned} C_{\text{Recurring, 5yr}} = N \times (C_{\text{Annual Maint}} + C_{\text{Downtime}}) \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} C_{\text{Recurring, 5yr}} = 5 \times (15 + 5) = 5 \times 20 = \mathbf{100 \text{ Lakh}} \end{aligned} $$ #### **Step 4: Sum to obtain TCO over 5 years.** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{TCO}_{5yr} = C_{\text{One-Time}} + C_{\text{Recurring, 5yr}} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \text{TCO}_{5yr} = 190 + 100 = \mathbf{290 \text{ Lakh}} \end{aligned} $$ #### **Step 5: Compute the total benefits over 5 years.** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Benefit}_{\text{Annual}} = 60 + 25 = \mathbf{85 \text{ Lakh/year}} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Benefit}_{5yr} = 5 \times 85 = \mathbf{425 \text{ Lakh}} \end{aligned} $$ #### **Step 6: Compute Net Benefit and ROI over 5 years.** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Net Benefit}_{5yr} = \text{Benefit}_{5yr} - \text{TCO}_{5yr} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Net Benefit}_{5yr} = 425 - 290 = \mathbf{135 \text{ Lakh}} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \text{ROI}_{5yr} = \frac{\text{Net Benefit}_{5yr}}{\text{TCO}_{5yr}} \times 100 \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \text{ROI}_{5yr} = \frac{135}{290} \times 100 = \mathbf{46.55\%} \end{aligned} $$ #### **Step 7: Compute the Payback Period (PP).** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{PP} = \frac{C_{\text{One-Time}}}{\text{Net Annual Cash Benefit}} \end{aligned} $$ The **net annual cash benefit** is the annual benefit minus annual recurring costs: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Net Annual Cash Benefit} = 85 - (15 + 5) = 85 - 20 = \mathbf{65 \text{ Lakh/year}} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \text{PP} = \frac{190}{65} = \mathbf{2.92 \text{ years}} \end{aligned} $$ --- ### ✅ **Final Answer Summary:** | Metric | Value | Interpretation | |---|---|---| | **TCO (5 years)** | **₹ 290 Lakh** | Total lifecycle cost | | **5-Year Net Benefit** | **₹ 135 Lakh** | Net positive gain | | **5-Year ROI** | **≈ 46.55%** | Profitable investment (> 0% ✅) | | **Payback Period** | **≈ 2.92 years** | Recovers cost in less than 3 years | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Examiner's Insight:** A ROI > **40%** over 5 years is considered a **highly successful** enterprise system implementation in board evaluation. A payback period under **3 years** is "excellent" rating. --- ## 📊 Worked Example 2: Comparative Analytical Matrix (Conceptual 14-Mark Style) > **Problem:** Compare and contrast **ERP, SCM, and CRM** along the dimensions: Primary Focus, Functional Scope, Key Modules, Target User, and Example Software. (Typical KTU 14-mark "Compare & Justify" question.) | Dimension | **ERP** | **SCM** | **CRM** | |---|---|---|---| | **Primary Focus** | Internal process integration | External supplier–logistics chain | Customer-facing sales & service | | **Functional Scope** | Finance, HR, Production, Inventory | Procurement, Logistics, Warehousing | Sales, Marketing, Helpdesk | | **Key Modules** | FICO, MM, PP, HRM | Demand Planning, Warehouse Mgmt | Sales Force Automation, Service | | **Target User** | Internal departments (back-office) | Suppliers, distributors, logistics | Customers, sales team, marketing | | **Example Software** | SAP S/4HANA, Oracle ERP Cloud | SAP SCM, JDA, Manhattan | Salesforce, Zoho CRM, HubSpot | | **Strategic Goal** | Operational excellence | Cost + speed advantage | Customer intimacy | > **One-line summary for KTU answer writing:** > *ERP unifies the company's **inside**, SCM stretches it **upstream/downstream**, and CRM connects it **outward to customers**.* --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Enterprise System Architecture — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow > The diagram below illustrates how a typical **Enterprise System** integrates functional departments through a central **Shared Database** and how it interfaces with external stakeholders via **SCM** and **CRM** modules. ```mermaid flowchart TB node1["EXTERNAL
CUSTOMERS"] --> nodeCRM node2["EXTERNAL
SUPPLIERS"] --> nodeSCM node3["EXTERNAL
LOGISTICS
PARTNERS"] --> nodeSCM subgraph nodeCORE["ENTERPRISE SYSTEM CORE PLATFORM"] nodeCRM["CRM MODULE
Sales, Service,
Marketing"] nodeSCM["SCM MODULE
Procurement,
Logistics, Warehouse"] nodeERP["ERP MODULE
Finance, HR,
Production, Inventory"] nodeKMS["KMS MODULE
Documents,
Best Practices, Expertise"] end nodeDB["CENTRAL SHARED
INTEGRATED DATABASE
Single Source of Truth"] nodeCRM --- nodeDB nodeSCM --- nodeDB nodeERP --- nodeDB nodeKMS --- nodeDB nodeDB --> nodeDSS["MIS / DSS / EIS
Decision Support
Dashboards and Reports"] nodeDSS --> nodeMGT["TOP MANAGEMENT
Strategic Decisions"] style nodeCORE fill:#e1f0ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:2px style nodeDB fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc9900,stroke-width:3px style nodeDSS fill:#e6f7e6,stroke:#009933,stroke-width:2px style nodeMGT fill:#ffe6e6,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:2px ``` --- ## 4.2 The Three-Layer Information System Hierarchy (Conceptual Map) > The next diagram presents the **Strategic – Management – Knowledge – Operational** layer model that KTU examiners frequently draw in 14-mark answers. ```mermaid flowchart TB nodeA["STRATEGIC LAYER
Executive Information Systems
Long-range planning and What-If analysis"] nodeB["MANAGEMENT LAYER
DSS and MIS
Monitoring, controlling, decision-making"] nodeC["KNOWLEDGE LAYER
KMS, Office Tools, CAD, Collaboration
Creating and sharing organizational knowledge"] nodeD["OPERATIONAL LAYER
TPS - Transaction Processing Systems
Day to day repetitive transactions"] nodeA --> nodeB nodeB --> nodeC nodeC --> nodeD style nodeA fill:#ffe6e6,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:2px style nodeB fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc9900,stroke-width:2px style nodeC fill:#e1f0ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:2px style nodeD fill:#e6f7e6,stroke:#009933,stroke-width:2px ``` --- ## 4.3 Before vs. After Enterprise System (Silo vs. Integrated) > This block-level flow contrasts the **as-is (siloed)** versus **to-be (integrated)** state of an organization — a frequently drawn diagram in KTU 14-mark answers. ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph nodeASIS["BEFORE: SILOED LEGACY SYSTEMS"] a1["Finance
Software A"] a2["Inventory
Software B"] a3["Sales
Software C"] a4["HR
Software D"] end a1 -. "Manual re-keying
data errors" .-> a2 a2 -. "Lag of days" .-> a3 a3 -. "No visibility" .-> a4 a1 -. "Inconsistent
reports" .-> a4 subgraph nodeTOBE["AFTER: INTEGRATED ENTERPRISE SYSTEM"] b1["ERP
Single Platform"] b2["Shared
Database"] b3["Real-time
Dashboards"] end b1 --- b2 b2 --- b3 style nodeASIS fill:#ffe6e6,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:2px style nodeTOBE fill:#e6f7e6,stroke:#009933,stroke-width:2px ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` > **Define an Enterprise System. List any TWO characteristics of an enterprise system.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** An **Enterprise System** is a cross-functional, integrated software platform that uses a **centralized database** to automate and share data across all functional areas of an organization (Finance, HR, Operations, Sales, Supply Chain). **Two characteristics (1.5 marks each):** 1. **Centralized Shared Database:** All modules access a single source of truth, eliminating data redundancy. 2. **Process-Oriented:** It enforces best-practice business processes (e.g., procure-to-pay, order-to-cash) across the organization, enabling real-time transaction processing and reporting. > *Alternate acceptable characteristics: cross-functional scope, real-time analytics, modular architecture, scalability.* --- ### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – June 2023]` > **Distinguish between Transaction Processing System (TPS) and Management Information System (MIS).** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** | Basis | **TPS** | **MIS** | |---|---|---| | **Purpose** | Records day-to-day business transactions | Produces summarized reports for managerial decisions | | **Data Type** | Detailed, raw, transactional | Summarized, aggregated, comparative | | **User** | Operational staff (clerks, cashiers) | Middle managers | | **Output** | Transaction receipts, logs | Periodic reports, exception reports | | **Frequency** | Real-time, continuous | Daily / weekly / monthly | | **Decision Level** | Operational | Tactical / management | > *The key differentiator is: TPS handles the **doing**; MIS handles the **monitoring and review** of what is being done.* --- ## 📝 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > *Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, answer **ONE** of the two choices.* --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`** > **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **three-tier architecture of an Enterprise Information System** with a neat block diagram. Differentiate between the **Strategic, Management, and Operational** levels of decision-making. > > **(b) [7 Marks]** Compare and contrast **ERP, SCM, and CRM** systems in terms of scope, focus, modules, and target users. Give **one real-world software example** for each. --- #### ✅ Model Solution for Q.A (a) [7 Marks] **1. Three-Tier Architecture of an Enterprise IS (4 marks):** An Enterprise Information System is typically organized into three tiers: - **Tier 1 — Presentation Layer (User Interface):** The web/UI through which employees, managers, and customers interact with the system. *Examples: SAP Fiori, Salesforce Lightning.* - **Tier 2 — Application Layer (Business Logic):** The middleware / server that processes the business rules — for example, the "Order Processing Engine" or "Inventory Replenishment Rule." - **Tier 3 — Database Layer (Data Storage):** The centralized relational database that stores every transaction in a normalized schema. *Example: SAP HANA, Oracle DB.* A neat block diagram: ``` [USERS: Employees, Managers, Customers, Suppliers] | v +--------------------------------------+ | TIER 1: PRESENTATION LAYER | | (Web UI / Mobile UI / Dashboards) | +--------------------------------------+ | v +--------------------------------------+ | TIER 2: APPLICATION LAYER | | (Business Logic / Process Engines) | +--------------------------------------+ | v +--------------------------------------+ | TIER 3: DATABASE LAYER | | (Centralized Shared Database) | +--------------------------------------+ ``` **2. Decision-Making Levels (3 marks):** | Level | Purpose | Time Horizon | IS Used | |---|---|---|---| | **Strategic** | Long-term goals, "what-if" scenarios | 3–5 years | EIS, Strategic dashboards | | **Management** | Monitoring, controlling performance | Monthly / Quarterly | MIS, DSS | | **Operational** | Day-to-day transactions | Real-time | TPS, Workflow systems | --- #### ✅ Model Solution for Q.A (b) [7 Marks] **Detailed Comparison Table (5 marks) + Examples (2 marks):** | Dimension | **ERP** | **SCM** | **CRM** | |---|---|---|---| | **Scope** | Entire organization (internal) | Extended enterprise (suppliers + logistics) | Customer-facing front-end | | **Focus** | Operational efficiency, integration | Cost minimization, speed, agility | Customer retention & acquisition | | **Key Modules** | Finance, HR, Production, Inventory | Demand planning, Warehouse mgmt, Logistics | Sales force automation, Helpdesk | | **Target User** | Internal departments | Suppliers, distributors, logistics partners | Sales team, marketing, customers | | **Real Example** | **SAP S/4HANA** | **SAP SCM / Oracle SCM** | **Salesforce CRM** | > **One-line summary for the conclusion (1 mark):** *ERP is the internal backbone, SCM is the supply-side extender, and CRM is the customer-side extender — together they form a complete enterprise system.* --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Alternative Choice]`** > **(a) [7 Marks]** What are the **major challenges** in implementing an Enterprise System in a large organization? Explain with examples. > > **(b) [7 Marks]** A manufacturing firm wants to evaluate an ERP project. Given the following data, calculate the **TCO over 4 years**, the **Net Benefit**, and the **Payback Period**. > > - Hardware: **₹ 30 Lakh** > - Software: **₹ 70 Lakh** > - Implementation: **₹ 25 Lakh** > - Training: **₹ 15 Lakh** > - Annual Maintenance: **₹ 12 Lakh** > - Annual Downtime: **₹ 3 Lakh** > - Annual Tangible Benefits: **₹ 50 Lakh** > - Project life: **4 years** --- #### ✅ Model Solution for Q.B (a) [7 Marks] **Major Challenges in Enterprise System Implementation (any 5–6 expected):** 1. **High Implementation Cost (₹ Crores):** Hardware + Software + Consulting + Training bills are massive. 2. **Resistance to Change:** Employees fear job loss; learning a new system is stressful. *Example: TCS implemented SAP globally, faced years of internal pushback.* 3. **Process Re-engineering Pain:** The ERP enforces "best-practice" processes, often conflicting with existing legacy workflows. 4. **Data Migration Issues:** Cleaning and migrating 20+ years of dirty data is risky. *Example: FoxMeyer Drug's failed SAP rollout (a classic KTU case study).* 5. **Customization vs. Standardization Dilemma:** Excessive customization increases TCO and future upgrade cost. 6. **Vendor Lock-in:** Once committed to SAP/Oracle, switching becomes prohibitively expensive. 7. **Implementation Time (12–36 months):** Long duration causes "scope creep" and budget overruns. 8. **Need for Top Management Commitment:** Without CEO sponsorship, the project fails. *(Allocate ~1 mark per well-explained challenge; 5 well-explained = 5 marks + conclusion = 2 marks.)* --- #### ✅ Model Solution for Q.B (b) [7 Marks] **Step 1 — Identify Costs.** | Cost Component | Value (₹ Lakh) | |---|---| | $C_{\text{HW}}$ | 30 | | $C_{\text{SW}}$ | 70 | | $C_{\text{Impl}}$ | 25 | | $C_{\text{Training}}$ | 15 | | $C_{\text{Annual Maint}}$ | 12 | | $C_{\text{Downtime}}$ | 3 | | $N$ (years) | 4 | **Step 2 — One-Time Cost:** $$ \begin{aligned} C_{\text{One-Time}} = 30 + 70 + 25 + 15 = \mathbf{140 \text{ Lakh}} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Recurring Cost over 4 years:** $$ \begin{aligned} C_{\text{Recurring, 4yr}} = 4 \times (12 + 3) = 4 \times 15 = \mathbf{60 \text{ Lakh}} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Total Cost of Ownership (TCO):** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{TCO}_{4yr} = 140 + 60 = \mathbf{200 \text{ Lakh}} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Total Benefits over 4 years:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Benefit}_{4yr} = 4 \times 50 = \mathbf{200 \text{ Lakh}} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 6 — Net Benefit:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Net Benefit} = 200 - 200 = \mathbf{0 \text{ Lakh}} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 7 — Payback Period:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Net Annual Cash Benefit} = 50 - 15 = \mathbf{35 \text{ Lakh/year}} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \text{PP} = \frac{140}{35} = \mathbf{4 \text{ years}} \end{aligned} $$ --- ### 📊 Final Verdict on Q.B (b): | Metric | Value | Interpretation | |---|---|---| | **TCO (4 years)** | ₹ 200 Lakh | Total lifecycle cost | | **Total Benefits (4 years)** | ₹ 200 Lakh | Breakeven point | | **Net Benefit** | ₹ 0 Lakh | **Break-even — not profitable over 4 years** | | **Payback Period** | 4 years | At the boundary of project life | > **KTU Examiner's Note:** A Net Benefit of zero means the project **just recovers its cost**, with no surplus. In board terms, this is rated as a **"Marginal Acceptance"** — the management should ideally renegotiate vendor terms or extend the project horizon to **5+ years** to make it profitable. --- > [!WARNING] > **🛑 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Confusing ERP with CRM/SCM:** Many students treat ERP, CRM, and SCM as synonyms. They are NOT — ERP is the **internal** integration system, SCM is **supply-side**, and CRM is **customer-side**. *(-2 to -3 marks deduction if confused.)* > 2. **Forgetting Annual Costs in TCO:** In numerical questions, students often include only the one-time cost. KTU always expects **TCO over the project life**, including annual maintenance and downtime. *(-2 marks if missed.)* > 3. **Wrong Formula for Payback Period:** It is **Initial Cost / Net Annual Cash Benefit**, not "Total Cost / Annual Benefit." *(-1 to -2 marks.)* > 4. **Not drawing diagrams in 14-mark answers:** A 14-mark theory answer without a **neat block diagram** typically loses **2 marks** in KTU valuation. > 5. **Skipping real-world examples:** Naming **SAP, Oracle, Salesforce** in ERP/SCM/CRM questions is mandatory for full marks. *(-1 mark if absent.)* --- ## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Save This for Last-Minute KTU Prep):** - ✅ An **Enterprise System** is a **cross-functional, integrated, real-time** information system built on a **centralized database**. - ✅ The **three primary enterprise systems** are: **ERP (internal)**, **SCM (supply-side)**, **CRM (customer-side)**. - ✅ The **four IS layers** in increasing order of decision-level are: **TPS → KMS → MIS/DSS → EIS** (Mnemonic: *OK-MS*). - ✅ **TCO** = One-time cost + (Annual Maintenance + Downtime) × Project life. - ✅ **ROI** = (Benefits − TCO) / TCO × 100. A positive ROI means the system is profitable. - ✅ **Payback Period** = One-time Cost / Net Annual Cash Benefit. Lower is better. - ✅ The **three-tier architecture** is: Presentation Layer → Application Layer → Database Layer. - ✅ **Major implementation challenges** are: high cost, change resistance, process re-engineering pain, data migration, vendor lock-in. - ✅ **Real-world software references** (must memorize for KTU): - **ERP** → SAP S/4HANA, Oracle ERP Cloud - **SCM** → SAP SCM, Manhattan Associates - **CRM** → Salesforce, Microsoft Dynamics 365 - **KMS** → Confluence, SharePoint - ✅ **Always draw at least one diagram** in any 14-mark answer (block diagram / comparison table). - ✅ **Cite real examples** (SAP, FoxMeyer case study) — KTU 2024 examiners reward industry context. - ✅ A system that achieves **TCO break-even within 3 years** is considered a **highly successful** implementation in KTU valuation. ---

topics.title: Planning for and Justifying IT Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT523 - INFORMATION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Planning for and Justifying IT Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Planning for IT Applications** is the structured managerial process of identifying, prioritizing, scheduling, and resourcing information technology initiatives that align with the strategic business objectives of an organization. It is the bridge between corporate strategic planning and the actual acquisition, development, and deployment of IT systems.

**Justifying IT Applications** is the formal, evidence-based activity of building a rational, defensible business case that demonstrates the economic, operational, strategic, and organizational value of a proposed IT investment, typically before significant financial resources are committed.

In KTU 2024 Scheme IS terminology, this activity falls within the **Planning** phase of Information Systems development and is tightly coupled with **strategic alignment** and **value creation**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme - PEADT523 Module 4):**
> This topic directly addresses the management decision-making layer above systems development. The examiner expects students to understand *why* organizations invest in IT, *how* proposals are screened, and *what* financial and non-financial criteria are used to approve or reject them.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a family planning a major home renovation. Before breaking ground, the family must:

1. **Plan** — Decide *what* to renovate, *when*, and in *what order* (kitchen first or roof first?).
2. **Budget** — Estimate total cost.
3. **Justify** — Convince themselves (and perhaps the bank) that the renovation will increase the home's value, comfort, or utility enough to warrant the expense.

**IT Planning and Justification works exactly the same way.** A company cannot simply buy the latest software because it is fashionable. It must:
- Align the IT spend with business goals (Plan),
- Rank competing projects (Prioritize),
- Prove the project will deliver measurable returns (Justify).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Planning answers *"Should we do this project, and when?"* while Justification answers *"How do we prove it is worth the money?"* Both are inseparable twins in the IT governance lifecycle.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in Justification (Bolded for Emphasis)

- **Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)** — the complete cost of acquiring, deploying, operating, and disposing of an IT asset.
- **Return on Investment (ROI)** — the percentage return derived from the IT investment.
- **Net Present Value (NPV)** — the discounted value of future cash flows minus the initial outlay.
- **Internal Rate of Return (IRR)** — the discount rate at which NPV equals zero.
- **Payback Period** — the time required to recover the initial investment from net cash inflows.
- **Break-Even Point (BEP)** — the volume of activity at which total revenue equals total cost.
- **Discount Rate / Cost of Capital (r)** — the required rate of return, often the Weighted Average Cost of Capital (**WACC**).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Standard:** Most numerical problems in this module expect students to compute NPV, ROI, Payback Period, and apply Break-Even logic. The typical **time horizon** used is **3 to 5 years**, and a common **discount rate** assumption is **10% to 12%** unless stated otherwise.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The IT Planning Process — Step-by-Step Logic

The planning process is sequential and iterative. The KTU syllabus emphasizes these stages:

1. **Strategic Alignment** — Derive IT initiatives from the organization's strategic business plan.
2. **Information Requirements Analysis** — Identify what information the business needs to execute its strategy.
3. **Project Identification & Scoping** — Generate candidate projects and define their boundaries.
4. **Feasibility Analysis** — Test each project on technical, economic, operational, legal, and schedule dimensions.
5. **Prioritization & Selection** — Rank projects using portfolio and scoring models.
6. **Resource Allocation & Scheduling** — Assign budgets, people, and time.
7. **Implementation Monitoring** — Track benefits realization post-deployment.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Planning Matters:** Unplanned IT spending is the single largest cause of project failure and cost overrun in mid-sized enterprises. Planning converts "wish-list" spending into a disciplined capital allocation process.

### 2.2 Justification Approaches — The Three Pillars

| Pillar | Question Answered | Typical Tools |
|---|---|---|
| **Financial / Economic Justification** | Is the project financially viable? | ROI, NPV, IRR, Payback, TCO |
| **Non-Financial / Operational Justification** | Will the project improve operations qualitatively? | Scoring Models, Weighted Factor Analysis, Balanced Scorecard |
| **Strategic Justification** | Does the project support long-term competitive advantage? | Porter's Five Forces, Value Chain Analysis, Strategic Alignment Matrix |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Students frequently answer justification questions with *only* financial numbers. The KTU 2024 scheme explicitly evaluates *qualitative* and *strategic* justification as well. Always mention all three pillars when asked "how do you justify an IT application?"

### 2.3 Capital Budgeting Techniques in Detail

#### (a) Payback Period Method
The simplest measure. It answers: *"How long until I recover my money?"*

**Logic Steps:**
1. Sum the annual net cash inflows.
2. Identify the year in which cumulative cash flow first equals or exceeds the initial investment.
3. Interpolate if recovery occurs mid-year.

**Advantages:** Simple, intuitive, low-risk focus.
**Disadvantages:** Ignores the **time value of money** and **post-payback cash flows**.

#### (b) Net Present Value (NPV) Method
The most rigorous method. It discounts all future cash flows back to present value at the cost of capital.

**Logic Steps:**
1. Forecast net cash flows $CF_t$ for each year $t = 0, 1, 2, \ldots, n$.
2. Choose a discount rate $r$ (cost of capital).
3. Discount each future cash flow.
4. Sum the discounted values.
5. Subtract the initial investment.

**Decision Rule:** Accept the project if NPV > 0.

#### (c) Internal Rate of Return (IRR)
The discount rate that makes NPV equal to zero. It represents the *effective* return of the project.

**Decision Rule:** Accept if IRR > Cost of Capital.

#### (d) Return on Investment (ROI)
A percentage measure of profitability.

#### (e) Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)
Captures *all* costs — direct (hardware, software, licenses) and indirect (training, downtime, support, disposal).

### 2.4 Non-Financial / Scoring Model Approach

A **Scoring Model** assigns weights to multiple criteria (strategic fit, risk, competitive advantage, regulatory compliance, etc.) and scores each project on a uniform scale (typically 1 to 5 or 1 to 10).

**Logic Steps:**
1. Identify evaluation criteria.
2. Assign weights summing to 1.0 (or 100%).
3. Score each project on every criterion.
4. Compute weighted score = $\Sigma (w_i \times s_i)$.
5. Rank projects by total weighted score.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Method | Formula | Decision Rule | Key Assumption |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Payback Period** | $\text{Payback} = \dfrac{\text{Initial Investment}}{\text{Annual Net Cash Inflow}}$ (uniform) | Shorter is better | Ignores time value of money |
| **Discounted Payback** | Find $t$ where $\sum_{t=0}^{T} \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t} \ge 0$ | Shorter is better | Considers time value of money |
| **Net Present Value (NPV)** | $\text{NPV} = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t}$ | NPV > 0: Accept | Cash flows are re-invested at $r$ |
| **Internal Rate of Return (IRR)** | $\sum_{t=0}^{n} \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+\text{IRR})^t} = 0$ | IRR > $r$: Accept | Cash flows are conventional |
| **Return on Investment (ROI)** | $\text{ROI} = \dfrac{\text{Total Benefits} - \text{Total Costs}}{\text{Total Costs}} \times 100\%$ | Higher is better | Usually expressed over project life |
| **Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)** | $\text{TCO} = \sum (\text{Direct Costs} + \text{Indirect Costs})$ over horizon | Lower is better | Captures full lifecycle cost |
| **Weighted Score (Scoring Model)** | $\text{Score} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} w_i \cdot s_i$ | Higher is better | $\sum w_i = 1$ |
| **Break-Even Point (units)** | $\text{BEP} = \dfrac{\text{Fixed Costs}}{\text{Unit Price} - \text{Variable Cost per unit}}$ | $\ge 0$ units | Linear cost-revenue model |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Formatting Note for Tables:** All absolute-value or conditional bars in the formulas above have been rendered as plain inline notation to preserve the markdown table integrity. In handwritten exams, always use proper $\vert x \vert$ or $\mid x \mid$ notation.

### 2.6 Real-World Utility

These techniques are used in:
