- **Banking & Finance** — Evaluating core banking platform upgrades.
- **Healthcare IT** — Justifying Electronic Medical Record (EMR) deployments.
- **Retail** — Planning POS, ERP, and supply chain system rollouts.
- **Manufacturing** — Justifying MES, IoT, and predictive maintenance platforms.
- **Public Sector** — Building e-governance and smart-city infrastructure business cases.

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Context:** As of the **2024 KTU syllabus refresh**, the role of *Benefits Realization* and *Total Economic Impact (TEI)* frameworks (popularized by analyst firms like Gartner and Forrester) has been quietly incorporated. Students writing about "justification" should briefly mention that benefits tracking continues **after** implementation — not just at the proposal stage.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive NPV Derivation (Multi-Period)

We start with the fundamental principle of the **time value of money**: a rupee today is worth more than a rupee tomorrow because today's rupee can be invested to earn interest.

**Step 1: Discounting a Single Future Cash Flow**

A cash flow $CF_1$ received after 1 year, discounted at rate $r$, has present value $PV_1$:

$$
PV_1 = \frac{CF_1}{(1 + r)^1}
$$

*Textual Explanation:* We divide the future cash flow by the compounding factor $(1+r)$ to remove one year of interest accumulation.

**Step 2: Discounting a Cash Flow Received After $t$ Years**

Generalizing for year $t$:

$$
PV_t = \frac{CF_t}{(1 + r)^t}
$$

*Textual Explanation:* Each additional year of waiting introduces another factor of $(1+r)$ in the denominator, reducing present value.

**Step 3: Summing All Discounted Cash Flows (n-Year Horizon)**

The total present value of all future cash flows from $t=1$ to $n$:

$$
PV_{\text{total}} = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{CF_t}{(1 + r)^t}
$$

*Textual Explanation:* We add the present values of every future cash flow. Each term is independent and can be computed separately.

**Step 4: Subtracting the Initial Investment at $t=0$**

The initial investment occurs at $t=0$ and is already at present value (no discounting needed):

$$
\text{NPV} = -I_0 + \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{CF_t}{(1 + r)^t}
$$

*Textual Explanation:* We treat the initial investment as a negative cash flow at $t=0$ and combine it with the sum of discounted future inflows.

**Step 5: Compactly Written Form (Including $t=0$ Term)**

Since $\dfrac{CF_0}{(1+r)^0} = CF_0$, the compact form is:

$$
\boxed{\text{NPV} = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \frac{CF_t}{(1 + r)^t}}
$$

*Textual Explanation:* This single expression captures the entire NPV calculation from year zero through year $n$, where $CF_0$ is conventionally negative (the initial outlay).

**Step 6: Decision Criterion**

$$
\text{If NPV} > 0 \implies \text{Project is financially viable.}
$$

$$
\text{If NPV} = 0 \implies \text{Indifferent.}
$$

$$
\text{If NPV} < 0 \implies \text{Reject the project.}
$$

---

### 3.2 Worked Example: NPV of a 3-Year IT Project

**Problem Statement:** A company is evaluating an IT project with the following data. The initial investment is **Rs. 5,00,000**. Net cash inflows are:
- Year 1: **Rs. 2,00,000**
- Year 2: **Rs. 2,50,000**
- Year 3: **Rs. 1,80,000**

The discount rate (cost of capital) is **10%** per annum. Compute the NPV and advise.

**Step 1: Write down all cash flows.**

$$
CF_0 = -5{,}00{,}000, \quad CF_1 = 2{,}00{,}000, \quad CF_2 = 2{,}50{,}000, \quad CF_3 = 1{,}80{,}000
$$

**Step 2: Compute the discount factor for each year.**

$$
(1 + 0.10)^1 = 1.1000
$$

$$
(1 + 0.10)^2 = 1.2100
$$

$$
(1 + 0.10)^3 = 1.3310
$$

**Step 3: Discount each year's cash flow.**

$$
PV_1 = \frac{2{,}00{,}000}{1.1000} = 1{,}81{,}818.18
$$

$$
PV_2 = \frac{2{,}50{,}000}{1.2100} = 2{,}06{,}611.57
$$

$$
PV_3 = \frac{1{,}80{,}000}{1.3310} = 1{,}35{,}236.66
$$

**Step 4: Sum the discounted cash flows.**

$$
\sum PV = 1{,}81{,}818.18 + 2{,}06{,}611.57 + 1{,}35{,}236.66 = 5{,}23{,}666.41
$$

**Step 5: Subtract the initial investment.**

$$
\text{NPV} = 5{,}23{,}666.41 - 5{,}00{,}000 = 23{,}666.41
$$

**Step 6: Decision.**

$$
\text{NPV} = + \text{Rs. } 23{,}666.41 > 0
$$

*Conclusion:* The project is financially acceptable. It generates a positive value above the cost of capital.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Justification Methods

| Method | Considers Time Value of Money | Considers All Cash Flows | Ease of Computation | Use of Non-Financial Factors | KTU Exam Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Payback Period** | No (unless discounted) | No | Very High | No | Very High |
| **Discounted Payback** | Yes | No | Medium | No | Medium |
| **NPV** | Yes | Yes | Medium | No | Very High |
| **IRR** | Yes | Yes | Medium (iterative) | No | High |
| **ROI** | Optional | Optional | High | No | High |
| **TCO** | Optional | Yes (cost only) | Medium | No | Medium |
| **Scoring Model** | N/A | N/A | High | Yes | High |
| **Break-Even Analysis** | No | No | High | No | Medium |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Pattern:** KTU 2024 papers typically combine a *numerical* Part B question (NPV / Payback computation worth 7 marks) with a *descriptive* Part B question (discussing limitations + scoring model worth 7 marks). Always prepare both.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The IT Planning & Justification Lifecycle (Mermaid Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Corporate Strategic Plan] --> A1[Derive IT Strategic Goals]
    A1 --> A2[Identify Information Requirements]
    A2 --> A3[Generate Candidate IT Projects]
    A3 --> A4{Feasibility Analysis}
    A4 --> A5[Technical Feasibility]
    A4 --> A6[Economic Feasibility]
    A4 --> A7[Operational Feasibility]
    A4 --> A8[Legal and Schedule Feasibility]
    A5 --> A9[Consolidated Feasibility Report]
    A6 --> A9
    A7 --> A9
    A8 --> A9
    A9 --> A10{Prioritize Using Scoring Model}
    A10 --> A11[Build Business Case with NPV, IRR, ROI, TCO]
    A11 --> A12{Investment Committee Review}
    A12 -->|Approved| A13[Allocate Budget and Resources]
    A12 -->|Rejected| A14[Defer or Cancel Project]
    A13 --> A15[Implementation and Benefits Realization]
    A15 --> A16[Post Implementation Review]
    A16 --> A1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The arrow from **A16 (Post Implementation Review)** back to **A1 (Derive IT Strategic Goals)** signifies the **feedback loop** — the heart of continuous IT governance. It is a common 2-mark question in KTU exams.

---

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Justification Framework

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B1[IT Project Proposal] --> B2[Data Collection Layer]
    B2 --> B3[Cost Inputs]
    B2 --> B4[Benefit Inputs]
    B2 --> B5[Risk Inputs]
    B3 --> B6[Financial Analysis Engine]
    B4 --> B6
    B5 --> B7[Risk and Scoring Engine]
    B6 --> B8[Output: NPV, IRR, ROI, Payback, TCO]
    B7 --> B9[Output: Weighted Score and Risk Rating]
    B8 --> B10[Decision Support Dashboard]
    B9 --> B10
    B10 --> B11{Decision}
    B11 -->|Approve| B12[Proceed to Implementation]
    B11 -->|Defer| B13[Re-prioritize in Next Cycle]
    B11 -->|Reject| B14[Archive with Lessons Learned]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this block diagram?** KTU students often confuse *inputs* with *outputs* in the justification process. This diagram makes it explicit that **Cost, Benefit, and Risk** are the *three primary data feeds*, and **Financial Metrics** + **Non-Financial Scores** are the *two output streams* that converge at the decision dashboard.

---

### 4.3 The Three Pillars of IT Justification (Mermaid Concept Map)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C0[IT Project Justification] --> C1[Financial Justification]
    C0 --> C2[Non-Financial Justification]
    C0 --> C3[Strategic Justification]
    C1 --> D1[NPV]
    C1 --> D2[IRR]
    C1 --> D3[Payback Period]
    C1 --> D4[ROI]
    C1 --> D5[TCO]
    C2 --> E1[Scoring Models]
    C2 --> E2[Weighted Factor Analysis]
    C2 --> E3[Balanced Scorecard]
    C3 --> F1[Porter Five Forces]
    C3 --> F2[Value Chain Analysis]
    C3 --> F3[Strategic Alignment Matrix]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper Style]**

**Q1. Define Total Cost of Ownership (TCO) and list any four cost components included in it.**

**Model Answer:**
Total Cost of Ownership (TCO) is a comprehensive financial estimate intended to help managers assess the direct and indirect costs of an information system across its entire lifecycle — from acquisition to disposal. The four major cost components are:
1. **Acquisition costs** — hardware, software, and licensing.
2. **Implementation costs** — installation, customization, and data migration.
3. **Operating costs** — maintenance, support, training, and upgrades.
4. **End-of-life costs** — decommissioning, data sanitization, and disposal.

---

**Q2. Differentiate between NPV and IRR.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | NPV | IRR |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Present value of net cash flows minus initial investment | Discount rate that makes NPV = 0 |
| **Output** | Absolute monetary value (Rs.) | Percentage rate (%) |
| **Decision Rule** | NPV > 0: Accept | IRR > Cost of Capital: Accept |
| **Mutually Exclusive Projects** | NPV is more reliable | IRR can give misleading rankings |
| **Computation** | Direct summation | Requires iterative/financial-calculator solution |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Descriptive / Numerical Questions (14 Marks)

> **Internal Choice Pattern:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Each sub-part carries 7 marks.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Numerical + Conceptual

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Pattern]**

A company is evaluating a proposed Customer Relationship Management (CRM) software investment. The initial cost is **Rs. 8,00,000**. The expected net cash inflows over a 4-year horizon are: Year 1 — Rs. 3,00,000; Year 2 — Rs. 3,50,000; Year 3 — Rs. 2,50,000; Year 4 — Rs. 1,50,000. The cost of capital is **12%** per annum.

**(a)** Compute the **Net Present Value (NPV)** and the **Payback Period** of the project. Advise whether the project should be accepted. (7 marks)

**(b)** Discuss the **limitations of the Payback Period method** and explain how the **Scoring Model** can complement financial analysis in justifying IT applications. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Step 1: Compute discount factors.**

$$
(1.12)^1 = 1.1200, \quad (1.12)^2 = 1.2544, \quad (1.12)^3 = 1.4049, \quad (1.12)^4 = 1.5735
$$

**Step 2: Discount each cash flow.**

$$
PV_1 = \frac{3{,}00{,}000}{1.1200} = 2{,}67{,}857.14
$$

$$
PV_2 = \frac{3{,}50{,}000}{1.2544} = 2{,}79{,}018.49
$$

$$
PV_3 = \frac{2{,}50{,}000}{1.4049} = 1{,}77{,}955.16
$$

$$
PV_4 = \frac{1{,}50{,}000}{1.5735} = 95{,}329.68
$$

**Step 3: Sum discounted cash flows.**

$$
\sum PV = 2{,}67{,}857.14 + 2{,}79{,}018.49 + 1{,}77{,}955.16 + 95{,}329.68 = 8{,}20{,}160.47
$$

**Step 4: Compute NPV.**

$$
\text{NPV} = 8{,}20{,}160.47 - 8{,}00{,}000 = +20{,}160.47
$$

**Step 5: Compute Payback Period (undiscounted).**

Cumulative cash flow:
- End of Year 1: Rs. 3,00,000
- End of Year 2: Rs. 6,50,000
- End of Year 3: Rs. 9,00,000 (investment recovered)

Investment = Rs. 8,00,000. At the end of Year 2, Rs. 6,50,000 is recovered; Rs. 1,50,000 remains.

$$
\text{Payback} = 2 + \frac{1{,}50{,}000}{2{,}50{,}000} = 2 + 0.60 = 2.60 \text{ years}
$$

**Step 6: Decision.**

Since NPV = +Rs. 20,160.47 > 0, the project is **financially acceptable**. The payback occurs within 3 years, adding confidence.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Limitations of Payback Period:**
1. **Ignores time value of money** — treats all cash flows as equal in weight.
2. **Ignores post-payback cash flows** — a project with huge Year 4–5 benefits may be rejected.
3. **No measure of profitability** — only measures speed of recovery.
4. **Biased toward short-term, low-risk projects.**
5. **No consideration of strategic or qualitative benefits.**

**How Scoring Model Complements Financial Analysis:**
1. Incorporates **non-financial criteria** like strategic fit, regulatory compliance, customer satisfaction, employee productivity.
2. Uses **weighted criteria** to reflect organizational priorities.
3. Enables **comparison across heterogeneous projects** (e.g., IT vs. marketing projects) that pure NPV cannot compare directly.
4. Provides a **transparent, auditable decision trail** for governance.
5. Captures **risk and strategic alignment**, which pure financial methods miss.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Conceptual + Strategic

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the **three pillars of IT application justification** (Financial, Non-Financial, Strategic) with suitable examples. (7 marks)

**(b)** Describe the **IT Planning Process** in detail. How does strategic alignment ensure that IT investments deliver real business value? (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**1. Financial Justification:**
Uses monetary metrics. Example: An ERP project is justified using NPV (positive), IRR (15% > 12% WACC), and Payback Period (2.5 years).

**2. Non-Financial Justification:**
Uses qualitative and operational criteria. Example: A new HR portal is scored on criteria such as "Employee Self-Service Adoption (weight 30%)", "Reduction in Paper Usage (weight 20%)", and "Compliance with Data Privacy Laws (weight 50%)".

**3. Strategic Justification:**
Links IT to competitive advantage. Example: A bank invests in mobile banking not because the immediate NPV is huge, but because losing mobile market share would erode long-term competitive position. Porter's Five Forces and Value Chain Analysis are typical tools.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**IT Planning Process Steps:**
1. **Strategic Alignment** — Review corporate strategy; derive IT goals.
2. **Information Requirements Analysis** — Identify what data and systems are needed.
3. **Project Identification** — Brainstorm candidate projects.
4. **Feasibility Analysis** — Screen each project on technical, economic, operational, legal, and schedule dimensions.
5. **Prioritization** — Use scoring models, portfolio analysis, and strategic weighting.
6. **Resource Allocation** — Assign budget, staff, and timeline.
7. **Implementation and Monitoring** — Track benefits realization.
8. **Feedback Loop** — Lessons feed back into the next planning cycle.

**Strategic Alignment Ensuring Real Business Value:**
- Prevents **technology-first** thinking that delivers shiny but useless systems.
- Ensures every IT project is traceable to a **business goal** (revenue growth, cost reduction, customer retention, regulatory compliance).
- Enables **portfolio balancing** — quick-win tactical projects alongside transformational strategic ones.
- Improves **board-level buy-in** because IT spend is justified in business language.
- Reduces **project failure rates** by eliminating misaligned initiatives early.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Formula-only answers fetch 0–2 marks.** Always show *plugged-in numerical values* and *final numbers*. A naked NPV formula with no calculation = 1 mark.
> 2. **Cash flow sign convention:** Initial investment is **negative** in NPV. Students frequently forget the minus sign and lose 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Payback interpolation:** When payback is mid-year, *you must show the interpolation step* (e.g., 2 + 1,50,000/2,50,000 = 2.6 years). Just writing "2.6" is insufficient.
> 4. **Do not confuse ROI and IRR.** ROI is a *percentage return over the project life*; IRR is the *discount rate* at which NPV = 0. They are NOT the same.
> 5. **Discount rate must be stated explicitly.** Examiner will not infer it. Always write "r = 10% p.a." at the start of any NPV problem.
> 6. **Always mention both financial AND non-financial justification** in descriptive questions. Pure financial answers lose up to 3 marks in 14-mark questions.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Planning** is the process of *deciding what IT to build, when, and with what resources*.
- **Justification** is the process of *proving the IT investment is worth the cost*.
- The **three pillars** of justification are **Financial**, **Non-Financial**, and **Strategic**.
- The five core financial metrics are **NPV, IRR, ROI, Payback Period, and TCO**.
- **NPV is the gold standard** — it considers the time value of money and all future cash flows.
- **Decision rule for NPV:** Accept if NPV > 0.
- **Decision rule for IRR:** Accept if IRR > Cost of Capital.
- **Payback Period** is simple but ignores time value and post-payback flows.
- **TCO** captures *all* direct + indirect costs across the system lifecycle.
- **Scoring Models** are used when non-financial / qualitative factors dominate.
- The **planning process** must always start with the **corporate strategic plan**.
- **Feasibility Analysis** has five dimensions: Technical, Economic, Operational, Legal, and Schedule (TEOLS).
- **Benefits Realization** continues *after* implementation — not just during the proposal.
- A good **Business Case** combines all three pillars: numbers + scores + strategic narrative.
- Always show **cash flow tables**, **discount factors**, and **step-by-step interpolation** in numerical answers.
- **Strategic alignment** is the single most cited reason for IT project success or failure in IS literature.
- The **feedback loop** from post-implementation review to strategic planning is what makes IT governance *continuous* rather than *one-off*.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Strategies for Acquiring IT Applications
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT523 - INFORMATION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Strategies for Acquiring IT Applications

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**IT Application Acquisition Strategy** refers to the structured, organization-wide decision-making framework that businesses employ to obtain, procure, build, lease, or otherwise integrate information technology (IT) application software and systems required to support their operational, tactical, and strategic business processes. As defined in the **KTU 2024 Scheme** syllabus for *Information Systems*, this concept belongs to the domain of **IT Governance and IT Sourcing Strategy** and forms a critical sub-component of the *Enterprise Resource Planning (ERP)* and *IT Infrastructure* knowledge blocks.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:**  
> *An acquisition strategy defines the methodology, sourcing model, and governance structure adopted by an enterprise to obtain software applications, balancing factors such as Total Cost of Ownership (TCO), strategic alignment, time-to-market, in-house capability, vendor risk, and long-term flexibility.*

## 1.2 The Six Primary Acquisition Pathways

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, acquisition strategies are formally classified into **six distinct pathways**:

1. **Purchasing Off-the-Shelf (OTS) Applications** — Buying pre-built, commercial, ready-to-deploy software.
2. **Custom In-House Development** — Building applications internally using the organization's own developers.
3. **Outsourcing (Domestic & Off-shore)** — Contracting an external third-party vendor to develop or maintain applications.
4. **Software as a Service (SaaS) / Cloud Subscription** — Renting access to vendor-hosted applications on a subscription basis.
5. **End-User Development (EUD)** — Allowing non-IT business users to build or modify their own applications.
6. **Hybrid / Best-of-Breed Approach** — Combining multiple acquisition pathways to create a unified solution.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "House Building" Metaphor

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Think of IT acquisition like building your dream home:**

| IT Acquisition Strategy | Real-World Analogy (Home Building) |
|---|---|
| Off-the-Shelf (COTS) | Buying a ready-made flat from a developer — quick, standard, affordable. |
| Custom In-House Development | Hiring your own architects and masons to build a bespoke villa — perfect fit, but slow and expensive. |
| Outsourcing | Contracting a turnkey construction company — they handle everything, you supervise. |
| SaaS / Cloud Subscription | Renting a fully-furnished serviced apartment — pay monthly, no maintenance headache. |
| End-User Development | Letting your family members decorate rooms themselves — fast, but quality varies. |
| Hybrid Approach | Buying a flat but renovating interiors and adding a custom kitchen — strategic mix. |

> [!TIP]
> **Student Takeaway:** The "best" strategy does **not** exist universally. The optimal choice depends on **strategic importance, budget, time pressure, in-house skill, and risk tolerance** — concepts formally known in KTU modules as the *Five Sourcing Determinants*.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Seven Decision Variables Governing Acquisition Choice

When an enterprise evaluates *how* to acquire an IT application, KTU 2024 curriculum emphasizes the evaluation of **seven critical decision variables**. Every student must master these before attempting exam questions.

### 2.1.1 Variable 1 — Strategic Importance
This measures how directly the application supports the **core competitive advantage** of the business. A core banking system for a bank is *strategically critical*; a cafeteria billing system is *peripheral*.

### 2.1.2 Variable 2 — Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)
TCO is the **complete lifetime cost** of owning and operating an application — not just the purchase price. The official KTU formula is given below.

### 2.1.3 Variable 3 — Time-to-Market (TTM)
The **duration** between project initiation and operational deployment. Critical in fast-moving industries like e-commerce and FinTech.

### 2.1.4 Variable 4 — In-House Technical Capability
The **maturity and skill level** of the organization's internal IT team. Measured using the Capability Maturity Model Integration (**CMMI**) scale of **1 to 5**.

### 2.1.5 Variable 5 — Vendor Dependency & Lock-in Risk
The degree to which switching to a different vendor later will be **costly, painful, or impossible** due to proprietary technology, data formats, or contractual obligations.

### 2.1.6 Variable 6 — Functional Fit (Requirements Coverage)
How closely the candidate solution matches the **business requirements specification (BRS)**. Expressed as a percentage: *Fit % = (Requirements Met / Total Requirements) × 100*.

### 2.1.7 Variable 7 — Scalability & Future-Proofing
The application's ability to **handle growth** in users, transactions, data volume, and feature scope without requiring fundamental redesign.

## 2.2 Detailed Strategy Analysis

### 2.2.1 Off-the-Shelf (COTS — Commercial Off-The-Shelf)

**Definition:** Pre-packaged, vendor-developed, mass-produced software sold through licensing (perpetual or subscription) to multiple customers.

**Examples:** Microsoft Office 365, SAP S/4HANA Standard, Tally ERP, Oracle E-Business Suite.

**Advantages:**
- **Low upfront cost** and predictable licensing.
- **Fast deployment** — typically weeks to months.
- **Vendor-supported maintenance, security patches, and updates**.
- **Proven reliability** — battle-tested across thousands of deployments.

**Disadvantages:**
- May include **unnecessary features** (bloat) that increase complexity.
- Requires **business process re-engineering (BPR)** to fit the software.
- **Limited customization** without costly add-ons or APIs.
- **Vendor lock-in** through proprietary data formats.

### 2.2.2 Custom In-House Development

**Definition:** Software built entirely by the organization's internal IT staff, following a formal Software Development Life Cycle (**SDLC**).

**Examples:** Banks developing proprietary fraud-detection engines; ISRO's mission control software.

**Advantages:**
- **Perfect alignment** with unique business processes.
- **Full ownership** of source code, intellectual property, and data.
- **No licensing fees** after initial development.
- **Maximum flexibility** for future modifications.

**Disadvantages:**
- **High upfront investment** in salaries, tools, infrastructure.
- **Long development timelines** — 12 to 36 months typical.
- **Project risk** — high failure rate in large custom projects (CHAOS report: **31%** of projects fail).
- Requires **ongoing in-house maintenance** commitment.

### 2.2.3 Outsourcing

**Definition:** Engaging an **external third-party vendor** to develop, host, manage, or maintain the IT application, often under a Service Level Agreement (**SLA**).

**Sub-categories:** Domestic Outsourcing, Nearshore Outsourcing, Offshore Outsourcing (e.g., India, Philippines, Eastern Europe).

**Examples:** Cognizant managing IT for American Express; TCS running the UK's National Health Service (NHS) systems.

**Advantages:**
- Access to **global talent pool** at potentially lower cost.
- Frees internal staff to focus on **core competencies**.
- **Predictable monthly OPEX** instead of large CAPEX.

**Disadvantages:**
- **Loss of direct control** over development priorities.
- **Communication barriers** in offshore scenarios (time zones, language, culture).
- **Confidentiality and data security risks** with sensitive information.
- Potential **hidden transition costs** when reversing the relationship.

### 2.2.4 Software as a Service (SaaS) / Cloud Subscription

**Definition:** Applications **hosted by a vendor on cloud infrastructure** and delivered to users over the internet, typically on a **per-user, per-month subscription** model.

**Examples:** Salesforce CRM, Workday HRMS, Google Workspace, Dropbox, Zoom.

**Advantages:**
- **Minimal upfront capital expenditure** — pure OPEX model.
- **Automatic updates** managed by the vendor.
- **Scalability on demand** — add or remove users within minutes.
- **Access from anywhere** — supports remote and hybrid work.

**Disadvantages:**
- **Recurring subscription costs** may exceed ownership cost over **5+ years**.
- **Data residency concerns** — data stored in vendor-controlled geographies.
- **Limited customization** beyond vendor-provided configuration tools.
- **Internet dependency** — outages affect productivity.

### 2.2.5 End-User Development (EUD)

**Definition:** Empowering **non-technical business users** to build, modify, or extend applications using low-code/no-code platforms, spreadsheets, or departmental databases.

**Examples:** Excel macros, Microsoft Power Apps, Airtable, Zoho Creator, Google AppSheet.

**Advantages:**
- **Rapid departmental solutions** without IT department bottleneck.
- **Empowers business users** with agility and innovation.
- **Reduces IT backlog** significantly.

**Disadvantages:**
- **Shadow IT risk** — IT loses visibility and governance.
- **Quality and security concerns** — inconsistent coding standards.
- **Data silos** and integration nightmares.
- **Vendor lock-in** to low-code platforms.

### 2.2.6 Hybrid / Best-of-Breed Approach

**Definition:** Combining multiple acquisition strategies to leverage the **strengths of each** while mitigating weaknesses.

**Examples:** Using Salesforce for CRM (SaaS) + Custom-built analytics dashboard + Microsoft 365 for productivity.

**Advantages:**
- **Optimized for each functional area** independently.
- **Avoids single-vendor dependency**.
- Allows **gradual modernization** of legacy systems.

**Disadvantages:**
- **Integration complexity** is the major challenge.
- Requires **robust middleware / API strategy**.
- **Higher governance overhead**.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Formulas for KTU ESE — Memorize the following:**

$$
TCO = C_{initial} + C_{deployment} + C_{training} + C_{maintenance} + C_{support} + C_{downtime}
$$

$$
TCO_{5yr} = C_{license} + \sum_{t=1}^{5} \left( C_{hosting} + C_{support} + C_{upgrades} \right)
$$

$$
ROI_{IT} = \frac{\text{Net Benefit from IT Investment}}{\text{Total IT Investment Cost}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\text{Payback Period} = \frac{\text{Initial Investment}}{\text{Annual Net Cash Inflow}} \quad \text{(in years)}
$$

$$
\text{Functional Fit \%} = \frac{\text{Requirements Satisfied by Solution}}{\text{Total Stated Requirements}} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{Vendor Lock-in Index} = w_1 \cdot D_{data} + w_2 \cdot D_{tech} + w_3 \cdot D_{contract} + w_4 \cdot D_{skill}
$$

> Where $D$ represents the *degree of difficulty* (scored 0 to 1) in switching away from a vendor across **data portability, technology proprietary-ness, contract penalties, and skill scarcity**, and $w_i$ are weights such that $w_1 + w_2 + w_3 + w_4 = 1$.

## 2.4 Strategy Comparison Matrix — KTU 2024 Reference Table

| Acquisition Strategy | Upfront Cost | Time to Deploy | Customization | In-House Control | Vendor Lock-in Risk | Best Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Off-the-Shelf (COTS) | Low–Medium | Weeks to Months | Low–Medium | Low | High | Standard business functions (HR, Accounting) |
| Custom In-House | Very High | 12–36 Months | Very High | Very High | None | Strategic, differentiating, proprietary systems |
| Outsourcing | Medium | 3–12 Months | High | Low–Medium | Medium | Large non-core enterprise systems |
| SaaS / Cloud Subscription | Very Low | Days to Weeks | Low | Low | High | Standardized cloud-hosted services |
| End-User Development | Very Low | Days | Medium | Very High (Local) | Medium | Departmental, tactical, shadow IT solutions |
| Hybrid / Best-of-Breed | High | 6–24 Months | High | Medium | Medium | Large enterprises with diverse needs |

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** The acquisition strategy directly determines the **System Architecture**, **Cybersecurity Posture**, **Integration Roadmap**, and **Annual IT Operating Budget**. Modern KTU case studies frequently reference the **Tata Consultancy Services iON** platform, **Infosys Finacle** for banking, and **Google Cloud Platform** SaaS examples.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivation & Analytical Decision Matrix

## 3.1 The Five-Phase Acquisition Decision Framework

In KTU 2024 scheme, the process of selecting an IT acquisition strategy is taught as a **five-phase linear decision framework**. Each phase is derived from formal IS planning literature and is examinable in detail.

### Phase 1 — Requirements Definition

The first step is converting business needs into a formal **Business Requirements Specification (BRS)** document. The BRS must contain:

- **Functional Requirements** — *what* the system must do.
- **Non-Functional Requirements** — *how well* it must perform (performance, security, availability).
- **Constraints** — budget ceiling, regulatory compliance, deployment deadline.

The requirements are then categorized using the **Kano Model** into:

- **Basic / Must-Have** requirements (e.g., payroll must calculate tax).
- **Performance / Linear** requirements (e.g., report must generate in under 5 seconds).
- **Excitement / Delighter** requirements (e.g., AI-driven predictive analytics).

> *Step 1 Logic:* Without a precise BRS, no acquisition strategy can be objectively selected. The BRS becomes the **evaluation yardstick** for all subsequent phases.

### Phase 2 — Strategy Shortlisting

Based on the BRS, the organization creates a **shortlist of 2 to 4 viable acquisition strategies** from the six pathways. This is achieved by applying the **Strategic Importance vs. Differentiation Matrix** (also called the *Make-or-Buy Grid*).

The decision rule is:

- **High Strategic Importance + High Differentiation Need** → Custom In-House Development.
- **High Strategic Importance + Low Differentiation Need** → Outsourcing (with strong SLA).
- **Low Strategic Importance + Standard Process** → Off-the-Shelf (COTS) or SaaS.
- **Low Strategic Importance + Tactical Departmental Need** → End-User Development.

### Phase 3 — Total Cost of Ownership (TCO) Calculation

For each shortlisted strategy, a **5-year TCO** is calculated. The KTU derivation formula is:

$$
TCO_{5yr} = C_{license} + C_{setup} + C_{training} + \sum_{t=1}^{5}\left(C_{hosting}(t) + C_{support}(t) + C_{upgrades}(t)\right)
$$

> Where:
> - $C_{license}$ = Initial licensing or development cost (Year 0).
> - $C_{setup}$ = Installation, configuration, data migration cost.
> - $C_{training}$ = User and administrator training cost.
> - $C_{hosting}(t)$ = Annual hosting/infrastructure cost in year $t$.
> - $C_{support}(t)$ = Annual vendor support or internal maintenance cost.
> - $C_{upgrades}(t)$ = Annual upgrade/version migration cost.

> *Step 2 Logic:* A common student error is comparing only **Year 0 license cost** between strategies. The TCO equation explicitly shows that **ongoing annual costs compound over 5 years** and often dominate the comparison.

### Phase 4 — Multi-Criteria Weighted Scoring

Each shortlisted strategy is scored on the **seven decision variables** identified in Section 2.1. Each variable is assigned a **weight** based on organizational priorities, and a **score from 1 to 10** is given to each strategy. The final score is:

$$
S_{strategy} = \sum_{i=1}^{7} w_i \times s_i
$$

> Where:
> - $w_i$ = Weight of decision variable $i$ (e.g., 0.25 for cost).
> - $s_i$ = Score (1 to 10) of the strategy on variable $i$.
> - Constraint: $\sum_{i=1}^{7} w_i = 1$.

The strategy with the **highest $S_{strategy}$** is recommended for acquisition.

### Phase 5 — Risk-Adjusted Selection

The final phase applies **risk adjustment** to the top-scoring strategy. Two key risks are quantified:

- **Vendor Failure Probability** $P_{f}$ — based on vendor's financial health, market position, and track record.
- **Project Failure Probability** $P_{p}$ — based on complexity, team experience, and methodology.

The **Risk-Adjusted Score** becomes:

$$
S_{final} = S_{strategy} \times \left(1 - P_{f}\right) \times \left(1 - P_{p}\right)
$$

The strategy with the **highest risk-adjusted score** is selected.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Style

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Practice Problem:** A mid-sized engineering college (similar to a KTU-affiliated institution) needs to acquire a **Student Lifecycle Management (SLM) System** — covering admissions, attendance, examinations, and placements. The CIO has shortlisted three strategies. Apply the **5-phase framework** to recommend the best strategy.

### Given Data

| Decision Variable | Weight $w_i$ | Strategy A: Custom In-House (Score) | Strategy B: Commercial COTS (Score) | Strategy C: SaaS Subscription (Score) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Strategic Importance | 0.20 | 9 | 6 | 6 |
| Total Cost of Ownership (5-Year) | 0.25 | 5 | 8 | 9 |
| Time-to-Market | 0.15 | 4 | 8 | 10 |
| In-House Technical Capability | 0.10 | 8 | 7 | 7 |
| Vendor Lock-in Risk (lower is better) | 0.10 | 10 | 4 | 5 |
| Functional Fit | 0.10 | 10 | 7 | 7 |
| Scalability & Future-Proofing | 0.10 | 7 | 8 | 9 |

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Calculate the weighted score for Strategy A (Custom In-House):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{A} &= (0.20 \times 9) + (0.25 \times 5) + (0.15 \times 4) \\
&\quad + (0.10 \times 8) + (0.10 \times 10) + (0.10 \times 10) + (0.10 \times 7) \\
&= 1.80 + 1.25 + 0.60 + 0.80 + 1.00 + 1.00 + 0.70 \\
&= 7.15
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the weighted score for Strategy B (Commercial COTS):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{B} &= (0.20 \times 6) + (0.25 \times 8) + (0.15 \times 8) \\
&\quad + (0.10 \times 7) + (0.10 \times 4) + (0.10 \times 7) + (0.10 \times 8) \\
&= 1.20 + 2.00 + 1.20 + 0.70 + 0.40 + 0.70 + 0.80 \\
&= 7.00
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the weighted score for Strategy C (SaaS Subscription):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{C} &= (0.20 \times 6) + (0.25 \times 9) + (0.15 \times 10) \\
&\quad + (0.10 \times 7) + (0.10 \times 5) + (0.10 \times 7) + (0.10 \times 9) \\
&= 1.20 + 2.25 + 1.50 + 0.70 + 0.50 + 0.70 + 0.90 \\
&= 7.75
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compare the three scores:**

$$
S_{C} (7.75) > S_{A} (7.15) > S_{B} (7.00)
$$

**Step 5 — Apply Risk Adjustment:**

Suppose vendor failure probability $P_{f} = 0.05$ and project failure probability $P_{p} = 0.02$ for the SaaS option.

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{C, final} &= 7.75 \times (1 - 0.05) \times (1 - 0.02) \\
&= 7.75 \times 0.95 \times 0.98 \\
&= 7.2145
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** Even after risk adjustment, **Strategy C (SaaS Subscription)** retains the highest score. **Recommended acquisition strategy: SaaS-based SLM platform** such as a cloud-hosted ERP with subscription pricing.

> [!TIP]
> **Why this works for KTU exams:** The numerical demonstrates that **Time-to-Market** and **Total Cost of Ownership** — typically weighted highest in academic scenarios — strongly favour SaaS for non-core, standardized business processes. Custom in-house wins only when **Strategic Importance** and **Functional Fit** are weighted disproportionately high.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — The IT Acquisition Decision Process

The following Mermaid block represents the **end-to-end decision flow** for selecting an IT application acquisition strategy, as prescribed in the KTU 2024 syllabus.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Business Need Identified]) --> stepB[Phase 1: Define BRS Document]
    stepB --> stepC[Categorize Requirements using Kano Model]
    stepC --> decD{Is the application STRATEGIC and DIFFERENTIATING?}
    decD -- Yes --> pathE[Shortlist: Custom In-House Development]
    decD -- No --> decF{Is the process STANDARDIZED across the industry?}
    decF -- Yes --> decG{Does the organization want LOW upfront cost and FAST deployment?}
    decF -- No --> pathH[Shortlist: Outsourcing with SLA]
    decG -- Yes --> pathI[Shortlist: SaaS / Cloud Subscription]
    decG -- No --> pathJ[Shortlist: Off the Shelf COTS]
    pathE --> stepK[Phase 3: Calculate 5-Year TCO for each shortlist]
    pathH --> stepK
    pathI --> stepK
    pathJ --> stepK
    stepK --> stepL[Phase 4: Apply Weighted Multi Criteria Scoring]
    stepL --> stepM[Phase 5: Apply Risk Adjustment using Probability Factors]
    stepM --> stepN[Select Strategy with Highest Risk Adjusted Score]
    stepN --> stepO[Proceed to Vendor Selection and Contract Negotiation]
    stepO --> stepP[Implementation, Testing, Go Live]
    stepP --> endQ([IT Application Operational])
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Hybrid Acquisition Model

For complex enterprises, no single strategy is sufficient. The following block diagram illustrates how a **Hybrid / Best-of-Breed architecture** integrates multiple acquisition pathways through a central **Enterprise Service Bus (ESB)** middleware layer.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph coreLayer["CORE LAYER: Custom Built Systems"]
        coreA["Proprietary Fraud Detection Engine"]
        coreB["In House Trading Algorithm Platform"]
    end
    subgraph operationalLayer["OPERATIONAL LAYER: Commercial Off the Shelf"]
        opsA["SAP ERP for Finance and HR"]
        opsB["Oracle Database Standard Edition"]
    end
    subgraph cloudLayer["CLOUD LAYER: SaaS Subscriptions"]
        cloudA["Salesforce CRM"]
        cloudB["Workday HRMS"]
        cloudC["Microsoft 365 Productivity Suite"]
    end
    subgraph endUserLayer["DEPARTMENTAL LAYER: End User Development"]
        euA["Excel based Budget Tracking"]
        euB["Power Apps for Equipment Booking"]
    end
    middleware["Enterprise Service Bus ESB Middleware API Gateway"]
    governance["IT Governance and Security Layer Identity Access SIEM"]
    coreA --> middleware
    coreB --> middleware
    opsA --> middleware
    opsB --> middleware
    cloudA --> middleware
    cloudB --> middleware
    cloudC --> middleware
    euA --> middleware
    euB --> middleware
    middleware --> governance
    governance --> endUsers([End Users Across Organization])
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:**  
> The **ESB Middleware** is the *integration backbone* that allows custom-built systems, COTS, SaaS, and end-user applications to exchange data seamlessly. The **IT Governance Layer** ensures that security policies, identity management, and audit logging are applied uniformly across all acquisition pathways. This is the **most commonly tested architecture** in KTU Module 4 questions.

## 4.3 Strategy Selection Logic — Quick Reference

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    q1{Requirement: Unique to Organization?}
    q1 -- Yes --> q2{Timeline: More than 1 year available?}
    q1 -- No --> q3{Budget: Capex or Opex preferred?}
    q2 -- Yes --> rA[Choose: Custom In House Development]
    q2 -- No --> rB[Choose: Outsourcing with Fixed Price Contract]
    q3 -- Capex --> rC[Choose: Off the Shelf COTS Perpetual License]
    q3 -- Opex --> rD[Choose: SaaS Cloud Subscription]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define IT application acquisition strategy. List any four commonly used acquisition strategies in modern enterprises.**

**Model Answer:**

An **IT application acquisition strategy** is the structured, organization-wide methodology that defines *how* a business obtains the software applications needed to support its operations. It involves evaluating sourcing options, selecting the optimal pathway, and managing vendor or development relationships to align IT capability with business goals.

The four commonly used acquisition strategies are:

1. **Off-the-Shelf (COTS)** — buying commercial pre-built software.
2. **Custom In-House Development** — building software internally.
3. **Outsourcing** — contracting external vendors.
4. **Software as a Service (SaaS)** — subscribing to cloud-hosted applications.

> **Examiner Note:** Students often confuse *licensing models* (perpetual vs. subscription) with *acquisition strategies*. Licensing is a *commercial term*; acquisition strategy is a *sourcing methodology*. Keep them distinct.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between Off-the-Shelf (COTS) software and Custom In-House Development. Mention two advantages of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Dimension | Off-the-Shelf (COTS) | Custom In-House Development |
|---|---|---|
| Source | Built by external vendor | Built by internal IT team |
| Cost | Low upfront, high long-term (license) | High upfront, low long-term |
| Deployment | Fast (weeks–months) | Slow (12–36 months) |
| Customization | Limited | Unlimited |
| Maintenance | Vendor-supported | Internal team responsibility |

**Two advantages of COTS:** Quick deployment, vendor-supported maintenance.

**Two advantages of Custom In-House:** Perfect functional fit, full ownership of source code and IP.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Extended Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**  
> Students frequently lose **3 to 4 marks** on this question by:
> 1. **Skipping the TCO formula** derivation — examiners *expect* the 5-year TCO equation explicitly written.
> 2. **Confusing acquisition strategy with vendor selection** — they are two distinct decisions. The acquisition strategy is chosen *before* the vendor is selected.
> 3. **Writing generic advantages without tying them to the case study** — every advantage must reference the *specific scenario* described in the question.
> 4. **Omitting risk adjustment** — the KTU 2024 rubric *explicitly awards marks* for the risk-adjustment phase in multi-criteria scoring.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**Case Study:** *Sunrise Hospitals, a 300-bed multi-specialty hospital chain in Kerala, plans to acquire a new Hospital Management Information System (HMIS). The CIO has identified three acquisition options:*

- *Option 1: Custom development by in-house IT team (estimated 18-month timeline).*
- *Option 2: Off-the-shelf COTS product (e.g., a well-known brand like CrelioHealth or similar).*
- *Option 3: SaaS-based subscription model from a cloud vendor.*

*The CEO emphasizes the need for fast deployment before the next NABH accreditation audit in 10 months, and strict compliance with the Ayushman Bharat Digital Mission (ABDM) standards.*

### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Recommend the most appropriate acquisition strategy for Sunrise Hospitals, justifying with reference to the case study's constraints (timeline, compliance, and strategic fit).**

**Model Solution:**

The recommended acquisition strategy is a **Hybrid Approach**, dominated by **SaaS Subscription** for the core operational modules (OPD, IPD, Billing, Pharmacy, Lab) with a **Custom-Developed Integration Layer** to connect with the **ABDM Health ID / ABHA** ecosystem and the National Digital Health Mission (NDHM) registry.

**Justification — Point-by-Point Analysis:**

**1. Timeline Constraint (10-month deadline):** Custom in-house development requires an estimated **18 months**, which **violates** the NABH audit deadline. COTS deployment takes 3 to 6 months but configuration for 300 beds is heavy. SaaS deployment takes 4 to 8 weeks, comfortably meeting the deadline. → **SaaS wins on time-to-market.**

**2. Compliance with ABDM Standards:** ABDM mandates specific APIs (FHIR-based), Health ID (ABHA) integration, and consent management. A pure COTS product may not have certified ABDM modules. A custom integration layer ensures compliance. → **Hybrid approach necessary.**

**3. Strategic Importance of Patient Data:** Patient data is **strategically critical** and **regulated by DPDP Act 2023**. Pure SaaS raises data residency concerns. A **Hybrid model with Indian cloud region hosting (e.g., AWS Mumbai, Azure Central India)** balances control with convenience. → **Hybrid wins on data sovereignty.**

**4. Functional Fit:** Hospital processes have unique workflow elements (e.g., Kerala-specific insurance schemes like Karunya, KASP). Pure COTS would require expensive customization. Custom-built modules around the SaaS core are most efficient. → **Hybrid wins on functional fit.**

**5. Total Cost of Ownership (5-year horizon):** SaaS subscription cost over 5 years for 300 beds ≈ ₹1.5 Cr. Custom development ≈ ₹2.2 Cr (with maintenance). COTS + customization ≈ ₹1.8 Cr. → **SaaS offers best TCO.**  
> **Final Recommendation:** Hybrid model — SaaS core + Custom integration with ABDM and state-specific modules.

### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Calculate the 5-year Total Cost of Ownership (TCO) for each of the three options and identify the financially most attractive option. Assume suitable data.**

**Model Solution:**

Let the assumed data be:

| Cost Component (₹ Lakhs) | Option 1: Custom | Option 2: COTS | Option 3: SaaS |
|---|---|---|---|
| Initial License / Development | 80 | 25 | 5 |
| Setup & Data Migration | 15 | 10 | 3 |
| Training (one-time) | 8 | 5 | 2 |
| Annual Hosting (per year) | 6 | 4 | 9 |
| Annual Support & Maintenance | 5 | 6 | 6 |
| Annual Upgrades / Customization | 4 | 5 | 2 |

**TCO Formula (5 years):**

$$
TCO_{5yr} = C_{license} + C_{setup} + C_{training} + \sum_{t=1}^{5}\left(C_{hosting}(t) + C_{support}(t) + C_{upgrades}(t)\right)
$$

**Step 1 — TCO for Option 1 (Custom In-House):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TCO_{Custom} &= 80 + 15 + 8 + 5 \times (6 + 5 + 4) \\
&= 103 + 5 \times 15 \\
&= 103 + 75 \\
&= 178 \text{ Lakhs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — TCO for Option 2 (COTS):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TCO_{COTS} &= 25 + 10 + 5 + 5 \times (4 + 6 + 5) \\
&= 40 + 5 \times 15 \\
&= 40 + 75 \\
&= 115 \text{ Lakhs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — TCO for Option 3 (SaaS):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TCO_{SaaS} &= 5 + 3 + 2 + 5 \times (9 + 6 + 2) \\
&= 10 + 5 \times 17 \\
&= 10 + 85 \\
&= 95 \text{ Lakhs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Comparison:**

$$
TCO_{SaaS} (95L) < TCO_{COTS} (115L) < TCO_{Custom} (178L)
$$

**Conclusion:** **Option 3 (SaaS) is the most financially attractive acquisition strategy** with the lowest 5-year TCO of ₹95 Lakhs. However, when combined with the strategic and compliance factors from Part (a), the **Hybrid SaaS + Custom Integration** approach remains the optimal recommendation.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the concept of Total Cost of Ownership (TCO) in the context of IT application acquisition. Derive the 5-year TCO formula and discuss why it is considered superior to comparing only initial license costs.**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:** Total Cost of Ownership (TCO) is a comprehensive financial framework that quantifies the **complete lifecycle cost** of acquiring, deploying, operating, maintaining, and disposing of an IT application over its useful life. In the context of IT acquisition, TCO enables **apples-to-apples comparison** between fundamentally different acquisition strategies (e.g., Custom vs. COTS vs. SaaS) that have radically different cost structures.

**Components of TCO:**

1. **Direct Acquisition Cost** — purchase price, license fees, subscription charges.
2. **Deployment Cost** — installation, configuration, data migration, hardware.
3. **Training Cost** — initial and ongoing user training, certification.
4. **Operational Cost** — hosting, infrastructure, electricity, bandwidth.
5. **Support Cost** — vendor support contracts or internal maintenance team salaries.
6. **Upgrade Cost** — version migrations, new feature rollouts.
7. **Disposal / Switching Cost** — exit cost, data archival, vendor transition.

**5-Year TCO Derivation:**

$$
TCO_{5yr} = C_{license} + C_{setup} + C_{training} + \sum_{t=1}^{5}\left(C_{hosting}(t) + C_{support}(t) + C_{upgrades}(t)\right)
$$

> Where the summation represents the cumulative operational, support, and upgrade costs over years 1 to 5.

**Why TCO is Superior to License-Only Comparison:**

- **Hidden Costs Revealed:** A COTS product with low ₹5 Lakh license may require ₹50 Lakh in 5-year customization. TCO captures this.
- **OPEX vs. CAPEX Normalization:** SaaS is pure OPEX; Custom is heavy CAPEX. TCO converts both to a common 5-year monetary basis.
- **Strategic Decision Support:** CFOs and CIOs can make financially defensible acquisition decisions.
- **Vendor Negotiation Leverage:** A TCO breakdown gives procurement teams data to negotiate better SLAs.

> **Real-World Example:** Many enterprises have discovered that SaaS subscriptions, while appearing cheap initially, become **3 to 5 times more expensive** than in-house ownership over a 7 to 10-year horizon. TCO analysis prevents such financial surprises.

### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Compare and contrast the Make-or-Buy decision framework in IT acquisition. Under what circumstances should an organization prefer building in-house over buying from the market?**

**Model Solution:**

The **Make-or-Buy Decision Framework** is the foundational strategic tool for IT acquisition. It is formally derived from **transaction cost economics** (Coase, 1937) and **resource-based view of the firm** (Barney, 1991).

**Comparison Table:**

| Dimension | MAKE (In-House) | BUY (Outsource / COTS / SaaS) |
|---|---|---|
| Strategic Control | Very High | Low–Medium |
| Time to Deploy | Slow (12–36 months) | Fast (days–months) |
| Upfront Cost | Very High | Low–Medium |
| Ongoing Cost | Low (after initial) | High (recurring) |
| Customization | Unlimited | Limited |
| Risk | Project execution risk | Vendor dependency risk |
| Skill Required | High (in-house team) | Low (vendor-managed) |
| IP Ownership | Full | None (license-based) |

**When to PREFER In-House (MAKE):**

1. **Core Competitive Differentiator:** When the application *is* the business's competitive advantage. *Example:* Google's search algorithm, Netflix's recommendation engine.

2. **Unique Business Processes:** When processes are highly customized and cannot be mapped to standard COTS workflows. *Example:* Specialty chemical manufacturers with proprietary formulations.

3. **Strict Data Sovereignty Requirements:** When data cannot leave organizational boundaries due to regulation. *Example:* Defence, space research (ISRO), classified government systems.

4. **Long-Term Cost Optimization:** When the application will be used for **7+ years** and the cumulative licensing cost exceeds development cost.

5. **Strategic Skill Building:** When the organization intends to build internal capability as a long-term strategic asset.

6. **No Suitable Market Alternative Exists:** When off-the-shelf products simply do not exist for the required functionality. *Example:* Early-stage AI applications before commercial offerings mature.

**Conclusion:** The Make-or-Buy decision is **not binary**. Most mature enterprises adopt a **hybrid** approach — *make* for strategic, differentiating systems and *buy* for standardized, commoditized functions.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 4**

- **Six acquisition strategies:** Off-the-Shelf (COTS), Custom In-House, Outsourcing, SaaS, End-User Development, Hybrid.
- **TCO formula** is the single most important equation — memorize the 5-year form: $TCO_{5yr} = C_{license} + C_{setup} + C_{training} + \sum_{t=1}^{5}(\ldots)$.
- **ROI formula:** $ROI = \frac{\text{Net Benefit}}{\text{Investment}} \times 100\%$.
- **Payback Period formula:** $PBP = \frac{\text{Initial Investment}}{\text{Annual Net Cash Inflow}}$.
- **Seven decision variables:** Strategic Importance, TCO, TTM, In-House Capability (CMMI 1–5), Vendor Lock-in, Functional Fit %, Scalability.
- **Five-phase decision framework:** BRS → Shortlist → TCO Calculation → Weighted Scoring → Risk Adjustment.
- **Make-or-Buy rule:** Make when *strategic + differentiating + unique*; Buy when *standardized + commoditized + non-core*.
- **Vendor Lock-in** is highest in SaaS and proprietary COTS, lowest in open-source and custom-built systems.
- **End-User Development** is fast but creates **Shadow IT** risks — must be governed by an **IT Governance Council**.
- **Hybrid model** is the *de facto* choice for large enterprises — always mention it as the *best-practice* answer in 14-mark questions.
- **SaaS TCO trap:** Subscription costs compound — over 5+ years, SaaS can exceed ownership cost by **3× to 5×**.
- **Outsourcing risk** escalates with **geographic distance** and **sensitivity of data**.
- **Real KTU exam weightage:** Acquisition strategies typically carry **1 question in Part A (3 marks)** and **1 full Part B question (14 marks)** per examination cycle.
- **Key acronyms to remember for KTU viva:** COTS, TCO, ROI, TTM, CMMI, SaaS, EUD, ESB, SLA, BRS, ABDM, DPDP Act 2023.
- **Modern exam trend:** Case-study-based questions on **Indian scenarios** (hospitals, banks, e-governance, educational institutions) — always frame your answer using the *Five-Phase Decision Framework* and *TCO calculation*.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Traditional Systems Development Life Cycle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT523 - INFORMATION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Traditional Systems Development Life Cycle

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Traditional Systems Development Life Cycle (Traditional SDLC)**, often referred to as the **Waterfall Methodology** or **Linear Sequential Model**, is a **plan-driven, phase-oriented, sequential approach** to building information systems in which each major phase (Planning → Analysis → Design → Implementation → Maintenance) is completed in its entirety before the next phase begins. The output of one phase acts as the approved input (formal deliverable) for the subsequent phase, with minimal backtracking.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEADT523 – Module 4)**, the Traditional SDLC is positioned as the **classical, document-intensive methodology** against which all modern agile and object-oriented approaches are benchmarked. It originated from the work of **Benington (1956 – SAGE system)** and was formalized by **Winston Royce (1970)**, who described the cascade of phases from feasibility analysis to operation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):**
> Traditional SDLC is the *baseline methodology* in your course. Every subsequent model (Prototyping, RAD, Spiral, Agile) is taught as a **response to its limitations**. You must memorize all 5 classical phases, their deliverables, and the role of users in each.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of Traditional SDLC like **building a multi-storey apartment block using a fixed architectural blueprint**:

| Real-World Analogy | Mapping to Traditional SDLC |
|---|---|
| Hiring an architect and getting blueprints approved by the municipality | **Planning / Feasibility Study** |
| Surveying soil, climate, and exact customer requirements | **Systems Analysis** |
| Drawing the floor plans, electrical, and plumbing layout | **Systems Design** |
| Pouring concrete, brick-laying, and installing fittings | **Systems Implementation / Coding** |
| Painting, fixing elevators, and handing over keys | **Systems Testing & Installation** |
| Pest control, plumbing repairs, structural audits | **Systems Maintenance** |

Just as you **cannot start the 5th floor** until the 4th floor is structurally complete, in Traditional SDLC you **cannot begin coding** until the design document is fully signed off. The walls between phases are rigid.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key takeaway:** Traditional SDLC assumes that *all requirements are knowable upfront* and that the environment will remain stable. It is best suited for projects where the user knows exactly what they want and the technology is well understood.

## 1.3 Core Terminology (KTU 2024 Vocabulary)

- **Deliverable** – A documented, sign-off-ready output produced at the end of each phase (e.g., *Requirements Specification Document*, *System Design Document*).
- **Phase Gate / Milestone Review** – A formal management checkpoint at the end of each phase to authorize moving forward.
- **Cascading Effect** – A single error in an early phase propagates and amplifies downstream.
- **Cold Start vs. Warm Start** – In traditional SDLC, the project is "cold" until the entire system is built; users only see results late in the cycle.
- **Big-Bang Implementation / Direct Cutover** – Replacing the old system in a single switch-over moment (a risk-heavy traditional strategy).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Phases of Traditional SDLC — Operational Breakdown

The classical model comprises **five logically distinct, sequential phases**. Each phase consumes the deliverables of the previous phase and produces a formal artifact for the next.

### Phase 1 — Systems Planning (also called Feasibility Study or Initiation)

- **Objective:** Identify the business need, define the project scope, and determine whether the proposed system is *worth building*.
- **Inputs:** Business problem statement, strategic plan, executive sponsor request.
- **Activities:**
  1. Identify and prioritize projects.
  2. Conduct a preliminary investigation.
  3. Perform **four classic feasibility studies**:
     - **Operational Feasibility** – Will users accept it?
     - **Technical Feasibility** – Do we have the hardware/software skills?
     - **Economic/Financial Feasibility** – Cost–Benefit Analysis (CBA), ROI, NPV, Payback Period.
     - **Schedule/Time Feasibility** – Can it be done within deadline?
  4. Develop the *Project Charter* / *Feasibility Report*.
- **Deliverable:** *Feasibility Report / System Proposal* — approved by management.
- **Why this phase?** Without it, organizations waste millions on systems nobody needs or cannot afford.

### Phase 2 — Systems Analysis

- **Objective:** Discover, document, and refine the *what* the system must do (functional & non-functional requirements). **No design decisions are made here.**
- **Techniques used:**
  1. Interviews (structured, unstructured, open/closed).
  2. Questionnaires / Surveys.
  3. Document Analysis.
  4. Observation (Job shadowing).
  5. Joint Application Development (JAD) sessions.
- **Modeling tools (Classical era):**
  - **Data Flow Diagrams (DFD)** – Context Diagram, Level-0, Level-1…
  - **Entity-Relationship (ER) Diagrams**
  - **Decision Trees / Decision Tables**
- **Deliverable:** *System Requirements Specification (SRS)* / *Functional Specification Document*.
- **Key principle:** "Analyze *what* the system must do, not *how* it will do it."

### Phase 3 — Systems Design

- **Objective:** Translate the SRS into a blueprint that *describes how* the system will fulfill the requirements. Two sub-divisions:
  1. **Logical Design** – Business-driven, technology-independent (also done in analysis in some texts).
  2. **Physical Design** – Maps logic to specific hardware, software, network, and database platforms.
- **Design artifacts produced:**
  - Architecture Design (1-tier / 2-tier / 3-tier / n-tier).
  - Database design (Tables, Normalization up to 3NF).
  - Input/Output design (Form layouts, screen mockups, report layouts).
  - Program specifications (HIPO charts, structure charts, pseudocode).
  - Network and security design.
- **Deliverable:** *System Design Specification (SDS)* / *Technical Design Document*.

### Phase 4 — Systems Implementation (Coding, Testing & Installation)

- **Objective:** Convert the design into a working, tested, and installed information system.
- **Sub-activities (in classical order):**
  1. **Coding / Programming** – Translating design specs into source code.
  2. **Testing (Verification + Validation):**
     - Unit Testing
     - Integration Testing
     - System Testing
     - User Acceptance Testing (UAT)
  3. **Installation / Conversion Strategies:**
     - Direct Cutover / Big-Bang
     - Parallel Conversion
     - Phased / Pilot Conversion
  4. **Training end-users** and preparing documentation (user manuals).
- **Deliverable:** *Operational Information System* + *Test Reports* + *User Documentation*.

### Phase 5 — Systems Operation, Support & Maintenance

- **Objective:** Ensure the system continues to deliver value and remains aligned with evolving business needs.
- **Maintenance types (the classic 4-way split):**
  1. **Corrective Maintenance** – Fixing bugs/errors.
  2. **Adaptive Maintenance** – Adapting to new hardware, OS, or regulations.
  3. **Perfective Maintenance** – Enhancing performance, usability, or features.
  4. **Preventive Maintenance** – Refactoring to prevent future failures.
- **Why this phase exists:** Research shows **60–80 % of total lifetime cost** of a system is incurred here.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Maintenance Iceberg":** Most students underestimate this phase. In real projects, maintenance often consumes 2× to 4× the resources of all earlier phases combined.

## 2.2 The KTU High-Yield "Cheat Sheet" Table

| Concept | Definition | KTU Exam Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Traditional SDLC** | Linear, phase-sequential, document-driven methodology | Always ask: "Why is this *traditional*?" |
| **Feasibility Study** | 4-dimensional study (Operational, Technical, Economic, Schedule) | Direct 3-mark question every year |
| **SRS** | Output of Analysis phase, defines *what* the system does | Distinguish from SDS in design questions |
| **SDS** | Output of Design phase, defines *how* the system does it | Often confused with SRS in answers |
| **JAD** | Group analysis session with users, analysts, and managers | Mentioned in module 4 – 5 marks |
| **DFD** | Graphical tool to show process flow (no design decisions) | Always asked in diagrams section |
| **Big-Bang Installation** | Direct switch from old to new system at once | High-risk, asked in 14-mark Qs |
| **Maintenance Types** | Corrective, Adaptive, Perfective, Preventive | Universal 3-mark question |
| **Cascading Error Effect** | Error in earlier phase magnifies in later phases | Asked under "limitations" |
| **Phase Gate Review** | Management sign-off checkpoint between phases | Define for 3 marks |
| **Documentation Cost** | Heavy documentation overhead in traditional SDLC | Reason agile methods emerged |
| **Linear Assumption** | Assumes requirements are *frozen* after analysis | Critique point |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Valuation Tip:** Whenever you write about Traditional SDLC, always end your answer by mentioning **at least 2 limitations** (rigidity, late user feedback, expensive to fix errors post-design, cold start, no iteration) and **2 advantages** (simple, well-documented, clear milestones, good for small known-requirement projects). Examiners *expect* this critique format.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Regulated industries** (Banking, Defence, Healthcare, Aviation) still mandate Traditional SDLC because the **audit trail and exhaustive documentation** are legal requirements (e.g., FDA, RBI, ISO 9001).
- **ERP rollouts** (SAP, Oracle) frequently blend traditional phasing with iterative modules.
- **Government e-governance projects** in India (e.g., GSTN, DigiLocker) used traditional phasing because public procurement rules demand fixed-scope bidding.
- **Embedded & safety-critical systems** (aircraft autopilot, nuclear plant SCADA) rely on V-Model — a *strict* derivative of traditional SDLC — because every requirement must trace to a test.

## 2.4 Strengths & Weaknesses (Critical for KTU 14-Mark Answers)

**Strengths**
- Simple, easy to manage; project manager can track progress phase-by-phase.
- Strong documentation discipline; supports knowledge transfer to new teams.
- Clearly defined milestones, deliverables, and sign-offs.
- Works well for *small-to-medium, well-understood* projects with stable requirements.

**Weaknesses**
- Assumes requirements are stable (rare in practice).
- User sees the system only at the end → high rejection risk.
- Errors detected late are *exponentially* expensive to fix (Boehm's curve: fixing in production can cost 200× more than fixing in requirements).
- No room for parallel development of phases.
- Encourages "analysis paralysis" — over-documentation delays coding.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> This section is *analytical* in style. Because Traditional SDLC is a methodology rather than a mathematical theorem, the "derivations" below are presented as **logical sequencing, decision logic, and cost-impact calculations** that examiners use to test depth of understanding.

## 3.1 Logical Derivation of the Phase Sequence

Let the SDLC phases be modelled as a directed state machine. We define:

$$ S = \{P, A, D, I, M\} $$

where $P$=Planning, $A$=Analysis, $D$=Design, $I$=Implementation, $M$=Maintenance.

The Traditional SDLC constrains the allowable transitions $T \subseteq S \times S$ to a single strict path:

$$ T_{\text{Traditional}} = \{ (P \to A), (A \to D), (D \to I), (I \to M) \} $$

**Step-by-step reasoning:**

1. The project cannot start without authorization → $P$ is the entry state.
2. A feasibility-approved plan defines the boundary of the system → $P \to A$.
3. Approved requirements are needed before design begins → $A \to D$.
4. Approved design must precede any code → $D \to I$.
5. After deployment, the system enters a long-lived steady state → $I \to M$.

No back-edges exist in the **pure** traditional model. (Royce's 1970 paper actually argued that *adding* a back-edge from $I$ back to $A$ improves it — this insight later birthed the **Iterative** and **Spiral** models.)

## 3.2 Cost-of-Error Amplification (Boehm's Curve — Empirical)

A foundational KTU concept is the **exponentially growing cost of defect correction** as the SDLC progresses. Let $C_i$ = cost of fixing a defect introduced in phase $i$ and detected in phase $j$.

$$ C_{\text{fix}}(i, j) = C_0 \cdot 10^{(j - i)} $$

where:
- $C_0$ is the **baseline cost** of fixing during the same phase where it was introduced.
- $i$ is the *introduction phase* index.
- $j$ is the *detection phase* index.
- The factor $10^{(j-i)}$ is the empirical multiplier (Boehm, 1981).

**Worked KTU-Style Calculation (very likely 3-mark or 7-mark sub-question):**

> **Question:** A requirements defect is introduced in the Analysis phase. It is detected during System Testing (in the Implementation phase). If the baseline fix cost $C_0$ = ₹ 1,000, compute the rectification cost.

**Step 1 – Identify phase indices.** Conventionally we number phases 1 to 5:
- $P = 1$, $A = 2$, $D = 3$, $I = 4$, $M = 5$.

**Step 2 – Identify $i$ and $j$.** Defect introduced in $A \Rightarrow i = 2$. Detected during System Testing, which is part of $I \Rightarrow j = 4$.

**Step 3 – Plug into formula:**

$$ C_{\text{fix}} = 1{,}000 \cdot 10^{(4 - 2)} $$

**Step 4 – Simplify exponent:**

$$ C_{\text{fix}} = 1{,}000 \cdot 10^{2} $$

**Step 5 – Final value:**

$$ C_{\text{fix}} = 1{,}000 \cdot 100 = \text{₹ } 1{,}00{,}000 $$

**Interpretation for the answer:** A defect that would cost ₹ 1,000 to fix if caught in the Analysis phase itself ends up costing ₹ 1,00,000 if detected in System Testing — a **100× amplification**. This is the central justification for *front-loading* effort in traditional SDLC: the more thorough the Analysis and Design, the lower the lifetime cost.

## 3.3 Payback Period Derivation (Economic Feasibility)

For the Feasibility phase, KTU frequently asks the Payback Period. Let:
- $I_0$ = initial investment.
- $C_i$ = net annual cash inflow in year $i$.

The **Payback Period** $N_{\text{pb}}$ is the smallest integer $n$ such that:

$$ \sum_{i=1}^{n} C_i \geq I_0 $$

**Worked example (3-mark sub-part style):**

> A traditional SDLC project costs ₹ 20,00,000 to build and yields annual cash savings of ₹ 6,00,000. Find the payback period.

**Step 1 – Year 1 cumulative:**

$$ 6{,}00{,}000 \;\textless\; 20{,}00{,}000 \quad \Rightarrow \text{not recovered} $$

**Step 2 – Year 2 cumulative:**

$$ 6{,}00{,}000 + 6{,}00{,}000 = 12{,}00{,}000 \;\textless\; 20{,}00{,}000 $$

**Step 3 – Year 3 cumulative:**

$$ 12{,}00{,}000 + 6{,}00{,}000 = 18{,}00{,}000 \;\textless\; 20{,}00{,}000 $$

**Step 4 – Year 4 cumulative:**

$$ 18{,}00{,}000 + 6{,}00{,}000 = 24{,}00{,}000 \;\geq\; 20{,}00{,}000 $$

**Step 5 – Result:**

$$ N_{\text{pb}} \approx 3.33 \text{ years} \;\; (\text{exact} = 3 + \tfrac{2{,}00{,}000}{6{,}00{,}000}) $$

**Interpretation:** The investment is recovered between Year 3 and Year 4 — a key number compared against the organization's threshold (e.g., 3 years max).

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Phase → Activity → Output → User Role

| Phase | Core Activity | Primary Output (Deliverable) | User Involvement Level | Error-Cost Multiplier |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **1. Planning** | Feasibility & scope | Feasibility Report, Project Charter | Low (executive sponsor) | $10^{0}$ |
| **2. Analysis** | Requirements elicitation | SRS Document | **High** (JAD, interviews) | $10^{1}$ |
| **3. Design** | Logical + physical design | SDS Document, ER Diagram, DFD | Medium (review) | $10^{2}$ |
| **4. Implementation** | Code, test, install | Working System + Test Reports | High (UAT) | $10^{3}$ |
| **5. Maintenance** | Operate, enhance | Patches, upgrades | Variable | $10^{4}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matrix matters for KTU:** Examiners ask "In which phase are users MOST involved?" (Answer: Analysis, with JAD). They also ask "Where is fixing an error most expensive?" (Answer: Maintenance, i.e., $10^{4}$ multiplier).

## 3.5 Comparison Matrix — Traditional SDLC vs. Modern Variants

| Dimension | Traditional SDLC | Prototyping | RAD | Agile |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Structure** | Rigid, linear | Iterative | Iterative, component-based | Iterative, time-boxed |
| **User feedback** | Only at the end | Early & continuous | Early & continuous | Continuous |
| **Documentation** | Very heavy | Light | Moderate | Minimal |
| **Requirement stability** | Requires stable reqs. | Allows unclear reqs. | Allows unclear reqs. | Embraces change |
| **Risk management** | Reactive | Limited | Moderate | Built-in |
| **Delivery** | Single delivery at end | Throwaway then real | Incremental | Incremental, frequent |
| **Best for** | Regulated, small projects | UI-heavy, unclear reqs. | Time-critical, modular | Dynamic, innovative projects |

## 3.6 Decision Logic for "Should we use Traditional SDLC?"

A project is a strong candidate for the traditional approach **if and only if** *all* of the following hold:

1. Requirements are clearly defined and stable.
2. The technology is mature and familiar to the team.
3. The project duration is short-to-medium (typically $\leq$ 12 months).
4. Documentation, audit trails, and compliance are mandatory.
5. The user community can wait until late in the cycle for the working system.

If *any* of these conditions fails, KTU expects the student to *recommend an alternative* (Prototyping, RAD, Spiral, or Agile) — a classic 14-mark answer structure.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid — The Classical Waterfall Cascade

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    phase1["PHASE 1 SYSTEMS PLANNING Feasibility Study and Project Charter"] --> gate1["GATE 1 Management Review"]
    gate1 --> phase2["PHASE 2 SYSTEMS ANALYSIS SRS Document and DFDs"]
    phase2 --> gate2["GATE 2 Requirements Sign Off"]
    gate2 --> phase3["PHASE 3 SYSTEMS DESIGN SDS Document and ER Diagrams"]
    phase3 --> gate3["GATE 3 Design Approval"]
    gate3 --> phase4["PHASE 4 SYSTEMS IMPLEMENTATION Coding Testing and Installation"]
    phase4 --> gate4["GATE 4 User Acceptance Sign Off"]
    gate4 --> phase5["PHASE 5 SYSTEMS MAINTENANCE Corrective Adaptive Perfective Preventive"]
    phase5 --> phase5

    classDef phase fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e40af,stroke-width:2px,color:#0f172a
    classDef gate fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#b45309,stroke-width:2px,color:#0f172a
    class phase1,phase2,phase3,phase4,phase5 phase
    class gate1,gate2,gate3,gate4 gate
```

## 4.2 Mermaid — Functional Block Architecture of Traditional SDLC

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    a1["Business Need and Sponsor Request"] --> b1["P1 Planning Feasibility Operational Technical Economic Schedule"]
    b1 --> c1["Project Charter Approved"]
    c1 --> b2["P2 Analysis Interviews JAD Questionnaires Observation"]
    b2 --> c2["SRS Document with DFDs and ER"]
    c2 --> b3["P3 Design Logical and Physical Architecture and Database"]
    b3 --> c3["SDS Document and Program Specs"]
    c3 --> b4["P4 Implementation Code Unit Integration UAT and Conversion"]
    b4 --> c4["Operational System Released to Users"]
    c4 --> b5["P5 Maintenance Corrective Adaptive Perfective Preventive"]
    b5 --> c5["System Retired or Replaced"]

    classDef inputblock fill:#e0f2fe,stroke:#075985,stroke-width:2px,color:#082f49
    classDef phaseblock fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#166534,stroke-width:2px,color:#052e16
    classDef outputblock fill:#fae8ff,stroke:#86198f,stroke-width:2px,color:#3b0764
    class a1 inputblock
    class b1,b2,b3,b4,b5 phaseblock
    class c1,c2,c3,c4,c5 outputblock
```

## 4.3 Mermaid — Boehm's Cost-of-Error Amplification Curve

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    p1["REQUIREMENTS Cost x 1"] --> p2["DESIGN Cost x 10"]
    p2 --> p3["CODING Cost x 100"]
    p3 --> p4["TESTING Cost x 1000"]
    p4 --> p5["MAINTENANCE Cost x 10000"]
    p5 --> p5note["Most expensive phase to fix a defect"]

    classDef costlow fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#166534,color:#052e16
    classDef costmid fill:#fef9c3,stroke:#a16207,color:#451a03
    classDef costhigh fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#991b1b,color:#450a0a
    class p1 costlow
    class p2 costmid
    class p3 costmid
    class p4 costhigh
    class p5 costhigh
```

## 4.4 Mermaid — Conversion Strategies (Installation Sub-Phase)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start["Old System in Production"] --> choice{"Choose Conversion Strategy"}
    choice --> big["Direct Cutover Big Bang Switch in one night HIGH RISK"]
    choice --> par["Parallel Run Both systems run together SAFE but expensive"]
    choice --> pha["Phased Pilot One department at a time MODERATE RISK"]
    big --> new["New System Live"]
    par --> new
    pha --> new
    new --> retire["Old System Retired"]

    classDef decision fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#b45309,color:#0f172a
    classDef strategy fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e40af,color:#0f172a
    classDef outcome fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#166534,color:#052e16
    class start,new,retire outcome
    class choice decision
    class big,par,pha strategy
```

## 4.5 Mermaid — Maintenance Type Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    m["Maintenance Phase"] --> m1["Corrective Bug Fixing"]
    m --> m2["Adaptive New OS or Regulation"]
    m --> m3["Perfective Performance or Feature Enhancement"]
    m --> m4["Preventive Refactoring to Avoid Future Failures"]
    m1 --> example1["Example Patch a payroll calculation error"]
    m2 --> example2["Example Migrate system to new GST tax rule"]
    m3 --> example3["Example Add a new dashboard for managers"]
    m4 --> example4["Example Rewrite a slow SQL query to prevent timeouts"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Legend (print this in your exam):**
> - Blue nodes = Phase inputs / outputs
> - Green nodes = Deliverables
> - Yellow nodes = Decision gates or risk-bearing points
> - Red nodes = High-cost or high-risk situations
