<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks)

### Q1. Define Traditional SDLC. List its three main characteristics. [3 Marks]
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**
Traditional SDLC is a *plan-driven, phase-oriented methodology* for building information systems where each major phase (Planning, Analysis, Design, Implementation, Maintenance) is completed in full before the next begins. Its three main characteristics are:
1. **Sequential / Linear flow** — phases cascade one after another.
2. **Document-driven** — every phase ends with a formal, sign-off-ready deliverable.
3. **Rigid phase gates** — movement to the next phase requires management approval.

*(Examiner's note: 1 mark for the definition, 1 mark for naming the methodology as Waterfall/Linear, 1 mark for any 2 characteristics.)*

---

### Q2. List the four types of system maintenance with a one-line example for each. [3 Marks]
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Model Answer:**

| # | Type | Example |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Corrective** | Fixing a billing miscalculation bug reported by users |
| 2 | **Adaptive** | Updating the system to comply with a new tax law |
| 3 | **Perfective** | Adding a new analytics dashboard requested by management |
| 4 | **Preventive** | Refactoring fragile modules to prevent future crashes |

*(Examiner's note: ½ mark per correct type + ½ mark per valid example. Memorize this verbatim — it is a guaranteed question.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (ESE Module Choice: 14 Marks)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 4]**

**(a) Explain the five phases of the Traditional Systems Development Life Cycle with their key deliverables. [7 Marks]**
**(b) Discuss the strengths and limitations of the Traditional SDLC. When would you prefer it over an Agile approach? [7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution — (a)

1. **Phase 1 – Systems Planning (1 mark)**
   - Identify business opportunity; perform **four feasibility studies** (Operational, Technical, Economic, Schedule).
   - Deliverable: *Feasibility Report / Project Charter* (½ mark).

2. **Phase 2 – Systems Analysis (1.5 marks)**
   - Elicit functional and non-functional requirements via interviews, JAD, observation.
   - Build *Data Flow Diagrams*, *ER Diagrams*, *Decision Tables*.
   - Deliverable: *System Requirements Specification (SRS)* (½ mark).

3. **Phase 3 – Systems Design (1.5 marks)**
   - Convert SRS into logical and physical design: architecture, database, I/O, security, network.
   - Deliverable: *System Design Specification (SDS)* (½ mark).

4. **Phase 4 – Systems Implementation (1.5 marks)**
   - Code the modules, conduct unit / integration / system / UAT testing.
   - Train users, choose a conversion strategy (Direct, Parallel, Phased).
   - Deliverable: *Operational System + Test Reports + User Manuals* (½ mark).

5. **Phase 5 – Systems Maintenance (1 mark)**
   - Apply *Corrective, Adaptive, Perfective, Preventive* maintenance.
   - Deliverable: *System updates, patches, and performance reports* (½ mark).

#### Model Solution — (b)

**Strengths (3 marks):**
- **Discipline & Control** – Phase gates enforce sign-offs, making it easy to manage.
- **Documentation** – Heavy documentation supports audits, handovers, and compliance.
- **Predictability** – Well-defined cost, schedule, and scope.
- **Best for small, stable, regulated projects.**

**Limitations (3 marks):**
- **Late user feedback** – Users see the system only at the end.
- **Expensive error correction** – Defects caught late cost 10–10,000× more (Boehm's curve).
- **Rigid** – No room for requirement changes once Analysis is signed off.
- **Cold start** – No working system is delivered until very late.

**When to prefer Traditional SDLC over Agile (1 mark):**
Choose Traditional SDLC when *requirements are stable, the project is small-to-medium, regulatory documentation is mandatory, the team is geographically distributed and needs formal sign-offs, or the technology is well understood*. Examples: banking core systems, defence command-and-control systems, government e-governance rollouts.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam – Supplementary Model Paper]**

**(a) What is a Feasibility Study? Describe its four dimensions in detail. [7 Marks]**
**(b) Explain the different Installation/Conversion strategies used in Traditional SDLC. Recommend the safest strategy and justify. [7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution — (a)

A **Feasibility Study** is a Phase-1 investigation to determine *whether the proposed system is worth building* and *whether the organization can build it*. It is performed *before* committing significant resources.

**The four dimensions (1.5 marks each + 0.5 for intro):**

1. **Operational Feasibility** – Measures *human, organizational, and political* acceptance.
   - Will users support the system? Will it disrupt current workflows?
   - Tools: user-attitude surveys, JAD sessions, organizational chart analysis.

2. **Technical Feasibility** – Assesses *whether the technology and skills* are available.
   - Hardware, software, network, and human-resource capability.
   - Tools: technology audit, vendor evaluation, proof-of-concept prototypes.

3. **Economic / Financial Feasibility** – Answers *"Is the financial return worth the cost?"*
   - Methods: **Cost–Benefit Analysis (CBA)**, **Return on Investment (ROI)**, **Net Present Value (NPV)**, **Payback Period**.
   - Tangible + intangible benefits are considered.

4. **Schedule Feasibility** – Verifies *whether the project can be completed in the desired time*.
   - Checks deadlines, team availability, dependency on external vendors.

#### Model Solution — (b)

The three classical conversion strategies are:

1. **Direct Cutover (Big-Bang) (2 marks):**
   - Old system is *switched off* and the new system is switched on the same day.
   - **Pros:** Cheapest, fastest, no duplicate data entry.
   - **Cons:** Extremely risky; if the new system fails, there is no fallback.

2. **Parallel Conversion (2.5 marks):**
   - Both old and new systems run *simultaneously* for a defined period.
   - **Pros:** Zero risk of business disruption; users can compare outputs.
   - **Cons:** Expensive (double running costs, duplicate data entry).

3. **Phased / Pilot Conversion (1.5 marks):**
   - The new system is rolled out *one module, one department, or one location at a time*.
   - **Pros:** Balanced risk and cost; learning from pilot informs subsequent rollouts.
   - **Cons:** Longest duration; interface issues between old and new modules.

**Recommendation + Justification (1 mark):**
The **Parallel Conversion** strategy is the **safest** because the organization retains the old system as a 100 % fallback during the cutover. Any error in the new system can be verified against the old one, ensuring business continuity. It is especially recommended for *mission-critical* systems such as banking or hospital information systems where downtime is unacceptable.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing SRS with SDS** — *SRS = What the system does (Analysis output); SDS = How the system does it (Design output).* This is the **#1 mistake** in KTU answer sheets. Examiners deduct up to 2 marks.
> 2. **Writing "users are involved in all phases"** — Wrong. In Traditional SDLC, users are *most* involved in **Analysis** and **UAT**, and *least* involved in **Coding**. Specificity wins marks.
> 3. **Forgetting maintenance types** — Every Traditional-SDLC answer must mention Corrective / Adaptive / Perfective / Preventive. Examiners explicitly look for the 4-way split.
> 4. **Ignoring limitations** — A purely descriptive answer that does *not* critique the model scores a maximum of 8/14. Always end with at least 2 limitations.
> 5. **Mixing up feasibility dimensions** — Memorize the order **O-T-E-S** (Operational, Technical, Economic, Schedule). Mixing Economic with Schedule is a common slip.
> 6. **Skipping the diagram in the 14-mark question** — Even a hand-drawn waterfall cascade is worth 1–2 marks. **Always include a phase-flow diagram.**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Full form:** Traditional SDLC = Traditional Systems Development Life Cycle (also called *Waterfall* or *Linear Sequential Model*).
- **5 canonical phases:** **P**lanning → **A**nalysis → **D**esign → **I**mplementation → **M**aintenance. Mnemonic: **"PAD-IM"**.
- **Phase-1 deliverable:** *Feasibility Report* (4 dimensions: **O-T-E-S**).
- **Phase-2 deliverable:** *SRS* with DFDs and ER diagrams; built using interviews, JAD, observation, questionnaires.
- **Phase-3 deliverable:** *SDS* with architecture, database, I/O, and program specs.
- **Phase-4 deliverable:** Working system + test reports; conversion strategies = Direct / Parallel / Phased.
- **Phase-5 deliverable:** Patches, upgrades; 4 maintenance types = Corrective / Adaptive / Perfective / Preventive (**C-A-P-P**).
- **Critical formula:** $C_{\text{fix}}(i, j) = C_0 \cdot 10^{(j-i)}$ (Boehm's cost-amplification rule).
- **Critical concept:** *Late-stage errors are exponentially expensive*; front-load quality into Analysis.
- **Key strengths:** Simple, documented, milestone-based, audit-friendly.
- **Key weaknesses:** Rigid, late user feedback, expensive late corrections, no iteration.
- **Best for:** Small, stable, regulated, well-documented projects.
- **Avoid when:** Requirements are unclear, environment is dynamic, or users need early prototypes.
- **Historical context:** Originated with Benington (1956, SAGE) and Royce (1970); agile methods emerged as a direct critique of its rigidity.
- **KTU exam weightage:** This topic typically contributes **14 marks** (one full Part-B question) plus **3–6 marks** in Part-A and module tests.
- **Mandatory inclusions in any 14-mark answer:** a phase diagram, mention of all 4 feasibility types, mention of all 4 maintenance types, and at least 2 limitations.
- **One-line exam-ready definition:**
  *"Traditional SDLC is a linear, phase-sequential, document-driven methodology where each phase must be formally completed and signed off before the next begins."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Alternative Methods and Tools for Systems Development
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT523 - INFORMATION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Alternative Methods and Tools for Systems Development

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4 — Alternative Methods and Tools for Systems Development

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of the **Systems Development Life Cycle (SDLC)**, traditional methodologies like the **Waterfall Model** and the **Systems Development Life Cycle (SDLC)** follow a rigid, sequential, and plan-driven approach. *Alternative Methods and Tools for Systems Development* refers to the family of methodologies, techniques, and software tools that deviate from this classical path. They are designed to accelerate delivery, enhance user participation, handle changing requirements, and reduce overall project risk.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PEADT523)**, these alternatives include:

- **Prototyping**
- **Rapid Application Development (RAD)**
- **Agile Development**
- **End-User Development (EUD)**
- **Component-Based Development (CBD)**
- **Object-Oriented Systems Development (OOSD)**
- **Web-Based Systems Development**
- **Commercial Off-The-Shelf (COTS) Packages / Outsourcing**
- **Joint Application Development (JAD)**
- **Computer-Aided Software Engineering (CASE) Tools**
- **Fourth-Generation Languages (4GL)**

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Alternatives Exist:** The traditional SDLC is criticized for being slow, inflexible to changing requirements, and producing systems that are not always aligned with actual user needs. Alternative methods address these limitations.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are building a house.

- **Traditional SDLC (Waterfall)** is like an architect drawing every single blueprint, getting approval, and then building the entire house top-to-bottom with no chance to change the design mid-way. If the family later says, *"We actually wanted a bigger kitchen,"* it's very expensive to fix.
- **Alternative Methods** are like building a small *model room* first (Prototyping), or using *pre-fabricated rooms* (Component-Based), or building the house in small *increments* with the family giving feedback after each piece (Agile/RAD).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Idea:** Alternative methods focus on *faster feedback loops*, *greater user involvement*, and *adaptive planning* rather than rigid documentation and sequential phases.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Parameters

Some key parameters used to evaluate these methods in KTU-style questions:

- **Time-to-Market (TTM)**: Days/weeks from project start to delivery.
- **User Involvement Index (UII)**: A qualitative scale (Low / Medium / High) of end-user participation.
- **Flexibility Score**: Ability to incorporate changing requirements.
- **Risk Reduction (%)**: Reduction in project failure probability.
- **Productivity Multiplier**: 4GL/CASE tools can deliver **5x to 10x** productivity over 3GL.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Examination of Each Alternative Method

### 2.1.1 Prototyping

Prototyping is an iterative process in which a working model (a *prototype*) of the system is built quickly, tested by the user, and refined based on feedback until an acceptable system is derived.

**Four Steps of Prototyping:**

1. **Identify the User's Basic Requirements**: Determine the core needs (output, inputs, performance).
2. **Develop an Initial Prototype**: Build a quick, low-cost working model using 4GLs or prototyping tools.
3. **User Test and Refine the Prototype**: User interacts with it; developer makes modifications.
4. **Repeat Until Acceptable**: Iterate steps 2–3 until the user is satisfied.

**Types of Prototypes:**

| Type | Description | Use Case |
|------|-------------|----------|
| **Throwaway / Rapid Prototype** | Built to learn requirements, then discarded | Unclear requirements |
| **Evolutionary Prototype** | Continuously refined into the final system | Web applications |
| **Incremental Prototype** | Several prototypes built, each adding features | Large systems |
| **Operational Prototype** | Becomes the production system | Pilot deployments |

> [!NOTE]
> **Advantage:** Reduces risk of misunderstanding user needs.
> **Disadvantage:** Users may mistake the prototype for the final system and expect too much too soon.

### 2.1.2 Rapid Application Development (RAD)

RAD is a methodology that prioritizes rapid prototyping and iterative delivery over heavy planning. The **James Martin** RAD model has four phases:

1. **Requirements Planning** — Combine business and data modeling.
2. **User Design** — Workshops + prototyping with intensive user involvement.
3. **Construction** — Use of 4GLs, CASE tools, and reusable components.
4. **Cutover** — Implementation, testing, and rollout.

**Key RAD Principles:**

- Active user involvement.
- Less formal documentation.
- Short development cycles (**60–90 days** typical).
- Use of **4GLs** and **CASE tools**.

### 2.1.3 Agile Development

Agile is an umbrella term for methodologies like **Scrum**, **Extreme Programming (XP)**, **Kanban**, and **SAFe**. It is guided by the **Agile Manifesto (2001)** with four core values:

1. **Individuals and interactions** over processes and tools.
2. **Working software** over comprehensive documentation.
3. **Customer collaboration** over contract negotiation.
4. **Responding to change** over following a plan.

**Scrum Framework Highlights:**

- **Sprint**: A time-boxed iteration of **2–4 weeks**.
- **Product Backlog**: Prioritized list of features (user stories).
- **Daily Standup (Scrum)**: 15-minute daily meeting.
- **Sprint Review & Retrospective**: Held at the end of every sprint.

### 2.1.4 End-User Development (EUD)

EUD allows end-users (non-programmers) to develop their own information systems using tools such as:

- **Spreadsheets** (Excel, Google Sheets).
- **Database Management Systems** (Access, FileMaker).
- **Low-code/No-code platforms** (OutSystems, Mendix, AppSheet).
- **Report generators** (Crystal Reports, Power BI).

**EUD Formula (for KTU derivations):**

$$
EUD\_Savings = C_{IT} \times (1 - P_{outsource}) - C_{user} - C_{training}
$$

Where:
- $C_{IT}$ = Cost if developed by the IT department.
- $P_{outsource}$ = Probability of outsourcing (0 to 1).
- $C_{user}$ = User's time/opportunity cost.
- $C_{training}$ = Training cost for the user.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Risk of EUD:** *Shadow IT* — systems developed outside the IT department's governance, leading to security, data integrity, and compliance issues.

### 2.1.5 Component-Based Development (CBD)

CBD focuses on building systems by **assembling pre-existing, reusable software components**. Each component has a well-defined interface and can be deployed independently.

**CBD Life Cycle:**

1. **Component Identification**.
2. **Component Acquisition** (built in-house, COTS, or open-source).
3. **Component Integration** using middleware / Enterprise Service Bus (ESB).
4. **System Testing**.

**Advantages:**

- Reduced development time (**30–70%** reuse).
- Improved reliability (components are pre-tested).
- Easier maintenance.

### 2.1.6 Object-Oriented Systems Development (OOSD)

OOSD combines data and behavior into *objects* and uses **UML (Unified Modeling Language)** for design. It emphasizes:

- **Encapsulation**
- **Inheritance**
- **Polymorphism**
- **Abstraction**

**OOSD Phases:**

1. Object-Oriented Analysis (OOA).
2. Object-Oriented Design (OOD).
3. Object-Oriented Implementation / Programming (OOP).

### 2.1.7 Web-Based Systems Development

This approach builds applications that run inside a web browser using:

- **Front-end**: HTML, CSS, JavaScript, React, Angular.
- **Back-end**: Node.js, Python (Django/Flask), Java (Spring), PHP.
- **Databases**: MySQL, MongoDB, PostgreSQL.
- **Cloud Platforms**: AWS, Azure, Google Cloud.

**Key Features:**

- Platform-independent.
- Easy to update (single deployment).
- Lower distribution cost.
- Supports **Service-Oriented Architecture (SOA)** and **microservices**.

### 2.1.8 Commercial Off-The-Shelf (COTS) & Outsourcing

- **COTS**: Pre-built software purchased and customized (e.g., SAP, Oracle, Tally).
- **Outsourcing**: Hiring an external vendor (e.g., Infosys, TCS) to develop the system.

**COTS Decision Matrix:**

| Criterion | Build In-House | Buy COTS | Outsource |
|-----------|----------------|----------|-----------|
| Cost | High | Medium | Variable |
| Time | Long | Short | Medium |
| Customization | Full | Limited | Full |
| Maintenance | Internal | Vendor | Vendor |

### 2.1.9 Joint Application Development (JAD)

JAD is a structured, facilitated workshop technique where key stakeholders, users, and developers work together intensively (**typically 3–5 days**) to define requirements and design the system.

**JAD Roles:**

- **Sponsor** — Senior executive authority.
- **Facilitator** — Leads the workshop.
- **Users** — Domain experts.
- **Developers / Analysts** — Technical experts.
- **Observers** — Take notes.

### 2.1.10 Computer-Aided Software Engineering (CASE) Tools

CASE tools automate the activities of the SDLC. They are classified along two dimensions:

- **Upper-CASE (Front-End)**: Support planning, analysis, and design (diagramming tools).
- **Lower-CASE (Back-End)**: Support coding, testing, and maintenance (code generators, debuggers).
- **Integrated CASE (I-CASE)**: Combines both.

**Examples:** Rational Rose, Visual Paradigm, Enterprise Architect, Lucidchart.

### 2.1.11 Fourth-Generation Languages (4GL)

4GLs are high-level languages designed to be closer to natural language, enabling faster development. Categories include:

- **Query Languages** (SQL).
- **Report Generators**.
- **Application Generators** (PowerBuilder, OutSystems).
- **Decision Support Systems Generators**.

**Productivity Multiplier:**

$$
PM = \frac{LOC_{3GL}}{LOC_{4GL}}
$$

A typical 4GL delivers the same functionality with **5x–10x** fewer lines of code.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table summarizes all key formulas and parameters relevant to KTU examinations for this topic.

| # | Concept | Formula / Parameter | Description / Use |
|---|---------|----------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | EUD Savings | $S = C_{IT} - C_{user} - C_{training}$ | Cost saved by EUD vs IT dev |
| 2 | Productivity Multiplier (4GL) | $PM = LOC_{3GL} / LOC_{4GL}$ | Lines of Code ratio |
| 3 | Reuse Savings | $R = C_{rebuild} - C_{reuse}$ | Cost saved by reusing components |
| 4 | RAD Time | $T_{RAD} = 60$ to $90$ days | Typical project duration |
| 5 | Sprint Duration | $T_{sprint} = 2$ to $4$ weeks | Agile Scrum time-box |
| 6 | JAD Workshop | $T_{JAD} = 3$ to $5$ days | Standard workshop length |
| 7 | COTS Payback | $PB = \frac{C_{COTS} - C_{inhouse}}{S_{annual}}$ | Payback period in years |
| 8 | Outsourcing Cost | $C_{out} = C_{vendor} + C_{mgmt}$ | Total outsourcing cost |
| 9 | CASE Tool ROI | $ROI = \frac{(B - C_{CASE})}{C_{CASE}} \times 100$ | Percentage return on CASE |
| 10 | Flexibility Score | $F \in \{Low, Medium, High\}$ | Qualitative measure |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Real-World Utility:** In industry, these methods drive **digital transformation**, **DevOps pipelines**, and **cloud-native development** for everything from banking apps to IoT systems.

# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Comparative Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Worked-Out Derivations (Sample KTU Numerical Problems)

### 3.1.1 Derivation 1 — 4GL Productivity Multiplier

A 3GL application requires **8,000 lines of C code** to implement a module. The same module is implemented in a 4GL using **1,200 lines**. Calculate the **Productivity Multiplier (PM)**.

**Step 1: Recall the productivity formula.**

$$
PM = \frac{LOC_{3GL}}{LOC_{4GL}}
$$

**Step 2: Substitute the given values.**

$$
PM = \frac{8000}{1200}
$$

**Step 3: Perform the division.**

$$
8000 \div 1200 = \frac{80}{12} = \frac{20}{3} = 6.667
$$

**Step 4: Interpret the result.**

$$
PM \approx 6.67
$$

> **Interpretation:** The 4GL developer writes approximately **6.67 times fewer lines** of code, which translates to faster development and lower defect rates.

---

### 3.1.2 Derivation 2 — EUD Cost-Benefit Analysis

A company's IT department estimates that building a small inventory system internally would cost **INR 2,00,000** over 6 weeks. An end-user can build the same system using Excel in **2 weeks**, with personal time cost valued at **INR 40,000** and a one-time training cost of **INR 15,000**. Calculate the **net savings** if the user builds it.

**Step 1: List the parameters.**

$$
C_{IT} = 200000
$$

$$
C_{user} = 40000
$$

$$
C_{training} = 15000
$$

**Step 2: Apply the EUD Savings formula.**

$$
S = C_{IT} - C_{user} - C_{training}
$$

**Step 3: Substitute values.**

$$
S = 200000 - 40000 - 15000
$$

**Step 4: Compute the result.**

$$
S = 145000
$$

> **Result:** Net savings of **INR 1,45,000**. However, quality, support, and integration risks must still be considered.

---

### 3.1.3 Derivation 3 — COTS Payback Period

A COTS package costs **INR 10,00,000** including customization. Building in-house would cost **INR 18,00,000**. Annual savings from COTS over in-house (e.g., licensing efficiency, lower maintenance) is **INR 2,00,000/year**. Find the **Payback Period (PB)**.

**Step 1: State the formula.**

$$
PB = \frac{C_{COTS} - C_{inhouse}}{S_{annual}}
$$

**Step 2: Substitute.**

$$
PB = \frac{1000000 - 1800000}{200000}
$$

**Step 3: Simplify numerator.**

$$
PB = \frac{-800000}{200000}
$$

**Step 4: Compute the result.**

$$
PB = -4 \text{ years}
$$

> **Interpretation:** A *negative* payback indicates that COTS is **cheaper from Year 1** itself (savings of INR 8,00,000 upfront). The organization recovers the difference **immediately** rather than waiting for years.

---

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define Prototyping. List its advantages and limitations.*

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:**
Prototyping is an iterative systems development approach in which a working model of the system (a *prototype*) is built quickly, demonstrated to the user, refined based on feedback, and iterated until the system meets user requirements.

**Advantages:**

1. Reduces risk of misunderstanding user requirements.
2. Encourages active user participation.
3. Useful when requirements are unclear or evolving.
4. Speeds up requirement elicitation.

**Limitations:**

1. Users may confuse the prototype with the final product.
2. Inappropriate for large, complex systems if used as the only method.
3. Iteration may lead to scope creep.
4. Documentation is often minimal.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often list only the advantages. Always include **at least two limitations** to secure full marks.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*What are 4GLs? Give two examples.*

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:**
Fourth-Generation Languages (4GL) are high-level, problem-oriented programming languages that are closer to natural English. They enable rapid application development with significantly fewer lines of code compared to 3GLs (e.g., C, Java).

**Examples:**

1. **SQL** — for database queries.
2. **PowerBuilder / OutSystems** — for application generation.
3. **SAS / SPSS** — for statistical report generation.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Do not confuse 4GLs with 3GLs or 5GLs. A 4GL example must be from *queries, report generators, or application generators* — not general-purpose programming.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A — *Choose either (a) or (b)*

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper]**
*Discuss in detail the **Rapid Application Development (RAD)** methodology. Compare it with the **Traditional SDLC** in terms of phases, user involvement, and time-to-market.*

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the four phases of RAD with diagrams.**

**Model Answer:**

The **James Martin RAD model** consists of four phases:

1. **Requirements Planning Phase (1 week – 1 month):**
   - Combines **business modeling** (identifying information flow, decision points) and **data modeling** (entities and relationships).
   - Output: A high-level *business model* agreed upon by stakeholders.

2. **User Design Phase (2–4 weeks):**
   - Intensive workshops and prototyping sessions with end-users.
   - Uses **4GLs** and **CASE tools** to rapidly build screen designs and report layouts.
   - Output: An approved *user design prototype*.

3. **Construction Phase (2–4 weeks):**
   - The approved prototype is hardened into a working system.
   - Reusable components and code generators are used.
   - Continuous testing is performed alongside development.

4. **Cutover Phase (2–4 weeks):**
   - Includes data conversion, user training, system testing, and rollout.
   - Parallel running or phased cutover may be used.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Compare RAD with Traditional SDLC.**

| Parameter | Traditional SDLC (Waterfall) | RAD |
|-----------|------------------------------|-----|
| Phases | Sequential (5–7 phases) | 4 parallel/iterative phases |
| User Involvement | Mostly at requirements stage | Continuous throughout |
| Documentation | Heavy, formal | Light, focused on working model |
| Time-to-Market | 6–18 months | 2–4 months |
| Risk Management | Detected late | Detected early |
| Best Suited For | Large, well-defined projects | Mid-size, urgent projects |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students forget to draw or describe a **RAD phase diagram**. Even a simple four-box flow diagram carries 2–3 marks.

---

### Question B — *Choose either (a) or (b)*

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Explain **Agile Development** and the **Scrum framework** in detail. Discuss the four values of the Agile Manifesto.*

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the Agile Manifesto values.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Agile Manifesto (2001)** was authored by 17 software practitioners and lists **four core values** and **twelve principles**.

**The Four Values:**

1. **Individuals and Interactions over Processes and Tools** — Communication and teamwork matter more than rigid processes.
2. **Working Software over Comprehensive Documentation** — A working product is the primary measure of progress.
3. **Customer Collaboration over Contract Negotiation** — Continuous engagement with the customer is preferred over fixed contracts.
4. **Responding to Change over Following a Plan** — Flexibility to adapt is valued over rigid plans.

> **Twelve Principles (Summary):** *Early and continuous delivery, welcome changing requirements, business people and developers must work together daily, build projects around motivated individuals, face-to-face conversation, working software is the primary measure of progress, sustainable development, technical excellence and good design, simplicity, self-organizing teams, regular reflection and adaptation.*

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Describe the Scrum framework in detail.**

**Model Answer:**

**Scrum** is the most widely used Agile framework. Its key elements are:

1. **Roles:**
   - **Product Owner** — Defines the product backlog priorities.
   - **Scrum Master** — Facilitates the process and removes impediments.
   - **Development Team** — Cross-functional team of 3–9 members.

2. **Artifacts:**
   - **Product Backlog** — Prioritized list of user stories.
   - **Sprint Backlog** — Items selected for the current sprint.
   - **Increment** — The working product after the sprint.

3. **Events:**
   - **Sprint Planning** (4–8 hours).
   - **Daily Scrum / Standup** (15 minutes).
   - **Sprint Review** (1–4 hours).
   - **Sprint Retrospective** (1–3 hours).
   - **Sprint Duration** — 2 to 4 weeks.

**Workflow:**

1. The Product Owner prioritizes the Product Backlog.
2. The team selects items for the Sprint Backlog during Sprint Planning.
3. The team works on the items, with Daily Standups for synchronization.
4. At the end of the sprint, the increment is demonstrated in the Sprint Review.
5. The team reflects on improvements in the Sprint Retrospective.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students confuse **Scrum Master** with a *project manager*. The Scrum Master is a *servant-leader* who *facilitates* the team, not a traditional boss.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final High-Density Revision Summary — Read This Before the Exam**

### Core Concepts to Remember

- **Prototyping** = iterative working model → 4 steps (Identify → Develop → Test → Refine).
- **RAD** = 4 phases (Requirements Planning → User Design → Construction → Cutover) — duration **60–90 days**.
- **Agile** = guided by the **Agile Manifesto (4 values, 12 principles)**; popular variant is **Scrum** with **2–4 week sprints**.
- **Scrum Roles** = **Product Owner, Scrum Master, Development Team**.
- **EUD** = end-users build their own systems using spreadsheets/low-code tools → risk = **Shadow IT**.
- **CBD** = reuse of pre-built components; reduces development time by **30–70%**.
- **OOSD** = uses **objects** (encapsulation, inheritance, polymorphism) and **UML diagrams**.
- **Web-Based Development** = browser-based, platform-independent, leverages **cloud and microservices**.
- **COTS** = pre-built software (SAP, Oracle); decision uses **Payback Period** formula.
- **Outsourcing** = external vendor develops the system; total cost = $C_{vendor} + C_{mgmt}$.
- **JAD** = structured 3–5 day workshop with key roles: **Sponsor, Facilitator, Users, Developers, Observers**.
- **CASE Tools** = classified as **Upper CASE (front-end)**, **Lower CASE (back-end)**, **Integrated CASE (I-CASE)**.
- **4GL** = high-level language (SQL, PowerBuilder); productivity multiplier = $\frac{LOC_{3GL}}{LOC_{4GL}}$ ≈ **5x–10x**.

### Key Formulas to Memorize

| # | Formula | Purpose |
|---|---------|---------|
| 1 | $S = C_{IT} - C_{user} - C_{training}$ | EUD Net Savings |
| 2 | $PM = \frac{LOC_{3GL}}{LOC_{4GL}}$ | 4GL Productivity |
| 3 | $PB = \frac{C_{COTS} - C_{inhouse}}{S_{annual}}$ | COTS Payback |
| 4 | $ROI = \frac{(B - C_{CASE})}{C_{CASE}} \times 100$ | CASE Tool ROI |
| 5 | $C_{out} = C_{vendor} + C_{mgmt}$ | Outsourcing Cost |

### Frequently Asked Comparison Pairs (Must Memorize)

- **Waterfall vs RAD**
- **RAD vs Agile**
- **Prototyping vs JAD**
- **COTS vs In-House vs Outsourcing**
- **Upper CASE vs Lower CASE vs Integrated CASE**

### Common Pitfalls to Avoid

- ❌ Confusing **Scrum Master** with a project manager.
- ❌ Treating **4GL** examples as 3GL examples (e.g., C, Java).
- ❌ Forgetting **limitations** of prototyping.
- ❌ Mixing up **Upper CASE** and **Lower CASE** functions.
- ❌ Not drawing a **phase diagram** for RAD/Agile questions.

### Last-Minute Memory Hooks

- **RAD** → *"Rapid 4 Phases: RUCC"* (Requirements, User design, Construction, Cutover).
- **Scrum Events** → *"Sprint Plan, Standup, Review, Retrospective"* — repeat every 2–4 weeks.
- **CASE** → *"Upper = design; Lower = code; Integrated = both."*
- **JAD** → *"3–5 days, 5 roles, 1 goal: agreed requirements."*
- **OOSD** → *"Four Pillars: EIPA"* (Encapsulation, Inheritance, Polymorphism, Abstraction).
